question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given the findings of the JAMA Oncology study into the clinical benefit of focal therapy, whether his Department will review the decision not to introduce focal therapy.
answer_text: My Department has not undertaken any formal assessment of whether introducing focal therapy would result in cost savings compared with current treatment options. This is because the Department relies on National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) endorsed evidence reviews and guidance to determine clinical and cost-effective interventions for commissioning in Northern Ireland. Given that NICE guidance currently does not deem focal therapy sufficiently evidenced for routine use, and that its clinical and cost-effectiveness has yet to be demonstrated to NICE standards, Focal therapy is not currently offered as a treatment option for prostate cancer in Northern Ireland, as noted in AQW 40478/22-27 (27th February 2026).My Department is aware of the emerging evidence base on focal therapy, including the recent JAMA Oncology publication from February 2026 ‘Salvage Focal Therapy vs Radical Prostatectomy for Localized Radiorecurrent Prostate Cancer’.  This study reports that focal therapy offers comparable long-term cancer control with fewer post treatment complications.NICE routinely monitors new research to determine whether guidance should be updated. When new evidence emerges, it is assessed for relevance and potential impact. The February 2026 JAMA study contributes to the evolving evidence base, and NICE may therefore reconsider its current position on focal therapy. Any review would need to assess the robustness of the evidence, long-term oncological outcomes, safety, and implications for service delivery.In NI, the Northern Ireland Cancer Network’s Urology Clinical Reference Group is convening a sub-group to review emerging prostate cancer diagnostics and treatments. This work will help ensure that the regional approach to prostate cancer remains aligned with the best available clinical evidence and cost effectiveness, and supports future service development.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made on whether introducing focal therapy would result in any cost savings to his Department, as opposed to the treatment options available.
answer_text: My Department has not undertaken any formal assessment of whether introducing focal therapy would result in cost savings compared with current treatment options. This is because the Department relies on National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) endorsed evidence reviews and guidance to determine clinical and cost-effective interventions for commissioning in Northern Ireland. Given that NICE guidance currently does not deem focal therapy sufficiently evidenced for routine use, and that its clinical and cost-effectiveness has yet to be demonstrated to NICE standards, Focal therapy is not currently offered as a treatment option for prostate cancer in Northern Ireland, as noted in AQW 40478/22-27 (27th February 2026).My Department is aware of the emerging evidence base on focal therapy, including the recent JAMA Oncology publication from February 2026 ‘Salvage Focal Therapy vs Radical Prostatectomy for Localized Radiorecurrent Prostate Cancer’. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has made any assessment of the feasibility of providing a third track or passing provision at Ballymena station, similar to the layout at Lisburn, in order to support additional commuter services.
answer_text: My Department has not carried out a specific assessment on providing a third track or passing loop at Ballymena station. However, Translink has undertaken timetable modelling on the Belfast to Derry rail corridor as part of its wider strategic network planning.That modelling looked at longterm service frequencies of up to four trains per hour to and from Ballymena. It indicated that the current station layout at Ballymena does not present a constraint to achieving those levels of service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department is taking to ensure that investments in park and ride facilities along rail corridors, are matched with sufficient service provision to accommodate increased passenger demand.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink NI Railways in their business cases for the expansion of Park & Ride sites have considered capacity on the rail network. Passenger growth on the entire network has increased and will exceed 2018/19 levels ( a record year). Since the record levels were achieved, capacity has also increased with the introduction of 7 x 6 car trains. The additional Park & Ride facilities will not only displace on-street parking in these areas but will encourage further growth that has surplus capacity for significant parts of the operational day.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment her Department has made of the operational constraints posed by the single track section on the Dargan Bridge; and (ii) whether her Department has considered double tracking this section, to support future service expansion.
answer_text: Translink has considered double tracking across the Dargan viaduct as part of longerterm strategic planning. While dualling would provide additional capacity, it remains a longterm aspiration and is constrained by the availability of capital funding and wider affordability pressures across the public transport network. The All-Island Strategic Rail Review sets out a long-term vision for enhancing rail connectivity and capacity across the island, including recommendations to improve infrastructure in key urban areas such as Belfast. The Review acknowledges that physical constraints, such as those posed by existing structures like Dargan Bridge, may impact future capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has considered stabling trains overnight at Ballymena, to facilitate early morning commuter services to Belfast.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink NI Railways do not have a train crew depot or associated facilities at Ballymena Station.  Such an arrangement would require the train to stable overnight on the platform, requiring crew transfers and overnight security.  Additionally, the stabled train would need to depart for Belfast ahead of peak commuter demand to ensure both platforms were clear for the passing of trains on this predominantly single line corridor.   All available trains are in operation during peak periods.  To allocate an additional train to start from Ballymena would reduce the number of peaktime services available on the rest of the North West corridor.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has considered extending future rail services, operating on a reopened Knockmore Line to Ballymena, in order to increase passenger capacity and improve connectivity along the Belfast to Derry line.
answer_text: I can confirm that the reopening of the Antrim to Knockmore line is one of the short to medium term priorities identified in the All-Island Strategic Rail Review.  Translink completed the first stage of development work on this in 2025 and the high-level findings from the study were published in December 2025 as part of the Shaping the Future document -https://www.translink.co.uk/about-translink/media/pressreleases/shaping-the-future  I have allocated £1m in the 2025/26 financial year to take forward the next steps.  This includes more detailed technical work on the proposed connection to Belfast International Airport, engagement with planning authorities, development of signalling and electrification proposals, and an assessment of user worked crossings along the route.Any further development of new or extended services, including the possibility of running future services through to Ballymena, depends on funding, approval of the necessary business cases, and securing statutory consents. As work is still at an early stage, no assessment has yet been carried out on extending services to Ballymena.  That can only be considered once the core feasibility work on the Knockmore line itself is further advanced.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has given any consideration to introducing commuter services, starting and terminating at Ballymena, similar to the existing Portadown commuter services, to increase capacity on the Belfast to Derry rail corridor.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink has reviewed its plans and is currently not in a position to further enhance commuter rail services between Belfast and Ballymena without considerable investment in infrastructure changes, new fleet and additional revenue funding.  They are currently drafting a business case for additional rolling stock, however this is not currently funded and would not be available for service until 2032 at the earliest.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) whether her Department has made any assessment of passenger overcrowding on morning commuter services between Ballymena and Belfast; and (ii) any steps being taken to address standing room only conditions.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink NI Railways regularly reviews passenger loadings on all services across the rail network and, where possible, adjusts capacity or frequency to better meet demand. Morning peak rail services from Ballymena to Belfast are indeed busy with many customers standing on these trains.  This situation is not unusual and mirrors commuter rail experiences in major cities globally during peak periods. It is important to note that railways are designed as mass transit systems where standing is permitted as part of normal operations. Translink wishes to assure its customers that all available fleet are in operation during peak periods.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment her Department has made of rail service capacity on the Belfast to Londonderry line, in light of the significant expansion plans of park and ride facilities at Bellarena, Ballymena and Mossley West, and the proposals to connect the line to the Ballymartin Park and Ride.
answer_text: My Department has considered the implications of the planned expansion of Park and Ride facilities at Bellarena, Ballymena and Mossley West for rail capacity on the Belfast to Derry line. In developing the business cases for these sites, NI Railways has assessed the capacity of the rail corridor. Passenger numbers on the North West line have grown by over 11% yeartodate and are set to exceed 2018/19 levels, which was a record year. Since this time, Park & Ride capacity on the line has doubled, and the additional provision not only alleviates current onstreet parking pressures in these areas but also supports further passenger growth on a corridor that continues to have surplus capacity during significant portions of the operational day.Regarding proposals to connect the line to the Ballymartin Park and Ride, this facility could not operate as a railserved site without further increases in train frequency and rolling stock capacity. Translink is currently developing a business case for additional rolling stock which, subject to funding approval, would allow for further capacity enhancements on the network.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will (i) rearrange; and (ii) provide the new date for, the meeting with the Northern Ireland Covid Bereaved Families for Justice, which was postponed in February 2025 and March 2026.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any reason why they have not met with the Northern Ireland Covid Bereaved Families for Justice, since the postponed meeting that was to take place in February 2025.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any further financial support that will be provided to domiciliary care workers who are facing inflated petrol and diesel prices to fulfil their duties.
answer_text: As part of negotiations to conclude industrial action undertaken during 2023, my Department, employer representatives and Trade Union Side undertook to consider a regional system of mileage reimbursement which might better reflect the integrated nature of the HSC service and the rurality of many of our population requiring care at home.Following these negotiations, a Departmental circular, which issued on 20 May 2024, informed HSC employers that the primary rate of mileage had increased, with effect from 1 April 2024 to 58 pence per mile for staff employed within HSC under Agenda for Change (AfC) terms and conditions.It is important to note that when AfC mileage rates were increased in 2024, fuel prices were at similar levels to those currently prevailing. Staff have, to some extent, benefited from lower fuel prices over the past couple of years as the mileage rates paid have remained the same.At present, the Department is not considering any further revisions to the published mileage rates or any additional financial support schemes related to fuel costs.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the impact of the First Minister's non-attendance at briefings by His Majesty’s Government, in relation to the conflict in Iran, on Northern Ireland citizens in the Middle East.
To ask the Minister of Finance when regulations will be in place for parents to avail of a baby loss certificate.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how much money has been raised annually over the duration of the Carrier Bag Levy.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of school staff who have had a myocardial infarction during school hours or activity, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised it does not hold this specific information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of pupils who have had a myocardial infarction during school hours or activity, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised it does not hold this specific information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions his Department is taking to address the issues raised by people working in health and social care whose holiday and sick pay does not reflect the overtime they do.
answer_text: From 1st April 2025, the HSC have applied an interim solution, as agreed with regional Trade Unions, to apply an uplift to holiday and sick pay calculations for HSC staff; this includes overtime payments.  This solution was agreed as an interim arrangement on the current payroll system HRPTS, due to the fact that it is to be replaced in late 2026 with a new Oracle based system known as ‘equip’.The replacement Oracle Payroll system, once implemented, will fully automate accurate payment during periods of annual leave and sick leave, again including overtime payments.   Claims for retrospective payments from HSC staff prior to April 2025 are being case managed through the Tribunal system at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of whether guidance on roadside hedge cutting during the closed season is sufficiently clear, robust and enforceable.
answer_text: DAERA has robust regulations and guidance in place to protect hedgerows through the new Farm Sustainability Standards (FSS) which were introduced from 1 January 2026. Any farmer claiming an applicable DAERA farm payment, must abide by FSS. Any farmer or member of the public can report hedge cutting during the closed season to the Department. Where DAERA receives a report of hedge cutting during the closed period, it is investigated and if a breach is identified, the farmer will incur a FSS penalty.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has given any consideration to strengthening the enforcement of the roadside hedge cutting guidance to prevent routine, non-essential hedge cutting during the bird nesting season.
answer_text: DAERA has an inspection regime in place to protect hedgerows through the new Farm Sustainability Standards (FSS) which were introduced from 1 January 2026. In addition, any farmer or member of the public can report hedge cutting during the closed season to the Department. Where DAERA receives a report of hedge cutting during the closed period, it is investigated and if a breach is identified, the farmer will incur a FSS penalty. The FSS will be subject to periodic review.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of whether Farm Sustainability Standards could be used to reinforce best practice, change behaviours and ensure that roadside hedge management reflects sustainability principles while still allowing necessary safety works.
answer_text: Yes, the new Farm Sustainability Standards (FSS) can help to reinforce best practice, change behaviours and ensure that roadside hedge management reflects sustainability principles while still allowing necessary safety works.The FSS has robust regulations and guidance in place to protect hedgerows. DAERA also regularly issues press releases informing farm businesses of the regulations and their responsibilities with regards to hedgerows.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of (i) the Health and Social Care Trusts' compliance with the Payments to Staff Guidance; and (ii) whether this policy is reflective of the extra hours health and social care staff members work.
answer_text: The terms and conditions of service for the relevant staffing group provides the framework through which additional hours, including overtime, unsocial hours, and other relevant allowances, are recognised and paid. This ensures staff receive appropriate remuneration for the hours they work, including hours worked beyond their contracted commitments.Off the back of the ruling in the Agnew v PSNI case, and further to Tribunal claims being lodged against HSCNI, a review has been carried out in relation to the wage types that have been historically included in the calculations for holiday pay.  HSCNI have identified that a number of wage types not previously included in the calculation of holiday pay needed to be added i.e. overtime, work done on-call etc.  To ensure that the revised calculations are compliant with the wording in the AFC terms & conditions, the same amendments were required in the sick pay calculations.  From 1st April 2025, the HSC, in agreement with Trade Unions, has applied an interim solution for both holiday pay and sick pay.  This solution was agreed as an interim arrangement on the current payroll system HRPTS, due to the fact that it is to be replaced in late 2026 with a new Oracle based system known as ‘equip’.  The replacement Oracle payroll system, once implemented, will fully automate accurate payment during periods of annual leave and sick leave, including additional hours, overtime etc.    Claims from HSC staff for retrospective payments prior to April 2025 are being case managed through the Tribunal system at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the drug abiraterone; and (ii) whether his Department has any plans to make this more readily available and easily accessible to men living with prostate cancer.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. This link has ensured that Northern Ireland has access to up-to-date, independent, professional, evidence-based guidance on the value of health care interventions. In practice this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also routinely available in Northern Ireland. Where NICE has recommended the use of abiraterone (Zytiga®) in the treatment of prostate cancer, this medicine is available to eligible patients in Northern Ireland, in line with the current NICE guidelines. Further information on this treatment is available at the following links:https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta259https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta387https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta951https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta1110Whilst there is a process to check the guidance for legal and policy applicability here, there is no Departmental reassessment of the clinical or cost evidence used by NICE in coming to its decisions and forming its advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has any formal plans to offer focal therapy as a means of treatment for men diagnosed with prostate cancer.
answer_text: Focal therapy is not currently offered as a treatment option for prostate cancer in Northern Ireland. This reflects the position set out in the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guideline on prostate cancer (NG131 Prostate cancer: diagnosis and management: https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng131), last reviewed in August 2025, which does not include focal therapy as a recommended treatment option due to insufficient evidence of clinical benefit. Although focal therapy is not available, men diagnosed with localised prostate cancer in Northern Ireland have access to the full range of evidence-based, nationally recommended treatments. These include external beam radiotherapy, incorporating advanced approaches such as stereotactic ablative radiotherapy (SABR); radical robot-assisted prostatectomy; and active surveillance or watchful waiting for patients where these approaches are clinically appropriate. These treatments are all supported by strong clinical evidence and are included in NICE’s recommended pathways for prostate cancer management.The Northern Ireland Cancer Network’s Urology Clinical Reference Group is establishing a working group to review prostate cancer investigations and treatments across the region. This review will support future service planning and help ensure that prostate cancer care in Northern Ireland remains aligned with the best available clinical evidence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration his Department has given to the role of a respiratory Modern Service Framework, in reducing winter pressures on the NHS, by simultaneously improving outcomes for long-term respiratory conditions, and short-term respiratory illnesses such as flu.
answer_text: Reference to ‘Modern Service Frameworks’ is cited in the NHS 10-year plan for England, Fit for the future.  This is not a term used in NI context, and I am not aware of any policy position / commitment to developing these in NI.My Department recognises the substantial impact that respiratory illness has on winter pressures across the health and social care system. Acute respiratory infections, including influenza, remain among the leading causes of unscheduled hospital attendance and admission during winter months.  In this context, improvements in respiratory care would contribute directly to alleviating winter pressures by reducing demand on emergency departments, freeing capacity in acute respiratory wards, and supporting better outcomes for patients.The establishment of the Northern Ireland Respiratory Forum, comprising clinicians, charities and officials, has provided a platform for cross-sector work. The Forum is tasked with identifying respiratory pathways requiring future system change or investment, and it contributes to ongoing consideration of how a modernised framework could support more consistent standards in respiratory care.The Forum explore and considers the most effective, evidence-based interventions, clinical standards, best practice pathways and support for consistent delivery of services across the region.  As part of this work, officials have participated in discussions regarding the role of diagnostics, digital tools and data-driven approaches to explore how these could help accelerate the safe adoption of evidence-based treatments and innovative service models, ensuring consistency of standards across Northern Ireland. This reflects the ethos of the Modern Service Framework and I can assure you that my Department and officials adhere to this in all aspects of the work they carry out whilst working to improve services in health across the region.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has any plans to make sex-selective abortion illegal, given under the current law, in some cases, an explanation is not required to obtain an abortion.
answer_text: Abortion is permitted only in circumstances set out in the Abortion (Northern Ireland) (No2) Regulations 2020.  There are no plans to change the current law in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any assessment his Department has made of the prevalence of respiratory disease; and (ii) any steps he is taking to ensure respiratory health is prioritised.
answer_text: Reference to ‘Modern Service Frameworks’ is cited in the NHS 10-year plan for England, Fit for the future.  This is not a term used in NI context, and I am not aware of any policy position / commitment to developing these in NI.My Department recognises the substantial impact that respiratory illness has on winter pressures across the health and social care system. Acute respiratory infections, including influenza, remain among the leading causes of unscheduled hospital attendance and admission during winter months.  In this context, improvements in respiratory care would contribute directly to alleviating winter pressures by reducing demand on emergency departments, freeing capacity in acute respiratory wards, and supporting better outcomes for patients.The establishment of the Northern Ireland Respiratory Forum, comprising clinicians, charities and officials, has provided a platform for cross-sector work. The Forum is tasked with identifying respiratory pathways requiring future system change or investment, and it contributes to ongoing consideration of how a modernised framework could support more consistent standards in respiratory care.The Forum explore and considers the most effective, evidence-based interventions, clinical standards, best practice pathways and support for consistent delivery of services across the region.  As part of this work, officials have participated in discussions regarding the role of diagnostics, digital tools and data-driven approaches to explore how these could help accelerate the safe adoption of evidence-based treatments and innovative service models, ensuring consistency of standards across Northern Ireland. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the future accommodation provision for Slemish College.
answer_text: The procurement of an Integrated Consultant Team for the major capital project is ongoing.It is anticipated that appointment of the Integrated Consultant Team will be made in Spring 2026.Following appointment, the Integrated Consultant Team will undertake a feasibility study to explore options to provide the school with suitable accommodation and outside sports facilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has any plans to provide funding to pro-life organisations, who assist women and girls in crisis pregnancies.
answer_text: The advisory service funded by my Department provides non-directive pregnancy choices counselling, support and information.  The organisation Life NI was awarded funding from my Department’s Core Grant Funding Scheme for the 2025/26 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any assessment her Department has made of the financial and emotional impact of probate delays.
answer_text: I am very conscious of the financial and emotional impact that the death of a loved one can have.  The granting of probate is a matter for the independent judiciary, supported administratively by the Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS). The average time for the processing of probate applications is currently just over 6 weeks.  The majority of cases are not contentious, and many can be dealt with relatively quickly without recourse to a solicitor; however, some cases, particularly those in which the person died intestate or if the Will is contested, are more complex and may result in delay and, in some cases, time-consuming litigation between the parties.NICTS monitors and reviews probate administrative performance targets on an annual basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of staff currently assigned to probate work; and (ii) whether this represents an increase or decrease compared to the last five years.
answer_text: (i) The Probate Office has an administrative complement of 6.5 Full-Time Equivalent (FTE) staff. There are currently 6 FTE staff in post. As the Office is part of the Royal Courts of Justice Central Office, there are also 2 FTE managers which work across a range of areas including Probate.(ii)  Staffing within the Central Office is fluid, allowing staff to move between work areas depending on demand, including Probate.  The Northern Ireland Courts & Tribunals Service (NICTS) is working with the Northern Ireland Civil Service Human Resources (NICS HR) to fill vacant posts substantively.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the current average processing time for probate applications; and (ii) how this compares with processing times in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The average processing times for probate applications in each of the last five years is presented in the table below.Grants Issued by the Probate OfficeYearNumberAverage Time in Weeks20217,7508.720227,5143.120237,5384.820247,3225.92025*7,4456.3Source: Integrated Court Operations System (ICOS) *2025 data is provisional

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the reason why it takes six to eight weeks to process a administrative refund, due to a quickly identified booking error on the MOT booking website.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency’s MOT cancellation period is four hours before the appointment test time for private cars and light goods vehicles. For all other vehicles the cancellation period is one clear working day, excluding weekends and public holidays.On-line and call centre refunds are processed immediately at the customer’s request; however, the time taken for the payment to appear in the customer’s account depends on the processing and clearance times applied by individual banks, which may vary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) any steps her Department is taking to reduce the existing delays in probate processing; and (ii) a timeline for when waiting times are expected to reduce.
answer_text: There are no significant existing delays in probate processing.  The most recent statistics indicate that the average time taken to process a Grant of Probate is just over 6 weeks.  NICTS monitors and reviews probate administrative performance targets on an annual basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any support he can provide military personnel and their families to ensure they are not disadvantaged due to their service to our country.
answer_text: My officials are developing a proposal for a dedicated Arms Forces liaison pathway. This will offer a single point of contact where individuals can receive a free and confidential needs assessment to determine potential eligibility for benefits, support, and services. The pathway will be available to veterans, service personnel transitioning to civilian life, and their families, helping to minimise the risk of financial disadvantage.My Department is participating in veterans’ roadshows, improving escalation routes, enhancing collaboration with the Northern Ireland Veterans Commissioner and the Office for Veterans’ Affairs, and increasing outreach between Jobs & Benefits Offices and Veterans Champions. In addition, my Department is developing a dedicated forum to identify gaps in provision and coordinate support more effectively.My Department and the Housing Executive uphold the Armed Forces Covenant to ensure fair treatment in social housing, homelessness assistance, and disabled facilities grants. The Housing Executive uses a traumainformed approach, taking servicerelated health and wellbeing needs into account, and allocates additional points for those leaving service accommodation. Its Housing Options service also includes referrals to the Veterans’ Adviceline for wider support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the reason why the official diary was blank on 17 December 2025, despite her Department stating that she was unable to attend the Executive meeting on that date, due to a diary clash.
answer_text: The Department correctly stated that I was unavailable due to “a prior arrangement”. The Executive meeting of 17th December, held during Assembly recess, was originally scheduled for the 11th December when I was available, but was rescheduled at relatively short notice and without prior consultation.  As I have publicly stated, I was unable to attend the rescheduled Executive meeting on 17th December as I had a longstanding prior arrangement of a personal nature. Personal appointments do not form part of the official diary by definition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) any process used when she is on leave, or not available for Ministerial duties; including how this is communicated to departmental officials.
answer_text: My availability is managed by my Private Office and takes account of personal commitments, as for all Ministers.  My Private Office staff liaise regularly with Departmental staff on my availability.  I remain contactable via phone and email, irrespective of any leave arrangements, to address urgent issues as and when they may arise.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the scoping exercise on the feasibility and sustainability of an industry levy on licenced gambling operators will be completed by the end of the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: I refer the member to the response to AQO 2695/22-27 answered on 17 November 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the implementation of the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence Clinical Guideline NG248 - Gambling-related harms: identification, assessment, and management.
answer_text: The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) Clinical Guideline NG248, Gamblingrelated harms: identification, assessment and management, was formally endorsed by the Department of Health in June 2025 and included in the list of NICE Clinical Guidelines endorsed for implementation in Health and Social Care in Northern Ireland.The existing process requires Trusts to provide assurance on the establishment of planning processes at the three month point following DoH endorsement and on full implementation after a further nine months.There is no dedicated funding source to support the implementation of NICE Guidelines in Northern Ireland and any resources required are considered against other competing priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Bluetongue Virus and its impact to North Antrim.
answer_text: Following the confirmation of Bluetongue Virus (BTV) on four farms in County Down before Christmas, my Department completed surveillance testing, including tracings onto and off, all four infected premises. A fifth case was detected on 21 January in one animal, as a result of pre-movement testing requirements at premises near Portavogie in County Down.In addition to the surveillance testing, representative sampling and testing of large cattle herds was progressed within the Temporary Control Zone (TCZ), established around the infected premises, to ascertain if there was further infection.  This surveillance was a necessary part of my department’s disease control response which has sought to mitigate any potential spread of disease for the benefit of local farmers and the wider agri-food industry.I announced a relaxation of animal movement restrictions from Friday 19 December 2025, allowing all animals, except for animals that have tested positive for Bluetongue, to be free to move under general licence within and out of the TCZ, unless they are in certain high-risk categories.You will be aware that on 8 January 2026, I announced financial support for where pre-movement testing was required for high-risk animals moving out of the TCZ.  North Antrim is outside of the TCZ and therefore movements of susceptible species and animal products within Northern Ireland can continue as normal.  Moves of susceptible species into the Temporary Control Zone from the free area, including North Antrim, can take place under general licence available on the DAERA website.Due to the location of the fifth premises within the existing TCZ and given that we are now in the vector inactive period, the existing TCZ has not been extended.  Movements of animals from this premises will be subject to the same conditions as other herds within the TCZ.  My officials will continue to ensure that the measures in place are proportionate to the risk and DAERA vets are assessing the next steps in close cooperation with key stakeholders. Announcements about any further changes will be supported by clear communication from my Department.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline why they have not responded to the request to meet a cross-denominational victims and survivors reference group following the submission of the reports from the independent research commissioned by their Department.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure that tenants, whose rent is covered by Universal Credit payments are required to inform the relevant department of the date they change address and that this is investigated for accuracy, to ensure the correct landlord receives payment, and to limit fraudulent activity.
answer_text: Universal Credit is a claimant-led benefit, with claimants required to report any change of circumstances promptly and provide supporting evidence within 14 days of request. For Social Rented Sector tenants, housing details are verified through an online system that cross-checks information with the landlord. Private Rented Sector tenants must supply proof of housing costs when a claim begins or when an address changes, and this is verified by an agent who also contacts the landlord to confirm accuracy. The Department remains committed to safeguarding public funds by ensuring payments are accurate and verifying information to minimise the risk of fraudulent activity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, regarding the Motability Scheme requirements when changing vehicle, to detail whether there is any provision in place to allow users to retain their existing vehicle, instead of getting a replacement.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information. Operational information about the Motability scheme can be sought from: 	correspondence@motabilityoperations.co.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) her Department's plans to implement an incentive scheme for installation of air source heat pumps; and (ii) the criteria that will be required to avail of the scheme.
answer_text: At present, financial assistance is available through the Northern Ireland Sustainable Energy Programme (NISEP) for the installation of energy efficiency measures and heat pumps, with most of the funding targeted at lower income customers.My Department has consulted on future support for low carbon heating in residential properties. Officials are currently finalising the analysis of responses provided, which will be used to inform policy decisions on the design, rules, and guidance of future support for low carbon heat, including heat pumps. My Department plans to publish a response to the consultation in the coming weeks.Further to this, my Department is progressing work on the high-level design of a Domestic Energy Efficiency and Heat Programme. The criteria required to avail of any future scheme forms part of the design and will be consulted upon later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the percentage of capacity of grit in the Ballymena storage centre, on the 19 December 2025; (ii) the number of days gritting this would facilitate; and (iii) how this compares to Monday 5 January 2026.
answer_text: Within the Mid & East Antrim area, Ballykeel depot is used to deliver winter service activities.  On 19 December 2025, the salt barn within Ballykeel depot was at 90% of its 1,350-ton capacity.It is not possible to equate this volume to days of gritting as each individual action constitutes a number of variables including spread width and the severity of the weather which determines the frequency of actions required on any particular day. An average gritting action constituting a 20 gram spread rate would utilise 40-45 tonnes of salt.On Monday 5 January 2026, the salt barn was at 12% capacity with additional deliveries of 1,000 tonnes completed the same week, returning the barn to sufficient capacity.  Furthermore, my Department has a strategic reserve of 20,000 tonnes of salt held with the Irish Salt Mining Company, Carrickfergus, ensuring supply is readily available throughout the winter season.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, regarding the Domestic Violence and Abuse Disclosure Scheme, when allegations against a person remain unproven or are proved to be false, to detail at what stage is that person not disclosed as a risk under the Scheme.
answer_text: As is the case with AQW 37510/22-27 this matter falls within the operational responsibility of the PSNI, and it would therefore be more appropriately directed to the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, regarding the Domestic Violence and Abuse Disclosure Scheme, when a person faces allegations, to detail at what stage are they disclosed as a risk to any applicants under the Scheme.
answer_text: As is the case with AQW 37510/22-27 this matter falls within the operational responsibility of the PSNI, and it would therefore be more appropriately directed to the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, regarding the Domestic Violence and Abuse Disclosure Scheme, when there has been a disclosure with information that has since been deemed inaccurate or false, whether the applicant would be informed of that.
answer_text: As the issue raised concerns operational policing matters, it does not fall within the remit of the Department of Justice to comment. Responsibility for operational decisions rests with the Police Service of Northern Ireland. It would therefore be more appropriate for the question to be directed to the Chief Constable of the PSNI, who is best placed to consider and respond in detail.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she has any plans for people holding a veterans pass or War Disablement Smart Pass to avail of concessionary fares on public transport.
answer_text: Under the NI Concessionary Fares Scheme, residents of the North who are in receipt of a War Disablement Pension or a Guaranteed Income Payment from the Service Personnel and Veterans Agency may apply for a War Disablement SmartPass, which allows the card holder free concessionary travel on both bus and rail services within the North.  In addition, everyone over the age of 60 is entitled to free travel on public transport services in the North, while those over the age of 65 are entitled to free travel across the island of Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of 3D printed firearms that have been found in Northern Ireland, over the last two years, including the type of firearm.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information, the PSNI would be best placed to answer your question to detail the number of 3D printed firearms found in Northern Ireland over the last two years.You may wish to redirect your question to the PSNI’s Operational Support Department, email OSDstaff@psni.police.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any legislation in place, in Northern Ireland, to cover 3D printed firearms.
answer_text: The manufacture, purchase, sale and possession of firearms, including 3D printed firearms, ammunition or component parts is governed by the provisions of the Firearms (Northern Ireland) Order 2004 [2004 Order].Under the 2004 Order, a firearm means a lethal barrelled weapon of any description from which any shot, bullet or other missile can be discharged.A firearms dealer’s certificate is required to manufacture, sell, transfer, test or prove any firearm or ammunition.The UK Government Border Security, Asylum and Immigration Act, which received Royal Assent on 2 December 2025, introduces measures to strengthen law enforcement and judicial powers in tackling serious and organised crime.  One key measure creates two new offences of possessing a specified article with intent or reasonable suspicion it will be used in a serious offence, and importing, manufacturing, adapting, supplying, or offering to supply such articles under similar conditions.Templates for 3D-printed firearm components are included as specified articles, addressing modern threats by criminalising possession or distribution linked to criminal intent.  In June 2025, I sought legislative consent from the Assembly for these provisions to be extended to Northern Ireland.  My officials are now preparing for commencement and implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department carried out a Regulatory Impact Assessment, prior to the implementation of the Firearms (Northern Ireland) Order 2004.
answer_text: The Department of Justice does not hold any records of a Regulatory Impact Assessment prior to the implementation of the Firearms (Northern Ireland) Order 2004, as firearms legislation and policy was the responsibility of the Secretary of State and the NIO at that time.  The Department does not have access to Northern Ireland Office files which pre-date devolution of policing and justice.Policing and Justice functions were devolved in 2010 and these functions are now shared, with my Department responsible for devolved firearms related matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in the context of firearms licensing internal processes for applications, to detail any reviews that have been carried out, over the last ten years, to ensure that the PSNI's Firearms and Explosives Branch's internal processes, are necessary.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answer to question AQW 36924 22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, given that the PSNI declared a critical incident within Firearms and Explosives Branch (FEB), in July 2022, due to a significant backlog of firearm licence applications, to detail (i) when the critical incident finished; (ii) the lessons learned from the critical incident; (iii) the number of staff in the FEB, at the time of when the critical incident was declared, including roles and grades; (iv) the number of staff currently in the FEB, including the roles and grades; (v) the backlog when the critical incident was declared, including the number of the applicat
answer_text: Firearms and Explosive Branch (FEB) and firearms licensing, within the Police Service of Northern Ireland, are the responsibility of the Chief Constable who is answerable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.The Chief Constable is best placed to answer your question regarding firearm applications data, staffing levels and grades, past and present, within PSNI FEB and information concerning the 2022 critical incident.You may, therefore, wish to redirect your questions to PSNI Operational Support Department, email OSDstaff@psni.police.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the processing targets that have been set at senior management level in the PSNI, for processing firearms applications, bearing in mind that the 2018 NI Audit Office's Firearms Licensing in Northern Ireland report stated that the 56 day target can be processed much more quickly.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answer to question AQW 36924 22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department has carried out a review of the Impact Assessment, since the last amendments to the Firearms (Northern Ireland ) Order 2002 were implemented, particularly banded system, young shooters and fees increase.
answer_text: In 2016 when the Firearms (Northern Ireland) Order 2004 (2004 Order) was amended in respect of the banded system, young shooters and fees, an impact assessment was carried out.  There has not been a need to conduct a further impact assessment as no policy changes relating to the 2004 Order have been brought forward to date.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of times the Northern Ireland Adverse Incident Centre has investigated (a) medical devices; (b) equipment malfunction; and (c) estate-related safety issues; (ii) the number of these that were logged in a regional database; and (iii) the number logged that were then escalated to the UK-wide Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Adverse Incident Centre (NIAIC) oversaw a manufacturer’s investigation and any subsequent outcome to a medical device incident report. All medical device incident reports submitted to NIAIC were logged on a departmental database and forwarded to the Medicines and Healthcare Products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) and the medical device manufacturer where necessary. Multiple reports of the same incident or where, after initial assessment of the incident, it was deemed that the device was not at fault were not forwarded. From 1st January 2025 medical device incident reports are reported directly to the MHRA Yellow Card system by the healthcare provider.   All Estates Safety incidents are logged on a departmental database but are not reported to the MHRA.Incident reports received by NIAIC from 2015 for both medical device and estates related issues are provided below. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of times the Strategic Planning and Performance Group and the Public Health Agency have (i) produced reports; and (ii) conducted investigations, under the Serious Adverse Incident Procedure, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The purpose of Serious Adverse Incident Procedure (SAI) procedure is to provide guidance to Health and Social Care (HSC) Organisations, and Special Agencies (SA) in relation to the reporting and follow up of SAIs arising during the course of their business or commissioned service. 		During the period 1 January 2015 to 08 December 2025 there were 156 serious adverse incidents (SAIs) reported by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG- formerly Health and Social Care Board) and the Public Health Agency (PHA). A breakdown is provided below: 						OrganisationTotal Number of SAIs Reported Current PositionNumber of ‘produced reports’ Total SAI Reports to be ‘produced’ (investigation/review ongoing)SPPG*1521466PHA 440Total 1561506		*Includes incidents reported by Primary Care 		The following table provides a detailed breakdown of the above data for each of the last ten years: Year SAI ReportedSPPG*PHATotal Number of SAIs ReportedNumber of ‘produced reports’ Total Number of SAIs ReportedNumber of ‘produced reports’ 20152020112016212111201715151120181818  20192727  202015151120211414  202277  202333  202465  202561  Total152146**44* includes incidents from Primary Care**6 reviews/investigations within Primary Care are ongoing and therefore the SAI Report is not yet completed. The SAI process aims to: Provide a mechanism to effectively share learning in a meaningful way; with a focus on safety and quality; ultimately leading to service improvement for service users; Provide a coherent approach to what constitutes a SAI; to ensure consistency in reporting across the HSC and SAs; Clarify the roles, responsibilities and processes relating to the reporting, reviewing, dissemination and implementation of learning arising from SAIs which occur during the course of the business of a HSC organisation / SA or commissioned/funded service; Ensure the process works simultaneously with all other statutory and regulatory organisations that may require to be notified of the incident or be involved the review; Keep the process for the reporting and review of SAIs under review to ensure it is fit for purpose and minimises unnecessary duplication; Recognise the responsibilities of individual organisations and support them in ensuring compliance; by providing a culture of openness and transparency that encourages the reporting of SAIs; Ensure trends, best practice and learning is identified, disseminated and implemented in a timely manner, in order to prevent recurrence; andMaintain a high quality of information and documentation within a time bound process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail when she intends to legislate, under existing powers of the Employment Rights Act 2016, to introduce a requirement for prescribed persons to produce annual reports and submit them to the Department.
answer_text: I intend to bring forward secondary legislation on this matter during the current mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any progress her Department has made on Action 22 of the Connected Causeway Transport and Parking Infrastructure Study and Action Plan 2022-2032 report.
answer_text: I can advise that the AECOM Connected Causeway Transport and Parking Infrastructure Study 2022-2032 and associated Action Plan was commissioned by Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council as part of the early development work on a number of Growth Deal projects.The actions identified in the plan have now largely been incorporated into the Connected Causeway growth deal project being taken forward by the Council. My officials will continue to work with their council counterparts and other stakeholders in the development and delivery of the project. My Department continues to regularly enforce parking restrictions around the Giant’s Causeway site and is reviewing the condition of the road markings to ensure that restrictions remain enforceable.  The department also continues to work with the National Trust at the Giant's Causeway to develop a programme of enforcement for seasonal periods which attract higher visitor numbers. Enforcement Patrol effectiveness is reviewed on a regular basis and can be increased subject to available capacity.
