question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail when a community group volunteer would require an AccessNI check.
answer_text: The statutory framework under which AccessNI operates does not place any requirements on an organisation to obtain checks.  The legislation sets out the circumstances in which those undertaking specific roles are eligible for certain levels of AccessNI checks, for example those undertaking Regulated Activity with children are eligible for an Enhanced check.It is a matter for each organisation, in developing their safeguarding policies, to determine which roles require AccessNI checks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any work currently being undertaken by her Department to ensure that the Waiting Restrictions (Giant's Causeway) Order (Northern Ireland) 2017 is fit for purpose and effective, in reducing the coach and car congestion problems in and around the Giant's Causeway area.
answer_text: I can confirm that the above legislation is fit for purpose and restrictions are regularly enforced around the Giant’s Causeway site. In addition, officials are reviewing the condition of the road markings to ensure that restrictions remain enforceable.I can also advise that my Department is providing input into the Connected Causeway project being led by Council as part of the Causeway Coast and Glens Growth Deal. The project aims to address parking issues arising at various visitor attractions along the Causeway Coast, including at the Giant’s Causeway. Other proposals being considered include the reconfiguration of parking at Dundarave, Bushmills.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the number of delirium beds in each Health and Social Care Trust, including the setting in which they are placed.
To ask the Minister of Finance to outline each Department's discretionary spend as a percentage of their overall budget for 2025/26.

To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Utility Regulator's investigation into whether the electricity generation company, EP Ballylumford Limited, has failed to meet the obligations of its licence to generate electricity.
To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the number of applications made to the Child Protection Disclosure Scheme in each of the last four years.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how Northern Ireland can capitalise on its historic links with the US to recognise the key role the Ulster Scots played in the foundation and history of the United States of America.
answer_text: The 250th Anniversary of the US Declaration of Independence is a unique platform to highlight our global significance, and to strengthen economic and cultural opportunities.The US 250 Commission recently visited NI to sign a Memorandum of Understanding. The Memorandum will underpin events, lectures, exhibitions, conferences, document exchanges and social media led by the Department of Communities and supported by the NI Bureau in the US.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail why it is necessary to have an nidrect account to use the My Care app.
answer_text: My Care contains personal health information for patients and service users, therefore it is essential that data is secure, private and only accessible by the correct person(s).NI Direct is the official trusted government identity verification service in Northern Ireland.  It was selected for encompass because it offers the most secure method of confirming a person’s identify while fully complying with GDPR requirements.  Although nidirect provides the necessary identity verification for accessing My Care, it does not create any link between encompass and other government systems.  This ensures that health information remains confidential and is only used for its intended purpose within My Care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of deceased people who had consented to organ donation, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Data on organ donation is collated by NHS Blood and Transplant, and they have confirmed that 265 out of 496 deceased donors in Northern Ireland consented to organ donation in the last ten years, as follows:FinancialYear2015/162016/172017/182018/192019/202020/212021/222022/232023/242024/25TotalExpressed consent12161724312631324531265Total donors47424044515154596444496											It should be noted that expressed consent is defined as when the individual expressed a decision to donate via any method, either verbally or via the Organ Donor Register and it was therefore their last known decision on organ donation.In cases where an individual had not expressed their decision to donate, a discussion would always have taken place with the family and donation would only have proceeded if consent was given by the family. Since the introduction of legislation in 2023, consent can either be expressed or deemed; however, a discussion would still take place with the family to try to establish the last known decision of the individual.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 32973/22-27, to detail when Land and Property Services first commenced engagement with Kilroot Power station, to recalculate the rateable value of the site.
answer_text: Land and Property Services (LPS) first commenced engagement with Kilroot Power Station regarding its rateable valuation in October 2023, following notification of the cessation of coal-fired generation. Engagement has continued in relation to the installation of new turbines, and for the purposes of assessing the Net Annual Value (NAV) for Reval 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) what personal details must be provided before an nidirect account can be availed of; and (ii) who retains this information.
answer_text: NI Direct is operated by Department of Finance and is the main gateway for individuals in Northern Ireland to access a wide range of government services. Full details can be found at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/identity-assurance#toc-0. A NI Direct account is unique to individuals and is trusted by government departments and agencies for secure digital transactions, such as accessing MyCare app, applying for licences, legal aid and more. The Department of Finance requires the following data to set up a basic account:  A valid email addressPersonal details including, name, date of birth A Secure password Some services require a verified identity, which can be done by uploading a valid passport or driving licence, take and submit selfie, confirm address details and complete multi-factor authentication via SMS or email. Personal information collected during the NI Direct Account creation and verification process is encrypted and stored in a database managed by the Department of Finance. Access is restricted, with only authorised personnel permitted to manage or administer the supporting technical infrastructure.The Department of Finance’s Privacy Notice, which provides a comprehensive explanation of data collection and retention practices, is available at: https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/publications/digital-security-and-finance-shared-services-privacy-notices.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the amount within her budget that was set aside for police pay.
answer_text: My Department’s Resource DEL budget for 2025-26 was allocated in full to DoJ business areas following careful consideration of the financial pressures they had reported as part of the budget setting process. The pressures included the estimated costs of pay awards in 2025-26, at 3%, including for PSNI staff and officers. As Accounting Officer for PSNI, the Chief Constable is responsible for determining the amount set aside for police pay within the overall allocated PSNI budget. The implementation of the 2025 police pay award remains a priority for me and I will continue to work jointly with the Chief Constable and the Policing Board to explore all options which, alongside the additional funding from the Executive, will allow PSNI to demonstrate affordability of the pay review body recommendations.  I remain optimistic that this can be resolved.Ultimately, the Chief Constable is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board for operational matters and I remain committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board. You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in the question to PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, given that the current Go Succeed programme comes to an end in March 2026, to detail any plans in place for a replacement scheme, or any co-design group, to progress preparatory design work.
answer_text: My Department does not have a role in the administration of Go Succeed, which is funded through the Shared Prosperity Fund. It was anticipated that this funding would be transferred to the Executive following the British Government’s Spending Review in June.		In anticipation of that announcement my Department was ready to establish a co-design group.  However, the British Government has instead created a role for the NIO. I have written to the Secretary of State seeking clarity on the future of this vital programme. Disappointingly, that clarity has not yet been provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will update messaging used in cervical screening information to make clear it does not detect ovarian or any other gynaecological cancer.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency’s (PHA) public facing information and resources for all screening programmes are kept under regular review and are updated as necessary. The PHA has confirmed they will update the information provided on the NIDirect website to advise that cervical screening does not detect ovarian cancer. PHA will also be making similar changes to their patient leaflets for the Northern Ireland Cervical Screening Programme at their next review. In addition, the PHA will continue to increase public awareness of the signs and symptoms of Ovarian Cancer, through regular social media channels to promote cancer awareness messaging. This includes information on signs and symptoms, and healthy changes people can make to help reduce the risk of developing cancer in the future.  In addition, the PHA website provides information about signs and symptoms of a range of cancers, including ovarian cancer, which explains what people can do if concerned, and signposts to recommended sources of support or further information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any further measures his Department is taking to recover the £145.4 million in unpaid rates as at 31 August 2025.
To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of Judge Burgess’s recommendations in the fundamental review of criminal legal aid that have been implemented by her Department, since August 2024.
answer_text: Judge Burgess made 27 wide ranging recommendations broadly covering four areas: remuneration; processes; oversight; and wider system issues.In considering the recommendations relating to remuneration made by Judge Burgess, the Department has had regard to the statutory criteria outlined in Article 37 of the Legal Aid, Advice and Assistance (Northern Ireland) Order 1981 and Article 47 of the Access to Justice (Northern Ireland) Order 2003, namely: • 	The time and skill which work of the description to which the Rules relate requires; • 	The number and general competence of persons undertaking work of that description; • 	The cost to public funds of any provision made by the Rules; and • 	The need to secure value for money.Therefore, the implementation of any of the recommendations arising from the review was subject to the consideration of the Department in the context of the current budgetary position and necessary financial approvals.		Over the last few months, we have also initiated consultation and published a post consultation report on proposals to implement a significant 16% uplift in fees for civil and criminal legal aid, enhanced remuneration of indictable only offences at the Youth Court, increased Stage 1 parole fees, enhanced remuneration for asylum and immigration work, increased travel and mileage rates, and initiated the legislative process to give effect to all new rates.				My Department has consulted upon a number of the recommendations contained within Judge Burgess report and are at an advanced stage of introducing an increase of 16% in legal aid fees, enhanced remuneration of indictable only offences at the Youth Court to align with equivalent Crown Court fees, increased Stage 1 parole fees, enhanced remuneration for asylum and immigration work, increased travel hourly rates and mileage rates; and interim payment of disbursements.				I have also established an independent Working Group under the Chairmanship of His Honour Judge Burgess to consider other potential changes to criminal legal aid fees. The focus of the Group, which includes representatives from the Bar and the Law Society, is on ensuring fair proportionate remuneration that represents value for money. It has proved a positive forum and has developed proposals for enhanced fees for PACE; interim payment of disbursements for public representation in criminal, civil and family cases; and a proposal for a refresher fee pilot. The Working Group is also developing interim payment of fees in criminal, civil and family and is considering potential remuneration models for other aspects of criminal legal aid.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the status of the Enabling Access to Justice Reform Programme, including what has been achieved to date, in line with delivery plan objectives.
answer_text: The Enabling Access to Justice Reform Programme is wide ranging and ambitious and therefore takes a multiyear approach to enhancing access to justice and ensuring the system is sustainable, transparent and resources are used to best effect. The Delivery Plan is therefore expected to evolve over time as additional evidence emerges and stakeholder engagement continues. The Programme is also predicated on resourcing. We have nonetheless made significant progress since the launch of Programme. That includes a consultation on the draft Delivery Plan earlier this year following which I expect to publish a revised Plan in coming weeks detailing actions for the remainder of the mandate. While consultation has been ongoing, we have continued to take steps to deliver on Plan objectives including development of new fees for advice and representation in applications for immigration and asylum. The new fees have been determined following positive detailed engagement with the Immigration Practitioners Group which has enabled us to develop the evidence base required to support changes to the fee regime that address the concerns of practitioners. Over the last few months, we have also initiated consultation and published a post consultation report on proposals to implement a significant 16% uplift in fees for civil and criminal legal aid and mileage rates and initiated the legislative process to give effect to all new rates. I have also established an independent Working Group under the Chairmanship of His Honour Judge Burgess to consider other potential changes to criminal legal aid fees. The focus of the Group, which includes representatives from the Bar and the Law Society, is on ensuring fair proportionate remuneration that represents value for money. It has proved a positive forum and has developed proposals for enhanced fees for PACE; interim payment of disbursements for public representation in criminal, civil and family cases; and a proposal for a refresher fee pilot. The Working Group is also developing interim payment of fees in criminal, civil and family and is considering potential remuneration models for other aspects of criminal legal aid. Some of the proposals emerging from the Working Group are in fulfilment of Delivery Plan commitments and others have emerged as the work of the Group has progressed.  Exploring those emergent issues has had an inevitable impact on timelines for formal consultation and implementation but I believe consideration has been critical to responsive, constructive engagement and the continued availability of high quality advice and representation through the provision of fair proportionate representation. The Working Group is due to report on 31 October 2025 and work is in train to ensure early response. 		I also expect to publish proposals shortly for reform of financial eligibility rules for legal aid and new approaches to merits testing to ensure the proper purpose of legal aid of protecting the most vulnerable is fulf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, following your statement on Enabling Access to Justice Programme in the Assembly on 2 December 2024, in which you accepted a recommendation from the independent fundamental review of criminal aid in Northern Ireland that there should be an immediate increase to fees for publicly funded legal work, to detail whether any fees for any element of legal aid work, undertaken by legal professionals, have been uplifted.
answer_text: The uplift to legal aid fees forms part of a major programme of reform within the Enabling Access to Justice remit. The necessary steps are being progressed by officials across the Department including finalising the business case which covers the wider Enabling Access to Justice programme not just the increases in legal aid fees.Progression of the business case has been delayed to enable further amendments to legal aid fees that have been developed through consultation with stakeholders and the criminal legal aid working group. It is important that the programme business case accurately reflects the concerns of the professions and reflects the current position. Further impediments have also resulted as officials reacted to emerging issues impacting the wider reform programme.It is forecast that the legislation giving effect the enhanced levels of legal aid remuneration will come into effect approximately 6 weeks after business case approval has been secured.The Legal Aid Services Agency (LSA) have updated the Case Management System to ensure the increase of fees - including backdating claims to 1 December 2024 – may be claimed and paid when the new fees come into operation. The LSA report as of the end of September that £3.87m of backdated payments are ready to be processed automatically, without the need for legal representatives to submit any additional claim.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail why the fees for legal services, provided to government departments, have increased in the region of 25 per cent, while fees for legal aid work have not been increased.
answer_text: The fees allocated for legal services provided to government departments are not within the scope of the Department of Justice and are set by the Department of Finance and overseen by a moderation panel.The uplift to legal aid fees forms part of a major programme of reform within the Enabling Access to Justice remit. The necessary steps are being progressed by officials across the Department including finalising the business case which covers the wider Enabling Access to Justice programme not just the increases in legal aid fees.Progression of the business case has been delayed to enable further amendments to legal aid fees that have been developed through consultation with stakeholders and the criminal legal aid working group. It is important that the programme business case accurately reflects the concerns of the professions and reflects the current position. Further impediments have also resulted as officials reacted to emerging issues impacting the wider reform programme.It is forecast that the legislation giving effect the enhanced levels of legal aid remuneration will come into effect approximately 6 weeks after business case approval has been secured.The Legal Aid Services Agency (LSA) have updated the Case Management System to ensure the increase of fees - including backdating claims to 1 December 2024 – may be claimed and paid when the new fees come into operation. The LSA report as of the end of September that £3.87m of backdated payments are ready to be processed automatically, without the need for legal representatives to submit any additional claim.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any campaigns his Department ran to inform people how to report adverse events from the COVID-19 vaccine.
answer_text: My Department has not run any specific campaigns informing people how to report adverse events, but it has published the following communications encouraging reporting of side effects and adverse incidents:  Yellow Card Reporting Site for Healthcare Products Used in Coronavirus:  https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/HSS%28MD%29-32-2020.pdfUpdated Guidance on Managing Allergic Reactions:  https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/hssmd-84-2020.pdfFact file: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/news/covid-vaccine-fact-file-bogus-claims-and-side-effectsFact file: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/news/fact-file-dont-be-misled-about-vaccine-side-effectsThe autumn 2025 COVID-19 vaccination campaign has just begun, and all individuals invited to receive the vaccine should have access to a leaflet (see link to the PHA COVID patient leaflet: https://www.publichealth.hscni.net/sites/default/files/2025-09/COVID%20patient%20leaflet%20%E2%80%93%20autumn%2009_25%20FINAL%20Approved.pdf) which sets out the common side effects and serious effects and explains what to do if anyone experiences these.  In addition the following page on the NI Direct website https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/coronavirus-covid-19-vaccine-safety - provides information on the COVID-19 vaccine, including side effects, and a link to the Yellow Card reporting site to report a side effect.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how they promote Northern Ireland to potential investors as part of their work on the Investment Strategy for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The ISNI is focused on investment in and improving our public infrastructure. We know this can act as a catalyst for attracting wider investment which will be key to delivering for all our communities.Much work continues on critical projects ahead of a final ISNI and on improvements in how we plan, prioritise and deliver major projects.  As we work to finalise the ISNI, our message is that this place is open for business. We are committed to working with partners across this society and globally to meet the needs of today while planning for the infrastructure we will need in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, given the additional 1,145 hours permitted for Kilroot Power Station are measured on a five year average, to outline how this will impact the security of electricity supply over the 2025-2030 period.
answer_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy,  to ask the Minister for the Economy, given the additional 1,145 hours permitted for Kilroot Power Station are measured on a five year average, to outline how this will impact the security of electricity supply over the 2025-2030 period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he would consider running a new public information campaign to assist people who have suffered injuries from COVID-19 vaccines.
answer_text: All vaccines and medicines have some side effects although not everybody gets them, and the vast majority are normally mild and short lasting.  Unfortunately, in some rare cases, an individual may suffer an injury due to receiving a vaccine.  Thankfully the number of actual injuries linked to vaccination, including COVID-19 vaccines, is extremely small and would not justify running a specific public information campaign.The autumn 2025 Covid-19 vaccination campaign has just begun, and all individuals invited to receive the vaccine should have access to a leaflet (see link to the PHA https://www.publichealth.hscni.net/sites/default/files/2025-09/COVID%20patient%20leaflet%20–%20autumn%2009_25%20FINAL%20Approved.pdf ) which sets out the potential common side effects and serious effects associated with vaccination and explains what to do if anyone experiences these.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what follow up surveillance and reporting was completed on serious adverse reactions to the COVID-19 vaccine.
answer_text: The Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) is responsible for regulating all medicines and medical devices in the UK by ensuring they work and are acceptably safe.  MHRA continuously monitors the safety of all medicines through robust monitoring and surveillance and operates the Yellow Card Scheme on behalf of the Commission on Human Medicines (CHM).  This collects and monitors information provided by healthcare professionals and members of the public (patients, users, or carers) regarding suspected adverse incidents or safety concerns that involve healthcare products (including vaccines).  Specific details of the follow up surveillance and reporting completed on suspected serious adverse reactions to a COVID-19 vaccine should be directed to the MHRA.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether recipients of the COVID-19 vaccines were told at the point of vaccination that ongoing post-marketing monitoring relied on their self-reporting of side effects.
answer_text: Prior to the launch of the COVID-19 vaccination programme in December 2020, and before all subsequent winter and spring vaccination campaigns held since, health care professionals are provided with training to enable them to deliver immunisations safely and effectively and to answer patients’ questions. This includes reporting of vaccine side effects.  In addition, individuals seeking to receive a COVID-19 vaccine are provided with a leaflet which provides information about the programme.  This includes information on who to contact if they are concerned with any side effects as well as how to report suspected side effects to the Yellow Card Scheme. (see attached link to the PHA leaflet for the current autumn 2025 campaign: https://www.publichealth.hscni.net/sites/default/files/2025-09/COVID%20patient%20leaflet%20%E2%80%93%20autumn%2009_25%20FINAL%20Approved.pdf).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether Land and Property Services have received all information required to recalculate the rates bill for Kilroot Power station.
answer_text: ANSWERLand & Property Services continues to engage with relevant stakeholders to obtain the necessary information required to complete the recalculation of the rates liability for the site in question. Whilst the process of assessing the rateable value at this site is complex the matter is at an advanced stage. Any change in liability will come into effect in line with relevant legislative provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Sands July 2025 report Lost In The System: Bereaved Parents Experiences of Mental Health Care Following Baby Loss, that showed only 10 per cent of bereaved parents received the psychological support they wanted through the NHS, to outline what can his Department do in order to ensure parents receive the best support possible.
answer_text: My Department recognises the profound emotional impact that pregnancy or the loss baby can have on individuals and families. Each of the five Health and Social Care Trusts provides a range of support services, which may include:- Bereavement Midwives: Specially trained midwives are available in many Trusts to provide compassionate, one-to-one support following miscarriage or baby loss.- Counselling Services: Trusts offer access to psychological therapies, either directly or through referral, to support individuals experiencing grief, trauma, or mental health challenges following pregnancy loss.- Referral Pathways: Health professionals can refer patients to external organisations such as the Miscarriage Association, SANDS, and Cruse Bereavement Support, which offer specialised resources and peer support.- Early Pregnancy Units (EPUs): These units often serve as the first point of contact for women experiencing miscarriage and can provide immediate emotional support and follow-up care.- Information and Signposting: Trusts provide written and digital resources to help individuals understand their options and access further support.In addition, my Department is actively supporting the development of a Baby Loss Certificate Scheme. This initiative, currently under public consultation, aims to offer formal recognition of pregnancy loss before 24 weeks’ gestation, acknowledging the emotional significance of such losses.Furthermore, my Department is progressing with the establishment of a Maternity and Neonatal Partnership, which will oversee the implementation of a regional action plan to improve maternity care, including bereavement support. This work is informed by the recent independent review of midwifery services and reflects our commitment to ensuring that all women and families receive safe, respectful, and compassionate care.We remain committed to listening to those with lived experience and to ensuring that support services are accessible, equitable, and responsive to the needs of all who experience pregnancy loss.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the duty of care placed on the prison service, when a released prisoner’s transport fails to arrive at the prison.
answer_text: Once a person is released from custody, they are no longer the responsibility of the Northern Ireland Prison Service.  However, each prison has informal arrangements in place to seek to support individuals where possible and in the event that their own transport fails to arrive. This may include the opportunity to phone another family member or friend and assistance in getting to the nearest public transport hub.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has made an assessment on the effect of the Gardasil HPV vaccine on neurological health, specifically the risk of developing functional neurological disorders.
answer_text: My Department follows the advice of the Medicines & Healthcare Products Regulatory Agency’s (MHRA) who have a statutory responsibility for ensuring the safety of all medicines and medical devices in use in the UK – including the Gardasil HPV vaccine. Vaccines are only deployed for use following rigorous clinical trials and extensive analysis of the vaccine’s safety, quality, and effectiveness by experts from the MHRA. The monitoring of vaccine safety does not stop once a vaccine has been approved - the safety continues to be monitored continually using well developed and pre-existing surveillance networks.Extensive reviews of vaccine safety have concluded that there is no credible evidence to support a link between the Gardasil HPV vaccine and the development of a range of chronic illnesses. This includes reviews carried out in other countries such as the Centre for Disease Control and Prevention in the US, the World Health Organization, and the European Medicines Agency, who have looked carefully at all the cases that have been reported.Studies have shown that the HPV vaccination programme is highly effective at reducing cervical cancer rates, with cervical cancer rates 87% lower in vaccinated women compared to previous generations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action his Department is taking to ensure that social workers have adequate training and experience to offer correct guidance and advice on direct payments.
answer_text: The majority of social workers in NI graduate from our local universities, having successfully completed the Degree in Social Work. The NI Degree in Social Work is made up of academic teaching and practice learning placements with a range of social work employers. Student social workers undertake a wide-ranging, generic training programme which includes teaching inputs on welfare systems and personalisation.Whilst on practice placement, where relevant, student social workers will also assist service users with accessing appropriate welfare benefits, including direct payments.Once they have graduated from university, social workers are provided with post-qualification induction and training in their chosen area of work. If working in adult services, this will include service user entitlements relating to direct payments, where that is relevant.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of persons released from prison, including Hydebank, that were provided with a tent to sleep in, over the last two years.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Prison Service does not provide tents to persons released from custody.The Service ensures that housing advice is provided to individuals leaving prison where there is an identified housing need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will urgently look at expanding perinatal mental health services to ensure that specialist psychological support pathways for bereaved parents are available in all Health and Social Care Trusts.

To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has considered adopting legislation similar to the Right to Rent scheme in Great Britain, in order to enhance landlord's obligations, to ensure due diligence regarding prospective tenants, and restrict profiting from overcrowding.
answer_text: The Right to Rent scheme was introduced as part of the Immigration Act 2014. The Immigration Act 2016 (the 2016 Act) increased the level of penalties to landlords, letting agents and householders and also increased their powers of eviction. The 2016 Act also included provisions to extend the scheme to Scotland, Wales or Northern Ireland.You will be aware, from my statement to the Assembly on Houses in Multiple Occupation, that I have written to the Home Secretary and the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland requesting they extend the Scheme to Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any actions he can take to ensure greater access and availability of specialist psychological support for bereaved parents.

To ask the Minister for Communities, given the issues communities are facing with Houses in Multiple Occupation, to detail whether his Department will consider legislation, like the Private Tenancies Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, that might better control numbers of occupants in a property, to help combat issues with overcrowding.
answer_text: The Houses in Multiple Occupation Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 allows councils when granting a licence for a House in Multiple Occupation (HMO) to set the maximum number of occupants in a HMO, taking account of the specified room standards and space standards. As I outlined in my statement to the Assembly, the Act provides for substantial penalties to be levied on those landlords who do not comply with licensing conditions. This includes a fine of up to £20,000 for exceeding the licensed occupancy of an HMO. I have written to all council Chief Executives to remind them of council responsibilities for HMOs and the extensive powers under the Houses in Multiple Occupation Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 to investigate, enforce and punish those landlords acting outside the law.  My letter also included the ask that their Environmental Health Officers remain vigilant to issues relating to HMOs and for the prompt reporting of any concerns to the NIHMO Unit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that it's not in the Executive Legislation Programme 2025-2026, when he intends to bring forward his Public Health Bill.
answer_text: An extensive public consultation on policy proposals to inform the development of a new Public Health Bill for Northern Ireland took place last year. The Department received over 8,000 responses to the consultation. Analysis of these responses was particularly complex given the volume, diversity and format of consultation responses received. A summary report has now been prepared and will be published on the Department’s website in the coming weeks.Taking into account the obvious level of public interest and the wide variety of issues highlighted in the public consultation response,  as well as the timescales required to address these adequately, I have taken the decision to instruct my officials to reengage with key stakeholders to refine and agree any policy proposals that will ensure any future legislation is fit for purpose.  As such I believe it is unlikely that any Public Health Bill will be introduced, or even drafted, in the next twelve months, but given the importance of the issue and the obvious strength of feeling in relation to it I would much rather the engagement be correct rather than hurried. The Bill has therefore been removed from the Executive Legislation Programme for 2025-2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, given her written statement on the 1 October 2025 regarding electricity supply and the increase in permitted operating hours for Kilroot power station, to detail (i) the amount it will cost the bill payers per each additional permitted hour; and (ii) the amount this will place on the average (a) domestic; and (b) industrial, electricity bill.
answer_text: Kilroot Power Station’s generation costs are recovered through the normal market mechanisms. There is not expected to be an impact on a typical consumer bill of the units running for an increased number of hours at this time.  A number of factors determine the cost of an electricity bill, such as global fuel prices, levels of demand, weather, costs of running, maintaining and upgrading the network, and power plant costs.  The Utility Regulator monitors such factors and the commercial behaviours of all market participants to ensure that only necessary costs are passed through to consumers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to detail  the payments made, to date, for each of the two new gas units in Kilroot power station from (a) the balancing market; (b) the system services; and (c) the capacity market; and (ii) given that these units have run hour restrictions of 1500hrs per year, whether the (a) capacity contracts awarded; and (b) capacity payments received, are derated to reflect this.
answer_text: Kilroot Power Station’s generation costs are recovered through the normal market mechanisms; it is paid for the energy it produces, system services it provides and receives capacity payments as per its capacity contracts. There is not expected to be an impact on a typical consumer bill of the units running for an increased number of hours at this time.  A number of factors determine the cost of an electricity bill, such as global fuel prices, levels of demand, weather, costs of running, maintaining, and upgrading the network, and power plant costs.  The Utility Regulator monitors such factors and the commercial behaviours of all market participants to ensure that only necessary costs are passed through to consumers.		Decisions in relation to the de-rating methodology are for the SEM Committee. The SEM Committee introduced de-rating for run hour limited power stations in 2022 so the capacity contracts awarded to Kilroot before this date would not be subject to the de-rating.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of additional permitted hours given to Kilroot power station, as per your statement on the 1 October 2025 on electricity supply; and (ii) whether these are additional hours or are they an advancement on next years permitted hours.
answer_text: Kilroot Power Station has had a variation on its permit to include a further 1,145 hours of operation in 2025.These hours are measured on a five-year average, with the station not permitted to exceed 2250 hours per calendar year.  These additional 1,145 hours will be included in the five-year average.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any requirements are placed upon landlords or letting agencies to conduct a background check into renters' settlement status to ensure a prospective tenant has the legal right to rent and live in the property.
To ask the Minister of Justice when the Northern Ireland Prison Service cap badge will feature King Charles III's cypher, as is the practice being rolled out in the UK.
answer_text: AQO 2516 22-27 - ANSWER A new Royal Cypher was created following the coronation of King Charles III.   The Royal Symbols of State guidance sets out the principles to be followed, including avoiding wastage and changes being implemented at no or low cost or if truly necessary.  The Cabinet Office has not placed a timeline on when symbols should be changed and is leaving that decision to organisations.   The Northern Ireland Prison Service intends to roll out necessary change gradually as items reach the end of their natural life cycle.  Prison Service officials will liaise with recognised trade unions on this process.   With respect to the UK prisons generally, Scotland is unaffected as the Royal Cypher is not incorporated in their uniform and HMPPS is following a similar process to NIPS and has not yet transitioned to the new cypher.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the additional action her Department will take to help improve the skills gaps that remain in our economy, particularly in sectors like construction and people in the trade business.
answer_text: I have launched my Skills Action Plan which will set out my approach to tackle the skills gaps in the economy. Central to my approach is to work collaboratively with sectors to develop pathways and curriculum that best meets their current and future skills needs. This new approach has seen some early success with the launch of the Green Skills Action plan. Following the launch we have now moved into the delivery phase which includes several working groups tasked with upskilling and reskilling the workforce. This includes close engagement with the Construction Industry Training Board who provide valuable insight into the needs of the construction sector through research and engagement with individual businesses. We also provide a wide variety of funded training programmes for the construction sector, including Apprenticeships, Traineeships and 50 individual short courses in areas such as retrofitting, electrical and welding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the steps her Department will take to provide further (i) support; and (ii) incentives, to self-employed tradespeople, when it comes to taking on apprentices.
answer_text: Self-employed tradespeople or sole traders who hire an apprentice are eligible for the Directed Training element of the apprenticeship (also known as off-the-job training) to be funded under the ApprenticeshipsNI or Higher Level Apprenticeships programmes .Under ApprenticeshipsNI, employers, including those who are self-employed, are eligible for an Employer Incentive payment upon successful completion of all elements of the apprenticeship framework by their apprentice.The Apprenticeships Action Plan contains a commitment by the Department to undertake a strategic review and research into apprenticeship effectiveness and experience, which will include the experiences of employers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, given the statistics from NISRA that showed 25,000 people aged 16-24 years old in Northern Ireland are not in education, employment or training, to outline any action her Department will take to expand the viability of apprenticeships, so that these people can develop their skills and seek employment.
answer_text: Apprenticeship provision here has seen substantial growth in numbers, with 40% growth in the past 5 years. I want to help more employers and participants benefit from apprenticeships.The Apprenticeship Action Plan, which was launched on 11 August 2025 outlines my commitment to improvement, growth, and inclusivity in apprenticeships.   The plan seeks to increase the range of employers and participants that can access high-quality opportunities, building a more skilled, innovative and inclusive economy.One key intervention is the Apprenticeship Inclusion Challenge Fund, which specifically promotes apprenticeship uptake from three groups who are currently underrepresented across apprenticeship programmes – women, individuals with a disability and individuals from areas of deprivation or disadvantage. In addition, the Department is increasing the visibility of apprenticeships in promotional materials to encourage broader participation. Careers Advisers also play a key role by providing impartial advice and guidance, encouraging individuals to explore all career options. The Careers Service ‘Guide to Career Entry Routes’, available on NI Direct, also sets out detail on how to enter both training and apprenticeships alongside other progression routes, for people of all ages and backgrounds. Furthermore, through the implementation of the Careers Action Plan, the Careers Service will seek to prioritise and strengthen support for those who experience barriers at all ages.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail when the Arts Council plan to contact bands who have applied for funding under the Musical Instruments for Bands Programme.
answer_text: All applicants have now been notified.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to outline how she intends to keep a stable electricity supply over the winter months; and (ii) for a breakdown of the cost for any additional measures she is considering to provide this security.
answer_text: I refer to my statement of 1 October 2025, providing an update on the electricity supply for the North.Department for the Economy - UPDATE ON ELECTRICITY SUPPLY

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact that the deemed consent laws have had on organ donation, including the number of people (i) receiving successful organ donation; and (ii) waiting for an organ transplant.
answer_text: Consent for organ donation to proceed after someone’s death can either be expressed (for example, by a decision recorded on the Organ Donor Register) or deemed (since the introduction of Dáithí’s Law, which came into effect from June 2023).  Although it is still too early to draw definitive conclusions about the full impact of the legislation on organ donation rates in Northern Ireland (NI), the early indicators are encouraging:  In 2023/24, 14 of the 73 consented donors in NI were via the deemed consent procedure.  Deceased donors from NI resulted in 168 life-saving transplants. In 2024/25, 11 of the 49 consented donors in NI were via the deemed consent procedure.  Deceased donors from NI resulted in 123 life-saving transplants. In the 5 months from 1 April to 31 August 2025, 13 out of the 35 consented donors in NI were via the deemed consent procedure.  Deceased donors from NI have resulted in 103 life-saving transplants so far this year. A further positive impact of the legislation has been the associated programme of public information and engagement, including the Public Health Agency’s “You Could Be a Lifesaver” campaign which continues to enable a positive cultural shift in Northern Ireland.  The campaign has encouraged many thousands of informed conversations and positive actions about organ donation and deemed consent in homes, workplaces, school and communities across NI, as evidenced by increased Organ Donation Register (ODR) registrations (increasing from 53% of the population in 2022/23 to 59% today – the highest of any UK region). Unfortunately, the number of people waiting on an organ transplant continues to exceed the number of donated organs.  Because the pool of potential donors is very small, and demand for donated organs each year continues to exceed supply, every potential donor matters and every consented donor can significantly impact the number of transplants.  As at 30 June 2025, 161 people in NI were waiting for an organ transplant.  Further information on the number of people receiving and awaiting an organ transplant in NI is published on the NHS Blood and Transplant website at: https://www.organdonation.nhs.uk/helping-you-to-decide/about-organ-donation/statistics-about-organ-donation/ My Department continues to work closely with the Public Health Agency and NHS Blood and Transplant to ensure the public receives accurate information, promote awareness and encourage registration on the NHS Organ Donor Register, and to support families in understanding and honouring their loved ones’ decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline all conditions placed on the £50 million of Financial Transactions Capital provided by His Majesty's Government in June 2025.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) current staff shortages in the Council for Catholic Maintained Schools; and (ii) how does this compare to the month of September, in the past five years.
answer_text: The Council for Catholic Maintained Schools (CCMS) currently has six vacant posts.In each of the past five years, the number of vacant posts in CCMS was as follows:YearNo. of Vacant Posts2020620216202262023720247

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how the Council for Catholic Maintained Schools is meeting its targets around prompt payment to (i) external Performance Review and Staff Development advisors; and (ii) all other external contractors.
answer_text: As set out in Managing Public Money Northern Ireland, public sector organisations are bound by legislation which seeks to ensure that, as it relates to commercial transactions, the payment period does not exceed 30 calendar days after a debtor receives an invoice.Notwithstanding, the Northern Ireland Executive recognises that the public sector should set a strong example by paying promptly and public sector organisations should aim to pay suppliers wherever possible within 10 working days. The Council for Catholic Maintained Schools (CCMS) is committed to meeting this target.To date, in the financial year 2025-26, CCMS has paid all suppliers within this 10-day target as set out in the table below:2025-26 AprilMayJuneJulyAugustYear-to-date totalsCCMSTotal invoices paid4632474928202Total invoices paid within 10 days4632474928202% of invoices paid within 10 days100100100100100100Prompt payment performance of all Arm’s Length Bodies within my Department are disclosed monthly on the Department's website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any financial support his Department can give to homeowners who need to meet Historic Environment Division requirements, given the increased cost of completing work to this standard and the fact a recent report into the condition of these buildings showed many were in disrepair and that their condition has declined by 25 per cent in twenty years.
answer_text: I doubled the support available this year to listed building owners from £240k to £500k. This support, via the Historic Environment Fund, is focused at helping owners keep the weather out by undertaking roof and window repairs. Applications opened last autumn to allow the maximum amount of time to carry out work in this financial year. Owners who would like to be informed when next year’s fund opens, can send their details to historicenvironmentfund@communities-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether her Department will ensure more support is given to homeowners, who need to meet the Historic Environment Division requirements, to help ensure that our built heritage is protected and suitable for future generations to come.
answer_text: Yes. I have already doubled the support available this year to listed building owners from £240k to £500k via the department’s Historic Environment Fund.  I am keen to do more, and to this end, my officials are currently developing a business case, building upon departmental research published earlier this year, looking at how best to stimulate investment in the historic environment. This will seek to improve support for homeowners and help them meet the costs they face, given the wide benefit these buildings bring to our society.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance Whether he will reconsider his Department's position on not providing rate relief to homeowners who need to meet Historic Environment Division requirements, given (i) research from the Department for Communities into the condition of our listed buildings that showed 36 per cent were in poor or very poor condition; and (ii) the high levels of expenses associated with repairs.
answer_text: ANSWERHistoric Environment requirements associated with properties is a matter that falls within the legislative, policy and funding competence of the Department for Communities. The concerns raised regarding the condition and cost of homeowners maintaining listed buildings are matters that should be raised directly with the Communities Minister.As regards the rating system the valuation process for rating reflects what a property might reasonably sell for on the open market between a willing buyer and seller, subject to the statutory assumptions. As valuations are based on market evidence, any constraints, such as listed status, planning restrictions, or significant repair obligations, will be reflected in the capital value to the extent that the market itself recognises and adjusts for them. In addition, listed buildings and historic monuments are excluded from vacant rating liability. There are no current plans to provide further rate relief for this area. The Department already legislates for over a quarter of a billion in non-domestic rate support, a further £64M of non-means tested domestic rate support, while means-tested support is also provided for domestic ratepayers at a more generous level than anywhere else in these islands. The rating system remains our only substantive revenue raising lever for public spending. Every pound provided in new rate relief will be paid for either by other ratepayers in the system paying more or by a reduction in public spending.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 27889/22-27, (i) why information for the Northern Health and Social Care Trust has not been provided; and (ii) when it will be provided.
answer_text: Following the receipt of AQW 27889/22-27, clinical data was requested from each of the relevant Health and Social Care Trusts in order to collate the data and complete the response.  Although the Corporate Communication team within each of the HSCTs endeavour to provide the required information from clinical systems or through clinical teams within the requested timeframe, on occasion this information is not provided within the timeframe.  This may be due to service capacity, staffing pressures or staff absence.  Northern Health and Social Care Trust have now provided the information in response to AQW 27889/22-27 and apologise for the delay. Northern Health and Social Care Trust have 14 patients who are currently using Dexcom 6 sensors; 5 children under 12 years and 9 young people between 12 and 16 years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 27889/22-27, how many of the Dexcom G6 sensors, including for the Northern Health and Social Care Trust, are funded by the NHS.
answer_text: For people living with diabetes using insulin pump therapy, the most appropriate system is determined in consultation between the clinician and the patient and/or the patient’s carer, based on their clinical need.Dexcom G6 senor is a type of complex Continuous Glucose monitoring device for monitoring blood glucose levels Across the five Health and Social Care Trusts, there are 166 children currently using Dexcom G6 sensors.  Across four Health and Social Care Trusts, 146 of these sensors are funded directly by the Trust in which the patient receives their diabetes care.   The remainder of Dexcom G6 sensors are self-funded. Information has been requested from the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust but responses are yet to be received due to staff absence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 27889/22-27, why there are more Dexcom G6 sensors used per head of population in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust compared to other areas.
answer_text: For people living with diabetes using insulin pump therapy, the most appropriate system is determined in consultation between the clinician and the patient and/or the patient’s carer, based on their clinical need.  Dexcom G6 senor is a type of complex Continuous Glucose monitoring device that enables closed loop technology, although it may not be the most appropriate system for some patients.Dexcom G6 sensor is currently not listed on the NI Drug tariff and therefore cannot be prescribed in Primary Care. Funding for Continuous Glucose monitoring devices which are not listed on the N. Ireland Drug Tariff would require to come directly from the Trusts or be self-funded by the patient / patient’s family.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, how many children currently using Dexcom G7 sensors for monitoring blood glucose levels are aged (i) under 16; and (ii) under 12 years, including how many are funded by the NHS.
answer_text: Dexcom G7 senor is a type of complex Continuous Glucose monitoring device for monitoring blood glucose levels. The most appropriate insulin pump therapy system is determined in consultation between the clinician and the patient and/or the patient’s carer, based on their clinical need.  Within SEHSCT, SHSCT, NHSCT and WHSCT there are currently 41 children aged between 12 and 15 years, inclusive, using Dexcom G7 sensors.  35 of these sensors are currently funded by the NHS, with funding coming directly from the local Trust. The remaining number are self-funded.Within SEHSCT, SHSCT, NSHCT and WHSCT there are currently 92 children under 12 years using Dexcom G7 sensors.  86 of these sensors are currently funded by the NHS, with funding coming directly from the local Trust. The remaining number are self-funded.NHSCT does not hold a database in respect of parental funded alternatives.Within BHSCT, Dexcom G7 is not funded by the NHS, any Dexcom G7 is brought at risk by individual services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the support her Department provides for people over the age of 18 with autism and severe learning disabilities, wishing to attend further education but who need assistance with transport, having availed of an essential taxi service throughout all their school years.
answer_text: My Department recognises that every person should have access to further education and that transportation should not be a barrier.  There are therefore a number of mechanisms to support people, including those over the age of 18 with autism and severe learning disabilities, who wish to attend further education and need assistance with transport. The Home to College Transport initiative is aimed at all students up to the age of 19, including those with autism and severe learning disabilities and provides parity with the transport assistance available to young people who stay in a school setting.  The online application process offers applicants the opportunity to declare if they have needs which require alternative transportation other than the normal public transport options.Eligible students with autism and severe learning difficulties who are over the age of 19 can apply for a Further Education Grant or to the Hardship Fund which both offer a range of financial assistance, including transport. Each Further Education college has student support staff in place to help students gain access to appropriate support measures which meet their needs.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in light of their Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework, to outline what resources their Department can provide to the people of Ballymena concerned about sexual violence against women and girls.
answer_text: Our thoughts are with the victim of the recent alleged sexual assault in Ballymena and her family.  We unreservedly condemn all forms of violence, abuse and harm against women and girls.  Recently published PSNI statistics highlight that over four thousand sexual offences were recorded in the period May 2024 to April 2025.  This violence does not occur in a vacuum.The focus of the EVAWG Strategic Framework is on prevention: stopping the violence before it starts.  Key to this work is our commitment to community investment.  We are working in partnership with all councils on the delivery of the EVAWG Change Fund.  Councils are embedding action to end violence against women and girls into their service delivery and developing signposting and resources for local communities.  A total of £160,000 has been provided to Mid and East Antrim Borough Council through the EVAWG Local Change Fund.   As part of this, Momentum funding has already supported a wide range of initiatives in the area, including specialised training for staff in the Northern Trust, female personal safety initiatives, and development of toolkits and resources.  We are currently identifying further areas of work and support. In addition, we are investing £1.2 million through the Regional Change Fund to strengthen the impact and reach of programmes offered by key regional community and voluntary sector organisations with EVAWG expertise.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the remaining steps and timeframe for the commencement of the Being Open Framework.
answer_text: I launched a public consultation on the Framework on 10 December 2024.  The consultation lasted 15 weeks and closed on 28 March 2025.  A total of 270 responses were received. The Department is currently reviewing the responses to identify issues and recurring themes, and to provide analysis of the key aspects of the feedback.  A new Being Open Framework Writing Group is being established to consider the consultation responses and develop a revised Framework document.  In continuation of co-production principles, membership of the Writing Group will come from the Department, HSC Trusts, Service Users and representatives from Health Service Trade Unions.It is anticipated that the implementation of the new Regional Being Open Framework will commence in September 2025 with the new Framework being published at that time.In parallel, I can confirm that a Departmental response, including detailed analysis of the responses to the Duty of Candour Being Open Framework consultation, will also be published in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to outline how the roll-out of encompass in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust affects external postal communication to patients, including appointment letters; and (ii) for his assessment of how it affects those with mental health issues.
answer_text: The implementation of the encompass programme within Health and Social Care allows service users who register for the MyCare platform to choose digital communication as their preferred method of receiving correspondence. However, to ensure inclusivity and accessibility, appointment letters and other important communications continue to be sent by post to all service users, regardless of their digital preferences.For those who opt for digital communication, an email notification is sent to inform them that a letter is available to view through the MyCare app.At present, the roll-out of the encompass programme does not impact how correspondence is provided to mental health patients. In fact, patients have experienced benefits from improved access to their comprehensive healthcare records across Trusts. Additionally, the introduction of electronic document transfer to GPs has enhanced the speed and efficiency of sharing mental health information, compared to traditional postal methods. Further benefits of the encompass progamme are expected to emerge as the programme continues to develop.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any support, financial or otherwise, available for fenders of the film industry in Northern Ireland; and (ii) how many local companies work in this industry full time, broken down by (a) special effects; (b) armoury; (c) stunts; (d) wardrobe; (e) rigging; (f) hair and makeup; (g) sound; (h) props; and (i) construction.
answer_text: NI Screen actively supports both businesses and individuals through training, skills development and funding programmes.  Detail on the various schemes can be found at this link - Skills & Training - Northern Ireland ScreenNI Screen also maintains a Crew and Facilities database for information on ‘Upcoming Productions’ and provides advice on production schedules.Local companies are not required to register with NI Screen.  However, NI Screen is aware of the following:three special effects companies;two armoury companies;one rigging company;one props house; andfour construction companies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline (i) the eligibility criteria for the Access to Work (NI) programme; and (ii) whether this criteria has changed over the past five years.
answer_text: Access to Work (NI) can help people with disabilities who wish to take up employment or who are in work and experience difficulty related to their disability. It can also help employers who wish to recruit or retain people with disabilities in employment. In order to receive Access to Work (NI) assistance the following eligibility conditions must be satisfied. The applicant must: -Be disabled; this includes all those within the definition provided in the Disability Discrimination Act 1995 (DDA), and people who are not covered by the Act because their disability affects them only at work.Be in need of support at a job interview with an employer; with a job to start; or in a job, whether as an employed or self-employed person.Be in need of support to progress in, or take up work, on a more equal basis with non-disabled colleagues.Not be in receipt of Incapacity Benefit, Income Support in lieu of Incapacity Benefit, Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Severe Disability Allowance, or if currently in receipt will not continue to claim whilst in work. Part time workers who claim Job Seekers Allowance are not eligible to apply for support unless they will not continue to claim whilst in work . People under the age of 16 are not eligible for Access to Work (NI) support. There is no upper age limit for help as long as the employment is likely to continue. Be resident and working within Northern Ireland and paying Income Tax and National Insurance contributions to HMRC.In instances where the applicant for support lives outside of Northern Ireland, i.e. Republic of Ireland (ROI) and pays Income Tax and National Insurance contributions to HMRC, each application will be considered on a case-by-case basis with consideration made regarding the type of support being sought (Travel to Work, Support Worker, Special Aids and Equipment etc). Applications for programme support for residents outside of the UK and ROI will not be considered.The support the client will receive will depend on the needs of the person and criteria for programme support has not changed over the past 5 years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of film studios in Northern Ireland; and (ii) the current (a) capacity; and (b) order sheet, of each.
answer_text: There are four film studios, namely Belfast Harbour Studios; Titanic Studios; Loop Studios; and Studio Ulster. Information on individual studio capacity may be found at these links - Film & Television Studios - Northern Ireland Screen and Studio Ulster - Studio Ulster. The Belfast Harbour and Loop Studios are currently unused.  Titanic Studios is leased to Home Box Office, and Studio Ulster is hired on a day by day basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on when the investigation into excess deaths by the Public Health Agency will be completed.
answer_text: On 23 August 2022, the former Health Minister requested a further assessment of excess deaths in Northern Ireland, beyond that conducted by Northern Ireland Statistics Research Agency and, if possible, for a UK comparison to be made.  Following this request, the Information Analysis Directorate from the Department of Health and the Public Health Agency were tasked with taking forward the further investigation of excess deaths using the Office for Health Improvement and Disparities methodology in the first instance. This request was made via e-mail and was received by the Permanent Secretary, Chief Medical Officer, Chief Scientific Advisor and the Director of Communications within my Department.Following this request, a discussion between senior officials in my Department took place and the Information Analysis Directorate and the Public Health Agency were tasked with taking forward the further investigation of excess deaths using the Office for Health Improvement and Disparities methodology in the first instance.My Department will not be publishing any of the available internal correspondence in relation to the setting up of this investigation. The methodology was refined and Public Health Agency have obtained the necessary data to complete an analysis which is currently being written up for submission to the Department.Discussions with NISRA about the relationship between the analysis and that carried out by NISRA have been completed.  The initial report, from the Public Health Agency, will be received by my Department for consideration by officials within the next four weeks.The final report containing the findings from the investigation will be published when it is available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount of funding from her Department that has been delivered into (i) Armoy; (ii) Bushmills; (iii) Dervock; (iv) Mosside; and (v) Stranocum in the last ten years, including the projects funded.
answer_text: My Department does not hold comprehensive records of funding delivered over the last ten years for the communities identified and any attempt to do so would require an extensive exercise to be conducted across the department which might only be expected to produce data with a limited degree of exactitude, and this exercise itself would very likely involve disproportionate costs.  However, I can advise the following:-RoadsMy Department publishes the details of roads schemes in reports which are presented to councils annually. This includes structural maintenance, local transport & safety measures, minor works, structures and street lighting. The reports for the Causeway Coast and Glens Council area are available at the following link: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/search?query=annual+report+to+causeway+coast+and+glensActive TravelDetails of Active Travel schemes from 2018 onwards are available at the following links:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/dfi2024-0461-details-broken-down-local-authority-area-01-january-2018-funding-provided-1-cyclescooter-share-schemes-2-cycling-infrastructure-3-active-travel-measureshttps://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/dfi2024-0175-details-individual-project-ps12-13-million-annual-spend-active-travel-each-year-2020-2021-2023-2024RiversFrom a flood risk management perspective, Rivers Operations undertakes routine maintenance on designated watercourses across all operational areas and responds to incidents of watercourse related flooding. These operational areas reflect river catchments and are not commensurate with administrative boundaries or by individual cities, towns or other settlements. Works carried out in the specified areas include watercourse inspection and maintenance, culvert and manhole inspections and repairs, flood defence maintenance and repairs and flood response/flood clear up costs. Over the last 10 years it is estimated that £750k has been spent on these activities across these areas. Water and Drainage Policy Division/NIWNI Water has advised that according to the available information they have spent £5.86m in the specified area in the past 10 years. TranslinkTranslink have recently invested £13m in new zero emission battery-electric buses and the associated charging infrastructure in the North Antrin area. These vehicles serve Armoy, Bushmills, Dervock, Mosside and Stranocum. Community TransportMy Department provides grant support to the rural Dial-a-Lift service which may be used by some of those who live in the locations listed.Additional fundingMy Department has also contributed a total of £12.25m towards the Covid Recovery Revitalisation Programme and Covid Recovery Small Settlements Regeneration Programme which is administered by the Department for Communities. This included an allocation to Causeway Coast and Glens Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the data on the tangible effects and benefits, that is actual benefits other than the Government saying it is complying with EU Directives or stating the measures put in place will protect the environment, that the implementation of the Water Abstraction and Impoundment (Licensing) (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2006 has achieved on (i) water quality; (ii) an increase or decrease in protected mammals; (iii) an increase or decrease in fish and invertebrate populations; and (iv) an increase or decrease in bio
answer_text: The Water Abstraction and Impoundment (Licensing) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2006 are derived from the Water (Northern Ireland) Order 1999 and the Water Environment (Water Framework Directive) Regulations (NI) 2003 and are part of an integrated suite of regulations. The Water Abstraction and Impoundment (Licensing) Regulations (provides the Department with the authority to issue Abstraction and Impoundment Licences (AIL) to ensure water abstractions are sustainable and do not impact on the ecological status of the aquatic environment. The standards for licence conditions are set by The Water Framework Directive (Classification, Priority Substances and Shellfish Waters) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015.The licensing of water abstractions aims to manage the use of water resources efficiently and ensure sustainable access to water for all including commercial uses and recreational uses in addition to protecting the ecology of the waterways. The Department has an inspection programme to ensure operators are compliant with the conditions of their AIL. Whilst this programme, does not include the measuring or reporting of water quality or the other factors listed; other relevant regulations (e.g. Water Environment (Water Framework Directive) Regulations (NI) 2017 and the Fisheries Act 1966) provide for the monitoring of water quality, protected mammals, fish and invertebrate populations, biodiversity and the spread of alien species.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of wildfires in Northern Ireland and to assist in preventing fire spread, to detail any work completed by Forest Service in North Antrim to remove fallen trees in forested areas following recent storms, especially over fire breaks.
answer_text: Forest Service has cleared wind damaged trees and trees that have fallen across forest roads across its estate as result of the recent storms. Access through the forests in North Antrim, including Ballycastle, Ballypatrick, Slieveanorra, Breen and Glenariff has largely been restored, with a small number minor non-through tracks still requiring some tree clearance work.   Clearance of the forest roads provides access for Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service, and other personnel, to control forest fires. It is the forest road network and adjacent unplanted areas that are utilised as fire control zones in the prevention of fire spread across forest areas.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the cost of the pavement resurfacing scheme in Rathin Drive, Ballymena, completed in January 2022.
answer_text: The pavement resurfacing scheme in Rathin Drive was completed on 14 January 2022 at a cost of £47, 727.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any training in place for teachers and school staff to assist them with managing pupils with autism that will only eat certain foods at any given time; and (ii) any accommodations in place to allow pupils with autism to obtain that food.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) Autism Advisory & Intervention Service (AAIS) does not currently offer training specifically for teachers or school staff (including EA catering staff) on “managing pupils with autism that will only eat certain foods at any given time”.  However, Tier 2 AAIS training includes content on sensory sensitivities and highlights the importance of understanding individual sensory profiles. It also provides guidance on adapting the classroom and wider school environment to better support sensory needs.  The EA will work closely with the school and the child’s parents to understand the child’s individual dietary requirements and will agree an approach to provide, as far as reasonably possible, a nutritionally balanced meal that meets the special dietary requirements of the child effectively and safely, alongside other practical accommodations. Based upon medical verification from a paediatric doctor, speech and language therapist or similar qualified medical professional, the EA can accommodate special dietary requests for pupils with autism.  As part of the special diet application process, parents can record foods that are to be avoided, potential substitute foods and can also detail any changes required to food texture.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to confirm that the Gambling Code of Practice, when published, will stipulate that no person should be permitted to withdraw cash from a financial institution, by ATM or any other method, within a premises for which bingo and gaming for prizes is authorised by a bingo club licence under The Betting, Gaming, Lotteries and Amusements (Northern Ireland) Order 1985.
answer_text: A 12-week consultation on a proposed initial gambling Code of Practice was launched on 1 October 2024 in accordance with the requirements of The Betting, Gaming, Lotteries and Amusements (Northern Ireland) Order 1985.  This consultation closed on 24 December 2024.  Responses to the consultation are currently being analysed and therefore the content of the Gambling Code of Practice has yet to be confirmed.
