To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people that have access to the Dexcom G6 sensor, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, there is currently provision of some Continuous Glucose Monitors in line with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidance. Continuous glucose monitors currently listed on the NI Drug Tariff can be provided, with the most appropriate system determined in consultation between the clinician and the patient and/or the patient’s carer.  Dexcom G6 senor is a type of complex Continuous Glucose monitoring device that is not currently listed on the NI Drug tariff and therefore is not commissioned in Northern Ireland. Some patients have been self-funding these devices as they enable closed loop technology.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people that (i) qualify to lease a car under the Motability Scheme in Northern Ireland; and (ii) have availed of this benefit.
answer_text: A person can join the Motability Scheme if they are in receipt of the enhanced rate of the mobility component of Personal Independence Payment or the higher rate of the mobility component of Disability Living Allowance and have at least 12 months of their award remaining. At the end of November 2024 (latest published statistics), there were 143,990 people who met these criteria.	My Department does not hold information in relation to the number of people availing of the Motability scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the role that (a) his Department; (b) the Chief Medical Officer; and (b) the Minister himself, has in relation to the Business Services Organisation and the Procurement and Logistics Service purchasing medical equipment; and (ii) the approvals that need to be sought before purchasing the medical equipment.
answer_text: The Business Services Organisation (BSO) is a Health and Social Care (HSC) Body (akin to an executive Non-Departmental Public Body). BSO is accountable to me as Health Minister for all funds allocated to it for the purposes of carrying out its core functions. The BSO Directorate of Operations includes Procurement & Logistic Services (PaLS). Neither I nor my Department, including the office of the Chief Medical Officer, have a role in the operations within BSO and PaLS.Contracts for the procurement of goods and services by HSC Organisations are co-ordinated centrally by BSO PaLS on behalf of all HSC organisations. The procurement and selection of medical equipment is based on a clinical specification developed by the HSC. Medical Devices are approved for the market by the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) and, if they meet the specification, may be procured by BSO PaLS and HSC organisations following normal procurement procedures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any role (i) the Chief Medical Officer; and (ii) the Minister himself had in the triage system within the (a) Business Services Organisation; and (b) Procurement and Logistics Service in relation to referrals for supplying medical equipment and personal protective equipment in 2020.
answer_text: The Business Services Organisation (BSO) is a Health and Social Care Body (akin to an executive Non-Departmental Public Body). BSO is accountable to me as Health Minister for all funds allocated to it for the purposes of carrying out its core functions. The BSO Directorate of Operations includes Procurement & Logistic Services (PaLS). Neither I nor my Department, including the office of the Chief Medical Officer, had a role in the operations within BSO and PaLS in 2020.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) role; and (ii) authority that the Chief Medical Officer has over the (a) Business Services Organisation; and (b) Procurement and Logistics Service.
answer_text: The Business Services Organisation (BSO) is a Health and Social Care Body (akin to an executive Non-Departmental Public Body). BSO is accountable to me as Health Minister for all funds allocated to it for the purposes of carrying out its core functions. The BSO Directorate of Operations includes Procurement & Logistic Services (PaLs). The Department including the office of the Chief Medical Officer has no role in the operations undertaken by BSO PaLs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department can take to continue to ensure adequate access for vulnerable children to social care workers, without having to rely on agency staff.
answer_text: The HSC Social Work workforce has been under considerable strain for a number of years and some of the Trust social work teams had become overly reliant on recruitment agencies to maintain frontline children’s services.The Department, in collaboration with the HSC Trusts, took the decision to end the use of recruitment agencies to employ social workers in December 2022. That decision was fully implemented in June 2023 when the Trusts ceased employing social workers via recruitment agencies. There has been no further recruitment agency usage for HSC social workers since September 2023.At the same time, there has been a huge effort to attract as many newly qualified social workers as possible into Trust vacancies. I am pleased to report that 209 newly qualified social workers were recruited in 2023 and a further 238 added in 2024. Another mass recruitment exercise is currently underway and it is anticipated that c.240 newly qualified social workers will be recruited to Trust vacancies in 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, given the UK Government are making a stipulation in the Children’s Wellbeing and Schools Bill that a school uniform PE kit cannot contain more than three branded items, whether this is something he will consider including in the School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Bill.
answer_text: The School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Bill must make a difference for parents in terms of affordability of school uniform requirements.That is why the Bill I Introduced to the Assembly provides the enabling powers for a cap to be set – this can be on daywear or PE kits.  Clause 3 enables a cap on numbers of branded items; clause 4, a cap on the upper limit – a financial amount - for an item or a set of items, or for the total uniform requirements.  Clauses 3 and 4 are subject to commencement orders.  This means that further work on the detail of how a cap might operate can be taken forward in parallel with – but without delaying – the implementation of statutory guidelines.  I want to take care that no unintended consequences materialise that cannot be readily addressed.  The important point in relation to the School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Bill is that the necessary powers are in place via clauses 3 and 4.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether Orange Halls pay for water.
answer_text: I can confirm that Orange Halls are charged for water. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com. Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department can take to increase the provision of play therapy access for children, given that a recent report from Play Therapy UK suggests these classes showed an improvement in the child’s mental health improve on average by 76 per cent.
answer_text: Play therapy is not one of the professions recognised as allied health professions in Northern Ireland, however, as part of a focused engagement initiative to inform the work and direction of the Special Schools Partnership Pilot, play therapists, along with music and art therapists have been involved in a recent Public Health Agency partnership pilot programme within 7 special schools. The work of the partnership groups secured this therapeutic support, to engage with Children and Young People with the most profound and complex learning needs and disabilities to capture their views on these elements of their lives. This work has delivered greater insights and an enhanced understanding of the Children and Young People within the schools.Alongside capturing the voice of the children and young people, the therapeutic approaches taken have also supported the emotional health and wellbeing of the Children and Young People involved, improved their engagement in the school environment and provided their education team additional tools and skills to continue to support them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans he has to introduce similar legislation to the Children’s Wellbeing and Schools Bill currently going through Westminster.
answer_text: The Department of Education has not undertaken research into the decline of the birth rate but is aware of the work the Department of Finance undertake on population estimates and projections.  The Department of Finance have advised that official statistics on birth registration numbers and fertility rates in Northern Ireland are produced by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA), using registration data collected by the General Register Office (GRO). NISRA has not conducted research into reasons for the reduction in fertility as it involves a wide range of factors which are not captured as part of the birth registration process. NISRA use birth registration data to produce mid-year population estimates and population projections.  Officials in the Department of Education’s Statistics and Research Team use NISRA’s population projections to produce school population projections. The Education Authority also uses NISRA’s birth rates and population projections, along with the Department’s school population projections to inform area planning, predominantly in the primary phase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the data on the tangible effects and benefits, that is actual benefits other than the Government saying it is complying with EU Directives or stating the measures put in place will protect the environment, that the implementation of the Water Abstraction and Impoundment (Licensing) (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2007 has achieved on (i) water quality; (ii) an increase or decrease in protected mammals; (iii) an increase or decrease in fish and invertebrate populations; and (iv) an increase or decrease in bio
answer_text: The Water Abstraction and Impoundment (Licensing) (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2007 introduced an amendment to require the Department to inform every water undertaker of receipt of an application within 14 days under regulation 8 of the Water Abstraction and Impoundment (Licensing) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2006.In Northern Ireland, the only Water Undertaker is Northern Ireland Water Ltd. The amendment you have asked about, which requires the Department to notify NI Water of receipt of an abstraction licence application, has no impact on water quality, protected mammals, fish and invertebrate populations nor, biodiversity including the spread of alien species.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action his Department can take to ensure children have access to expert mental health support, such as play therapy classes.
answer_text: My officials continue to work alongside colleagues from the Department of Health with the continued combined investment of over £5m per annum to support programmes under the joint Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework.It is important to note that while our schools are encouraged to provide a supportive and nurturing environment, the provision of mental health and therapeutic services for children is ultimately within the remit of the health service and should be delivered by appropriately qualified and registered health professionals (i.e. counsellors and therapists).Schools do have flexibility to use their individual budgets to best meet the identified needs of their pupils and in some circumstances, they may employ the services of a counsellor or therapist from existing funds.  Any immediate and appropriately assessed need for counselling or therapy remains available through health services, including CAMHS and associated pathways and support services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action his Department can take to increase the provision of play therapy access for children, given that a recent report from Play Therapy UK suggests these classes showed an improvement in the child’s mental health improve on average by 76 per cent.
answer_text: My officials continue to work alongside colleagues from the Department of Health with the continued combined investment of over £5m per annum to support programmes under the joint Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework.It is important to note that while our schools are encouraged to provide a supportive and nurturing environment, the provision of mental health and therapeutic services for children is ultimately within the remit of the health service and should be delivered by appropriately qualified and registered health professionals (i.e. counsellors and therapists).Schools do have flexibility to use their individual budgets to best meet the identified needs of their pupils and in some circumstances, they may employ the services of a counsellor or therapist from existing funds.  Any immediate and appropriately assessed need for counselling or therapy remains available through health services, including CAMHS and associated pathways and support services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to putting in place Direct Payments for domiciliary care, to detail (i) what processes people must complete regarding (a) Access NI; (b) insurance; (c) redundancy payments; (d) holiday payments; and (e) pay in lieu; (ii) the advice people receive on this; and (iii) where people receive this advice.
answer_text: (i) (a) All Personal Assistants (PA) must have an enhanced Access NI check carried out before they can commence employment via a Direct Payment (DP). This is mandatory in all programmes of care and includes family members or those who have previously had an Access NI check completed for other roles.  Access NI’s should be applied for each time a PA is caring for a different person.  The Trust will cover the cost of the Access NI checks for each PA.  The PA must sign a consent form before starting the process on line and enter a 6-digit number provided by the key worker. The PA must provide a copy of Identification documents, (ID), including photographic ID to the keyworker (in the Trust), who takes a copy and forwards it to the Counter Signatory who will sign off on behalf of the Trust. The PA receives a disclosure certificate which they must share with their employer and keyworker on behalf of the Trust. If there are no issues the PA can commence employment but if there are issues noted the PA must give an explanation and a senior manager with the Trust must agree that the PA can commence employment via the DP. All ID documentation must be destroyed within 90 days in line with GDPR(i) (b) All DPs where there are PAs employed, must have Employers Liability Insurance in place on commencement of service (DP cannot commence prior to the date the insurance is taken out). This includes DPs where self-employed PAs are engaged.   Employer’s Liability Insurance must include £10million employer’s liability, £5million public liability and redundancy cover. The insurance must be renewed annually and a copy of the certificate and policy schedule forwarded to the keyworker to be held in the service user records. This is set out in the DP Scheme Agreement which must be signed by the person managing the DP and the keyworker.(i)(c) The person managing the DP must follow employment law as they are the employer. When the DP is ceased and the PA is no longer required they may be entitled to Redundancy payment, depending on the length of time they have been employed via this DP.  The Trust advises the person managing the DP to liaise with their payroll service if they use one, seek advice from the Centre for Independent Living NI (CILNI) and/or their insurance company or the Labour Relations Agency. The person managing the DP should also liaise with their keyworker to inform them of the date ceased.  Finance will liaise with the person managing the DP in regards to any monies that have to be recouped by the Trust or if a further payment is required to ensure all responsibilities are met.(i)(d) The PA may be entitled to holiday payments and the Trust advises the person managing the DP to liaise with their payroll service, if they use one, seek advice from the Centre for Independent Living NI, Insurance company or Labour Relations Agency.(i)(e) When the DP ceases and the PA is no longer required, they may be entitled to Pay in Lieu of service. The Trust advises the pe

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will publish (i) the findings from the Regional Obesity Management Service consultation; and (ii) policies and services derived from the consultation, including the establishment of a specialist weight management service.
answer_text: The findings from the Regional Obesity Management Service (ROMS) consultation have been published and can be found on the Department’s website.I have approved a phased approach to establishing a ROMS and work is being taken forward by my Department to develop a service specification which will set out access criteria. In its first phase, which is expected to commence early 2026, the focus will be on the provision of medical injections for weight loss, with associated lifestyle support to ensure maximum effectiveness. As work progresses, there may be further phases to this service development to include other interventions, such as bariatric (weight loss) surgery, subject to funding.

To ask the Minister of Finance to confirm that the Programme for Government 2024-2027 commitment of up to £135 million a year to reduce waiting lists is separate from the £61 million for the Department of Health Primary Care Multi-disciplinary Teams through the transformation board.
answer_text: As I outlined in my letter of offer to departments, funding provided through the Transformation Fund is ring-fenced specifically for successful transformation projects and is distinct from other commitments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 22162/22-27, to detail what proportion of the £3.4 million Capital and £1.2 million Resource already spent on the RAPID IT system is now redundant or been wasted on the system.
answer_text: I refer the member to response provided to AQW 23884/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 22162/22-27, to detail how many of the business case benefits will now not be realised with the agreed pause on the RAPID IT system.
answer_text: I refer the member to response provided to AQW 23884/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 22162/22-27, to detail (i) what is meant by an agreed pause on the RAPID IT system project; (ii) what this means for the three-stage phased approach; and (iii) what proportion of the measurable benefits that RAPID would have delivered in Phase 1, Phase 2 and Phase 3 are expected to be delivered by the revised approach in (a) December 2026; (b) April 2027; and (c) February 2028.
answer_text: The purpose of Revenue and Benefit Partner Project was to enable service improvement through modernised IT capability. The current IT system known as ABBACUS, provides the capability to issue over 900,000 rate bills that facilitates the collection of c£1.6bn annually on behalf of the Executive and local government. Given its importance, it is imperative that changes in the development of new technology to be used in the collection of rates does not compromise existing rate collection services. Land & Property Services (LPS) and its Supplier have entered a mutually agreed pause on project work to consider options that best deliver service improvements and achieve anticipated benefits whilst mitigating risk on the continuity of service delivery. As these discussions are ongoing and have not yet completed, it is not possible to provide an update on project phasing, investment already made or changes to benefits the project is expected to achieve.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 22162/22-27, when he expects the revised approach to deliver 100 per cent of the benefits that the RAPID IT system was expected to deliver.
answer_text: I refer the member to response provided to AQW 23884/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 22162/22-27, how he will ensure that the revised approach supports the aims of rates recovery to the same degree as RAPID was expected to do.
answer_text: I refer the member to response provided to AQW 23884/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer (i) AQW 18265/22-27; (ii) AQW 18266/22-27; (iii) AQW 18267/22-27; (iv) AQW 18268/22-27; (v) AQW 18269/22-27; (vi) AQW 18352/22-27; and (vii) AQW 19929/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 18915/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 20167/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 20732/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any impact assessments that were conducted on the decision to cut funding to the Samaritans from the Core Grant Funding Scheme 2025-26, given the mental health crisis.
answer_text: The delivery of the Core Grant Funding scheme from its inception has been based on 12 month agreements with the Community and Voluntary organisations involved, with the caveat that funding was subject to the Department’s final budget allocation.  There was therefore never any guarantee of funding from one year to the next.The Scheme is intended to contribute to the central administration costs (such as salaries; rent and office administration) of organisations.  Core grant is not intended to directly fund the provision of health and social care services.  I do acknowledge though, that any reduction in total organisational income may have an indirect impact upon an organisation’s ability to deliver all of the things that it had intended to deliver in the year.The Core Grant Funding Scheme for 2025-26 was co-designed in 2024 with Community and Voluntary Sector representatives, and included the following scoring criteria:1)	Evidence of how an organisation supports the department’s strategic objectives;2)	Evidence that an organisation is involved in carrying out relevant activities;3)	Evidence that an organisation will deliver clear outcomes, including how those will be measured and the targets that will be met;4)	Evidence that an organisation has delivered similar outcomes previously; 5)	Evidence that an organisation has suitable monitoring and control systems in place, and suitably qualified and experienced staff; and6)	Evidence that an organisation will work in partnership across the statutory, community and voluntary sectors. These scoring criteria and the application process are listed and explained in the guidance for applicants, available on the departmental website.I am committed to increasing the Core Grant budget, to allow my Department to help more of the important Voluntary and Community Sector organisations that need our support. £1.8m falls considerably short of the £13m total value of the bids submitted through the Scheme. However, my ability to do that will be dependent upon increases to the wider Health budget in the coming years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 20809/22-27, how many domestic and commercial rate payers have committed to a payment plan to reduce debt.
To ask the Minister of Finance to provide clarity in relation to the funding his Department will receive from HM Treasury to cover the increase in employers' National Insurance contributions.
answer_text: ANSWERTreasury has confirmed that the devolved governments will receive Barnett consequentials on additional funding provided to Whitehall department for the increase in employers National Insurance Contributions.Internal assumptions following discussions with Treasury are that the Executive will receive Barnett consequentials in the region of £140/£145 million.The actual quantum will not be confirmed until Westminster Main Estimates, which usually conclude in June.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any support he can provide to ensure children with disabilities, who require transport support travelling to and from medical appointments, will not face any disruption to their services given the challenge these transport providers will face in light of the rise in employers' National Insurance contributions.
answer_text: All HSC Trusts have confirmed that when children with disabilities are in the care of their respective Trust, transport will be provided. This will be different when the child is not under the care of the Trust, in this case the majority of children requiring transport to medical appointments are facilitated by their parents or carers.There are not any known concerns from a Trust perspective with regards to challenges due to National Insurance contributions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether his Department has considered any potential support payments for those in the public sector given the increase in employers' National Insurance contributions.
answer_text: ANSWERI refer the member to AQW 23515

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any support he can provide to special educational needs and disability transport operator groups, given the impact the increase in employers' National Insurance contributions will have upon these vital services.
answer_text: School transport is one of many vital services provided by private sector employers on behalf of the public sector and I am concerned about the impact the recent Budget may have on the cost to employers of providing those services. The impact on employers of the increase in National Insurance Contributions (NIC) will vary depending on the size of the business, how many it employs and how it manages the new arrangements. I understand that a number of the private bus and taxi operators, who hold contracts with the Education Authority (EA) to provide school transport for pupils with special educational needs or disability, are smaller businesses whose NIC liability may not increase or are self-employed and therefore unaffected. The EA does not, however, hold information that would allow it to identify the private bus and taxi operators who will be impacted or to make a detailed assessment of the extent to which this may increase their operating costs. The EA works closely with its transport operators and has advised that to date none have raised concerns about the rise in operating costs due to the increase in NICs, however it will engage with any who are impacted and for whom this presents difficulties.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 20637/22-27, to detail (i) how the thousands of patients have guided his Department; and (ii) where the responses and comments are recorded.
answer_text: The Duty of Candour Workstream and Being Open Sub-Group commissioned a public opinion survey to gain insight into service user and patient experience of openness, honesty and involvement when interacting with the Health and Social Care System.The survey, which ran for four days from 22-25 January 2021 attracting 2,295 valid responses, formed an important part of the pre-consultation involvement plan, and provided important quantitative and qualitative evidence for consideration when finalising the policy proposals post-consultation.  The full results of the NIVIEW - Openness Health Survey are available on my department’s website. My Department initiated a 20-week public consultation on 12 April 2021, on draft policy proposals for the statutory Duty of Candour and a Being Open Framework.  The consultation closed on 31 August 2021. The consultation yielded 334 individual and 93 organisational responses.  The submissions were collated and analysed, engaging the Duty of Candour Workstream to help interpret and assess what the public had reported.  Both individual (redacted) and organisational responses are published on my department’s website.The Department also hosted eight virtual consultation events during the consultation period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any further action his Department can take to support children whose needs are too high for mental health support teams to provide the sufficient support, but do not meet the threshold for a Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service referral.
answer_text: Access to Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) are based upon regionally agreed threshold criteria. Working Together: A Pathway for Children and Young People through CAMHS sets out a 5 Stepped Care Model. This model ensures children and young people get access to the right service at the right time. CAMHS services start at Step 2 of the model and for children that don’t meet threshold for CAMHS every child and young person gets access to Step 1 - Universal Services. Support at this level usually focuses on targeted prevention for potentially vulnerable children and their families/carers and involves the adoption of a range of services designed to create the best developmental and emotional start for all children. This includes wider children and young people’s services and other community based services geared towards promoting all aspects of health and wellbeing. To support universal provision there are currently 29 Family Support Hubs in operation covering all of Northern Ireland. A Family Support Hub is a multi-agency network of statutory, community and voluntary organisations that provide early intervention services, or work with families who need support. The network accepts referrals and uses their knowledge of local service providers to signpost families with specific needs to an appropriate serviceMy Department developed the Northern Ireland Family Support website. The purpose of this website is to create a comprehensive database of family support and registered childcare services across Northern Ireland.My Department also supports the Youth Wellness Web which has a range of resources and help on wellbeing that help children, young people, parents and teachers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions his Department can take to ensure early intervention for children and young people who are developing mental health problems, given there is currently no statutory measures in place to guarantee this intervention.
answer_text: Early intervention and prevention is one of the four priorities areas in the Mental Health Strategy Delivery Plan for 2024/25.  The Public Health Agency is leading on this priority through implementation of the Early Intervention & Action Plan which focuses primarily on Actions 1 & 2 in the Strategy. Under the Early intervention and Action Plan, the Public Health Agency commissions a number of programmes which deliver early intervention supports for children and families including: Early Intervention Support Service, which comprises a Family Support Team in each of the Trust localities aimed at providing a 12—14 week programme of support for families with emerging vulnerabilities to address problems early and reduce risk of crisis escalation. The Service supports 650 families annually.Regional Mental Health Awareness Training Programmes, are delivered directly to young people both in a school and a youth environment on a regional basis, programmes include: Mindset, which is delivered by Action Mental Health is adolescent training for 14-17 yrs.  This programme provides sources of support, resources available, and strategies to look after individual’s mental health and provide a more positive understanding of how to talk about mental health and how to support self and others during difficult times and AWARE delivers ‘Mood Matters’ for Young People.  There is additional Mental health awareness training delivered directly to young people on a locality basis including the following programmes:Gimme 5 Student ResilienceB Positive Mindfulness in Schools Provoking Thought  Healthy MeRoots of Empathy, an emotional health and wellbeing programme which has reached over 20,000 primary school in Northern Ireland since 2011.Targeted Prevention Services for Young People, which develops and delivers age-appropriate drug & alcohol life skills/harm reduction programmes for young people in the age ranges of 11-13 years, 14-15 years and 16+ years across Northern Ireland.  These programmes are delivered to young people identified as being at risk of substance misuse and are not delivered to the universal population of young people.Youth Engagement Service, delivered to children and young people aged 11-25 throughout each of the five Health and Social Care Trust areas, to provide universal social and recreational services to children and young people aged 11-25 years and to provide early and appropriate information on the issues that are causing them concern.The Department also work very closely with the Department of Education, jointly funding the Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Schools Framework which was launched in 2021. The Framework is a key strand to our Early Intervention and Prevention work, and provides a range of interventions in primary and secondary schools, including:Emotional Wellbeing Teams in Schools project, the objective of which is to ensure post-primary schools are aware of appropriate pathways for support. The Resilience in Educati

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any further action that can be taken to ensure staff in schools are properly equipped and trained to (i) spot children and young adults who are struggling with their mental health; and (ii) appropriately deal with this.
answer_text: It is important to note that our educational staff are not mental health professionals, however it is essential that settings provide caring, supportive environments for children and young people ensuring that where issues arise these are identified and acted upon at the earliest opportunity.The joint Department of Education and Department of Health ‘Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework’ provides overarching guidance to empower those working in educational settings in the promotion of positive emotional wellbeing and to strengthen self-esteem and resilience in our children and young people. This is underpinned by an investment of over £5m which supports several emotional health and wellbeing projects and services directly to schools and youth services. Details of the Framework and the projects it supports can be found at https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/articles/emotional-health-and-wellbeing. My department, in conjunction with the Education and Training Inspectorate, also recently issued the ‘Emotional Health and Wellbeing - Effective Practice in Educational Settings’ guide to schools, which can be found at https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/publications/effective-practice-educational-settings. This provides case studies which demonstrate effective practice across a range of settings in Northern Ireland as well as links and signposting to mental health information and services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any further action that can be taken to deal with mental health waiting times, given that the most recent data shows that by October 2024 there were 170 people waiting over nine weeks to access Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Northern Trust, similar to others, is reporting recruitment and retention issues along with more complex presentations and both of these factors are having an impact on capacity and increased waiting times. As of 31st October 2024, the Northern Trust had a 16% vacancy rate across its Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service (CAMHS).Action 32 of The Mental Health Strategy (MHS) recognises the staffing challenges across CAMHS and a comprehensive workforce review was completed in March 2022. This review considered; existing workforce, the need to adopt new models of working and the importance of increasing the focus on training, recruitment and retention issues. My Department is committed to creating a workforce that will meet the needs of the population and which is fit for the future.  The Mental Health Strategy acknowledged that CAMHS requires additional investment and Action 10 of MHS gives a commitment to increase funding to 10% of the Adult Mental Health Budget. My Department will continue to work closely and collaboratively with Health and Social Care Trusts and other key stakeholders to develop services that meet not only current but emerging needs of this population and work with Executive colleagues to secure the necessary resources required to meet demands.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that no additional capacity has been secured for percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgeries across UK hospitals and the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust's acknowledgement of the independent sectors' inability to provide aftercare, to detail (i) how he plans to secure more capacity for those surgeries and provide the sufficient follow up care; and (ii) what his Department is doing to ensure the appropriate capacity and properly equipped teams are in place to carry out these surgeries.
answer_text: My officials have been working closely and continuously with Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) over the past number of months to identity sustainable solutions to this complex issue.  This work has included: -Implementing additional Waiting List Initiatives (WLIs); Exploring additional capacity with providers in Great Britain; Seeking capacity within other Trusts across Northern Ireland; and Engaging with the Independent Sector.Additional capacity has been secured with Evelina London Children’s Hospital; however, this has not been required.  To date, seven procedures have been carried out at the Ulster Hospital.  The Belfast Trust has liaised directly with the parents of the children treated as part of these initiatives.The Belfast Health and Social Trust continues to provide additional WLI capacity, to carry out additional procedures, subject to bed and theatre availability.As of the 3rd October 2025, the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children (RBHSC) has advised that 12 children are “actively” waiting for Percutaneous Gastrostomy Surgery.  Of these:6 children are scheduled for surgery in October, including a child classified as urgent;2 children are dated for November;4 children are currently undated, having been added to the waiting list in late September.In addition, 13 children are currently suspended from the waiting list, including one child who is awaiting a Multi-Disciplinary Team (MDT) assessment.My officials will continue to work closely with BHSCT to consider and agree sustainable, safe and clinically appropriate solutions to this issue.It is important to note that PEG tube insertion is one critical area of surgery among a wide range of complex and high-priority surgical needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) what other UK hospitals were approached in relation to securing additional capacity for percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery; and (ii) the responses received.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) contacted twenty hospitals across GB to ascertain if they had capacity to carry out these procedures.  Only one hospital outlined that they may have capacity to carry out a number of the procedures on patients.  A limited number of patients were clinically appropriate to transfer to GB for the procedure and BHSCT contacted the relevant families directly.  Two of the families contacted refused to go to London.  However, due to the additional waiting list sessions provided in Belfast and the Ulster Hospital, and the subsequent reduction in the waiting list, travel to London was no longer necessary and all remaining procedures have been carried out in Northern Ireland. BHSCT also contacted colleagues in the Health Service Executive (HSE) in Republic of Ireland (ROI) but they were unfortunately unable to provide any capacity for Northern Ireland patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to explain the reduction in relation to the number of children awaiting percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery, which went from 92 children in April 2024 to 55 children in November 2024, given that a response from his Department acknowledges 66 children awaiting the surgery in December 2024 without clarifying how these changes occurred.
answer_text: The difference between the numbers in November and December can be explained below.The waiting list is a “active” list with children being added to it and removed from it daily as children receive their treatment or as children requiring surgery are added to the list. At any time, a consultant is also likely to have a number of patients who are unsuitable for admission for surgery due to clinical or personal reasons.These patients are suspended from the active waiting list until they are ready for admission.  During this time of suspension these patients are not included in the statistic count of patients “actively waiting” but will be added back onto the list following their period of suspension, if required. The increase between November and December was the result of an error, when twenty children were transferred to South Eastern Trust list for assessment of surgery and were mistakenly removed from the Belfast Trust list.  This error was discovered and addressed.  The waiting list is managed to patient level detail by my officials to ensure that this error does not occur again.  The December figures were as a result of the active and suspended list as outlined above.However, as of the 14th November 2025, the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children (RBHSC) has advised that only 7 children are “actively” waiting for Percutaneous Gastrostomy Surgery.  Of these:2 children are scheduled for surgery end of November;3 children are dated for surgery in December;2 children are currently undated, aiming for surgery in January 2026.In addition, 10 children are currently suspended from the waiting list, 9 medically and 1 socially suspended including two children who are awaiting a Multi-Disciplinary Team (MDT) assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to children awaiting percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery, why families are not being offered access to (i) the Extra Contractual Referral Scheme; and (ii) the S2 Scheme.

To ask the Minister of Health whether he will commit to ensuring families of children requiring percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery are kept up to date with regular updates with the progress on this issue.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of hospital admissions for Influenza from (i) week 40 of 2018 to week 01 of 2019; and (ii) week 40 of 2019 to week 01 of 2020.

To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the delivery project for the RAPID IT system, including (i) the number of staff that have worked on it since the commencement; (ii) the amount of money spent on it; and (iii) an outline of the benefits of the system.
answer_text: The delivery project for the Rapid IT system commenced on 2nd August 2021. The project planned to deliver a new IT Rate Collection System on a three-stage phased approach: Phase 1: December 2026Phase 2: April 2027Phase 3: February 2028LPS and the Supplier have recently entered a mutually agreed pause on project delivery and are currently negotiating terms on a revised approach to project delivery that could realise key benefits from the project earlier than December 2026. the number of staff that have worked on it since the commencement. 13.25 FTE staff have been working directly on the project. the amount of money spent on it. £3.4m Capital and £1.2m Resource has been spent to date and is in line with the approved Project business case. an outline of the benefits of the system.A range of measurable benefits have been set out in the Project business case in following areas; Improve the quality of Customer Service Improve service delivery to and from internal/ external stakeholdersReduce costs of service delivery Increase the net collectable rate Improve LPS Revenue & Benefits staff experience Improve the quality of Revenue & Benefits data

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail all occasions where water turbines have been found to be responsible for killing fish in the last ten years, including (i) location; (ii) date; (iii) number of fish involved; and (iv) action taken by his Department.
answer_text: My department does not hold information on fish mortalities at turbines in Northern Ireland over the past 10 years. I fully understand the potential impacts of water abstraction on fish stocks and I am committed to implementing effective conservation measures to protect them.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will publish all documentation reflecting the previous Minister of Health's decision on 23 August 2022 to request that the Public Health Agency and the Department's Information Analysis Directorate investigate excess deaths.
answer_text: On 23 August 2022, the former Health Minister requested a further assessment of excess deaths in Northern Ireland, beyond that conducted by Northern Ireland Statistics Research Agency and, if possible, for a UK comparison to be made.  This request was made via e-mail and was received by the Permanent Secretary, Chief Medical Officer, Chief Scientific Advisor and the Director of Communications within my Department.Following this request, a discussion between senior officials in my Department took place and the Information Analysis Directorate and the Public Health Agency were tasked with taking forward the further investigation of excess deaths using the Office for Health Improvement and Disparities methodology in the first instance.My Department will not be publishing any of the available internal correspondence in relation to the setting up of the this investigation. However, the report containing the findings from the investigation will be published when it is available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on how her Department plans to improve (i) drinking water quality; and (ii) water quality in rivers and loughs.
answer_text: The management of public water infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:(i)	Drinking water quality is assessed against the Water Supply (Water Quality) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017. Drinking water supplies are tested 365 days per year, with samples taken from raw water before treatment, interstage samples and final water produced at water treatment works, throughout the distribution network and at customer taps. Overall compliance with the regulations is consistently above 99.8%, with a compliance outturn of 99.88% for the calendar year, 2024.Drinking water supplies provided by NI Water are also independently assessed on a continual basis by the Drinking Water Inspectorate (DWI). The 2023 DWI report on drinking water quality is available at Drinking Water Quality in Northern Ireland, 2023NI Water uses the World Health Organisation Drinking Water Safety Plan approach to the provision of clean, safe drinking water. This approach assesses all risks in the raw (untreated) water, and water treatment facilities are designed, built and operated to ensure that those risks are dealt with appropriately. (ii)	NI Water works with environmental Non-Government Organisations (eNGO), such as the Mourne Heritage Trust, National Trust and RSPB, as well as other Government Departments, to improve drinking water catchment areas across Northern Ireland and improve the quality of water entering rivers and loughs.  For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any action his Department is considering in relation to recommencing stocking from Movanagher Fish Farm into public angling estate waters.
answer_text: No decisions will be taken regarding stocking of the Public Angling Estate from Movanagher Fish Farm until a stocking protocol is in place to ensure compliance with the Wildlife Order (NI) 1985 and minimise the risk of zebra mussel spread.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail why there is no up to date information on the website or in the public domain on progress made on (i) the zebra mussels detected in public angling waters; and (ii) the restocking of fish.
answer_text: The information on the DAERA website reflects the current situation with regards to Zebra Mussels and the impact on the Public Angling Estate (PAE). In summary the advice is that the presence of Zebra Mussels is still preventing the stocking of fish to the PAE from Movanagher Fish Farm. No decision has been taken regarding whether PAE waters will be stocked in 2025. The Department is currently working with the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute to develop a stocking protocol that complies with the Wildlife Order (NI) 1985.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the zebra mussels found in the water used to stock the public angling estate.
answer_text: My officials continue to work with the Agri Food and Biosciences Institute to develop a protocol to allow future stocking, which is compliant with the Wildlife Order (NI) 1985 and minimises the risk of the spread of zebra mussels.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given that since November 2023 and June 2024 there have been 72,601 fish culled as a result of overcrowding due to Department measures, to detail any action his Department can take to reassure fish farm managers and owners, like Movanagher Fish Farm, that restocking in public angling waters will be able to recommence as soon as possible.
answer_text: Despite the suspension of stocking, the department’s Movanagher Fish Farm remains open with staff taking care of fish stocks until a stocking protocol compliant with the Wildlife Order (NI) 1985 is established. As fish cannot be moved off the farm, the culls undertaken to date were the minimum necessary to create space for the remaining fish to prevent over-crowding, cannibalism and the outbreak of diseases. The department continues to work with the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute to develop a stocking protocol.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans to address Pseudomonas bacteria in the new regional Maternity Hospital.
answer_text: I can advise that the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust continues to work with their consultant design team, their water specialist and DoH Health Estates on the delivery of the remediation works for the new Maternity Hospital.The remediation works proposed involves the installation of a new domestic water system to the neo-natal unit, with localised remediation through the remainder of the domestic water systems in the building.The Trust will continue regular testing to monitor rates of pseudomonas and other water born pathogens in the building and any further action will be based upon these results. The Trust has advised that they may need to review the wider system replacement if assurance on safety and robustness is not achieved by completion of the remediation works. The Trust is exploring early occupation of areas where remediation could be completed ahead of installation of the new Neonatal water system. Any early occupation will depend on delivery of the remediation programme, Trust assurance regarding water safety, and clinical risk assessments, servicemodel analysis and staffing sustainability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how the Housing Supply Strategy 2024-2039, which sets a target of 100,000 homes delivered by 2037, will be funded and delivered.
answer_text: The delivery of ‘at least 100,000 homes’ across all tenures set out in the Strategy, is intended as a long-term ambition over a 15-year period (2024 – 2039) to meet our housing needs and demands.It should be noted that the majority of these homes will be delivered through private market development and therefore funded by the private sector. The ambition to deliver one third of the 100,000 as social homes will be delivered through the Social Housing Development Programme, funded by capital grant which is allocated to my Department.The Strategy provides a framework for the whole system changes needed but will only deliver if Executive Departments prioritise the policy changes and budgetary allocations required.The publication of the Housing Supply Strategy by the Executive, alongside the inclusion of Housing as a priority in the draft Programme for Government, demonstrates the Executive’s commitment to meeting this ambition, and with that, to ensure that funding is allocated to actions identified to deliver the supply we need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how he will address economic inactivity given that, according to the January 2025 Review of the first year of the restored Northern Ireland Executive Pivotal report, over a quarter of the working age population in Northern Ireland are economically inactive.
answer_text: While the Department for Communities (DfC) has lead policy responsibility for employability and reducing economic inactivity, my department, along with others, has an important contribution to make through our skills and education remit. Education, training, and skills development opportunities, at all levels and for all ages, are available at our Higher Education Institutions, Further Education (FE) Colleges, and through a range of other training providers.There is a range of financial support available to FE learners, including the Additional Support Fund for those learners with a disability, as well as support towards childcare costs.  We also run programmes such as Skills for Life and Work, Skill Up, Step Up and All Age Apprenticeships, which aim to make skills and education more accessible to a wide range of the population, including those experiencing additional barriers.My department also works with DfC to support individuals into employment through the Labour Market Partnerships, where officials from my Skills team and Careers Service are represented.I also recognise the relationship between the provision of adequate childcare support and the ability for parents to participate in education and employment. To this end, £740k of the Skills Fund was allocated this year to support pathways into the childcare sector, helping ensure an adequate supply of workers into this critically important sector.	Finally, the Good Jobs consultation sought views on several policy proposals which would help those with ill health, and those caring for dependents, to remain within or access the workforce.		Ultimately, this is a complex and persistent issue which will require a range of approaches and collaboration across government agencies and the private sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how he plans to address challenges facing the wastewater infrastructure and the delay of over 11,000 development application according to the January 2025 Review of the first year of the restored Northern Ireland Executive Pivotal report.
answer_text: I am fully aware of the challenges facing wastewater infrastructure and the impact that this is having on development. Therefore, I will continue to build on the three-pronged approach that is in place. Firstly, I will continue to work with my Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment for NI Water, secondly, I am bringing forward new legislation in relation to sustainable drainage systems (SuDS), which will help to store stormwater so that it releases slower into the wastewater network and thirdly, my officials are examining the legislative change required to bring forward developer contributions.I am also  committed to ensuring that the planning system in the North operates in an efficient and effective way, to facilitate development. My Department has been working collectively with local government and other stakeholders to improve the planning system through the Planning Improvement Programme (PIP).  The PIP has and will deliver a number of changes to planning legislation which will enhance and make a tangible difference to the planning system here in the North. These changes include the introduction of legislation to enable council planning authorities to introduce statutory validation checklists which will improve the quality of planning applications entering the system, and in turn reduce the potential for delay through the application process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any support he can provide in order for house building completions to increase, given the January 2025 Review of the first year of the restored Northern Ireland Executive Pivotal report shows 2023 had the lowest number of housebuilding completions since 1959.
answer_text: The publication of the Housing Supply Strategy and the inclusion of Housing as a priority in the draft Programme for Government underlines a commitment by the Executive to address the issues that are impacting on supply of homes across all tenures. This includes issues such as wastewater infrastructure constraints, planning, land availability and finance. My Department is taking forward implementation of the Strategy, including setting up of cross departmental structures to develop the first short term action plan in collaboration with those Departments.I have been clear that social housing delivery is a priority for me and my Department will continue to spend most of its capital budget on the delivery of new social homes.In the past 3 years, my Department has invested over £510M into the Social Housing Development Programme to start 5177 new social homes. There are around 5,000 social homes currently under construction: 1,400 of these are expected to complete in 2024/25.With the extra budget secured in January Monitoring, this year’s allocation for the Social Housing Development Programme will be around £170M. This will deliver in excess of 1400 new social housing starts

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he intends to address the issues highlighted in the January 2025 Review of the first year of the restored Northern Ireland Executive Pivotal report that states the median wait time to see a consultant is 59.4 weeks, despite his Department's target being no more than one year.
answer_text: Long waiting times are unacceptable and addressing this issue is a key focus for me. The Elective Care Framework (ECF), May 2024, provides the strategic plan for tackling lengthy waiting times, however, delivering on that plan requires levels of investment which the current budget which your party supports does not contain. Reducing waiting times will not be a quick fix. Positive achievements have been made through the development of elective care centres, rapid diagnosis centres and mega clinics. In addition, service reviews have been completed across a number of specialities, subsequently leading to improvements. My Department continues to work with Trusts to increase productivity and efficiency through the use of a wide range of best practice service improvements. I want to ensure that all patients get timely access to sustainable, high-quality assessment and treatment.  Work will continue in these areas to build on the good work taken forward to date and maximise capacity as far as possible.However, I have made it clear that sustainable investment will be fundamental to addressing the issue of lengthy waiting times here.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail how the Wellbeing Dashboard, referenced in the Draft Programme for Government, will be utilised.
answer_text: The PfG Wellbeing Framework has ten strategic domains of wellbeing supported by a selection of indicators, each of which is a high-quality official statistic. We are in the process of linking commitments set out in the PfG to the domains in the Wellbeing Framework, in the longer term, this will help us understand if improvements are being made across society, or if an adjustment in our approach is needed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any action he plans to take to further increase productivity in Northern Ireland, given the findings in the January 2025 Review of the first year of the restored Northern Ireland Executive Pivotal report that Northern Ireland is 13 per cent below the UK average for productivity.
answer_text: Boosting productivity in the North remains a core priority for both me and my Department. Delivering real, tangible improvements in this area is essential for strengthening our economy, driving business growth, and raising living standards for our people.		To that end, we have already taken a number of concrete steps to improve productivity. For example, in November 2024, my Department launched the SME Productivity Booster, a £350,000 initiative designed to support over 70 small to medium sized enterprises (SMEs) across the north. Delivered through further education colleges, this programme is equipping businesses with the skills and insights they need to enhance their productivity, improve their competitiveness, and secure their long term growth prospects.	Through other skills interventions such as Skill Up, Skills Focus, ApprenticeshipsNI, and Higher Level Apprenticeships, my Department is investing over £3m in training directly related to productivity growth including leadership, project management, digital skills, and business improvement techniques.		Additionally, through Invest NI and Innovate NI, my Department has also overseen the launch of the Business Innovation Grant, a £4.3 million scheme designed to support innovation in micro-businesses, sole traders, and SMEs. This initiative provides grants of up to £20,000, removing financial barriers that often prevent smaller businesses from adopting new processes, improving efficiency, and increasing their competitiveness in an evolving marketplace. By making innovation more accessible, the scheme is helping local businesses to modernise and expand.		The need to increase our export capacity and encourage more foreign direct investment into high productivity sectors is also guiding the work of Invest NI in these areas, and InterTradeIreland continues to support more businesses to trade cross-border, which is often the first step for many here in expanding their market reach.		It is important to recognise that many of the factors impacting productivity extend beyond my Department’s direct remit. Issues such as the need for healthcare reform and increased infrastructure investment require a coordinated approach across government. That is why I am committed to working closely with other departments and stakeholders to tackle these wider structural barriers to economic growth.		In addition, I will continue to engage with my Department’s critical friend on productivity, David Jordan. His research and expertise will help shape our approach, ensuring that our interventions deliver meaningful, long term improvements to productivity. Through these combined efforts, we are laying the foundation for a stronger, fairer, and more prosperous economy in the years ahead.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she will consider rate relief for householders who need to meet Historic Environment Division requirements that can restrict the use of efficiencies for the building.
answer_text: ANSWERListed buildings and historic homes, are entitled to a vacant rating exclusion under rating law. This means they will not pay rates where they are out of occupation. This is in recognition of the restrictions you refer to and the fact that there are often significant barriers to bringing such properties back into use.The system of rating is a property tax and is calculated using the capital value of a property. Critically domestic rate support within the system is targeted towards those who need it most. It is important to note that any rate relief means either foregoing revenue or charging other ratepayers more. An exemption or relief is often viewed from the perspective of who it benefits, but there is a real cost, as every pound raised through the rating system stays here as a resource to help pay for hospitals, schools, and other essential regional services.I therefore have no current intention to implement new rates exemptions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what lessons have been learned after Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: Question 10 – AQO 1519/22-27Multi agency co-ordination was stood up before, during and after Storm Éowyn through established Civil Contingencies mechanisms. NIE Networks and partner agencies should be recognised for the tireless work that was undertaken during and after the storm.Storm Éowyn was an unprecedented event, leaving almost one third of people (283,000 homes and businesses) without power. There were faults in over 5,000 locations, with each of these requiring engineers to physically attend and repair. NIE Networks has a systematic process for preparing for major weather events, for managing them, and for reviewing them after the event occurs. My Department will continue to engage with NIE Networks and the Utility Regulator once it has conducted its post review of Storm Éowyn.Given that climate change brings more frequent and more intense weather events, it is all the more important to learn lessons.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will investigate the DFI Roads Portal in light of incorrect geographical locations being received when inputting areas and addresses.
answer_text: The Report-a-Fault system receives over 100,000 customer enquiries relating to road and lighting defects/ issues each year.  The OSNI basemap and location address search function is derived from Spatial NI within the Department of Finance, and has generally worked well.  My department is not aware of any incorrect geographical locations when searching for an address on the system.  My officials would be happy to investigate this further if details of any example errors can be provided, and these will be corrected to improve the portal for all users.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of hospital admissions for influenza from week 40 of 2022 to week 01 of 2023.
answer_text: YearInfluenza AInfluenza BAll influenza2020-20215382021-20223022522022-202313664114072023-2024449284772024-20251802471849Date Periods2020/21: Week 40, 2020 (commencing 28th September 2020) to week 01, 2022 (week ending 10th January 2021); 2021/22: Week 40, 2021 (commencing 4th October 2021) to week 01, 2022 (week ending 9th January 2022);2022/23: Week 40, 2022 (commencing 3rd October 2022) to week 01, 2023 (week ending 8th January 2023);2023/24: Week 40, 2023 (commencing 2nd October 2023) to week 01, 2024 (week ending 7th January 2024); and,2024/25: Week 40, 2024 (commencing 30th September 2024) to week 01, 2025 (week ending 5th January 2025).NotesData are presented using epidemiological weeks (a standardised method of counting weeks [Monday-Sunday] to allow for the comparison of data year after year).Community-acquired influenza emergency admissions to acute hospitals are estimated by combining data from hospital administrative data and laboratory reports.  Admissions are counted where there was a positive test up to seven days before admission or up to one day after admission, and the method of admission was ‘Emergency’.  Admissions count first admission per infection episode.  Where it is currently possible (SHSCT and WHSCT) only admissions where the method of admission was ‘Emergency’ are counted.  It is not currently possible for this report to distinguish emergency from other types of admission for SEHSCT, BHSCT and NHSCT hospital data, following the introduction of a new electronic healthcare record on 06/11/2023, 06/06/2024 and 07/11/2024 respectively.  For this report, all community-acquired admissions for SEHSCT, BHSCT and NHSCT are included, which will include non-emergency admissions (which are a small minority of the total admissions reported).  Work is ongoing to adapt systems to new data sources and re-instate differentiation of emergency admissions. Ongoing developmental and quality assurance work may result in adjustments to figures.The availability and practice of testing for influenza has changed during the time period reported. Please note that the Department of Health’s Information Assets Directorate publishes data on influenza admissions as part of its routine work. https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/topics/hospital-activity-statistics
