question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in relation to the figures in the NISRA Annual Report 2023-24, why the percentage of working days lost due to sickness absence was 5.2 days for not temporary promoted staff, whilst only 1.6 days for temporary promoted staff.
answer_text: According to NISRA, no analysis has been carried out to this level of detail into absence levels of staff on temporary promotion compared to staff not on temporary promotion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in relation to the figures in the NISRA Annual Report 2023-24, why the percentage of working days lost due to sickness absence was 9.3 days for not temporary promoted staff, whilst only 2.9 days for temporary promoted staff.
answer_text: No analysis has been carried out to this level of detail into absence levels of staff on temporary promotion compared to staff not on temporary promotion.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to the figures in the NISRA Annual Report 2023-24, why the percentage of working days lost due to sickness absence was 8.1 days for not temporary promoted staff, whilst only 2.8 days for temporary promoted staff.
answer_text: I am not aware of any factual reason as to why the percentage of working days lost due to sickness absence was greater for not temporary promoted staff, compared to temporary promoted staff.  I am not in a position to provide an opinion on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in relation to the figures in the NISRA Annual Report 2023-24, why the percentage of working days lost due to sickness absence was 4.8 days for not temporary promoted staff, whilst only 1.8 days for temporary promoted staff.
answer_text: ANSWERTo date, there has been no analysis carried out to this level of detail into absence levels of staff on temporary promotion compared to staff not on temporary promotion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the figures in the NISRA Annual Report 2023-24, why the percentage of working days lost due to sickness absence was 4.8 days for not temporary promoted staff, whilst only 2.0 days for temporary promoted staff.

To ask the Minister of Education, in relation to the figures in the NISRA Annual Report 2023-24, why the percentage of working days lost due to sickness absence was 5.7 days for not temporary promoted staff, whilst only 2.5 days for temporary promoted staff.
answer_text: NICS staff sickness absence is monitored centrally through NICS HR within the Department of Finance.There has been no analysis carried out to this level of detail into absence levels of staff on temporary promotion compared to staff not on temporary promotion.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in relation to the figures in the NISRA Annual Report 2023-24, why the percentage of working days lost due to sickness absence was 7.1 days for not temporary promoted staff, whilst only 2.7 days for temporary promoted staff.
answer_text: I am not aware of a factual reason that explains why the percentage of working days lost due to sickness absence was greater for not temporary promoted staff, compared to temporary promoted staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the figures in the NISRA Annual Report 2023-24, why the percentage of working days lost due to sickness absence was 6.2 days for not temporary promoted staff, whilst only 2.3 days for temporary promoted staff.
answer_text: To date, there has been no analysis carried out to this level of detail into absence levels of staff on temporary promotion compared to staff not on temporary promotion.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any work undertaken in her Department in the last 12 months to implement the Armed Forces Covenant.
answer_text: While the Armed Forces Covenant is implemented in Northern Ireland somewhat differently to the rest of the United Kingdom, my Department is committed to ensuring that everyone is treated with fairness and respect when accessing any services provided by the Department.  That includes those who have served in the armed forces and their families. The Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme (TPDPS) was established to provide those who suffered permanent disablement (either physical or psychological) as a result of an injury caused through no fault of their own in a Troubles-related incident with payments, primarily in acknowledgement of the acute harm which they have suffered.  The TPDPS is delivered by The Victims’ Payments Board (VPB), an arm’s-length body of The Executive Office, and officials within my Department deliver the administration functions of the VPB on the Board’s behalf.  In that context, officials within my Department engage routinely with a wide range of stakeholders to raise awareness of the TPDPS, including groups that represent veterans.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in relation to the figures in the NISRA Annual Report 2023-24, why the percentage of working days lost due to sickness absence was 5.1 days for not temporary promoted staff, whilst only 3.2 days for temporary promoted staff.
answer_text: My Department has been advised that no analysis has been carried out to this level of detail into absence levels of staff on temporary promotion compared to staff not on temporary promotion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of days that his Department took to provide the Committee for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs with a written or oral briefing on its submission to the January Monitoring Round.
answer_text: The Department provided written briefing to the Committee for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs on both stages of the January Monitoring Round on 6 December 2024.  This was on the same day as the Stage 1 return to DoF.  Officials also provided oral briefing on both stages of this Monitoring Round on 12 December 2024 which was six days after the Stage 1 return to DoF.  The Stage 2 return was submitted to DoF on 3 January 2025 and included the information in the written and oral briefing that was provided to the Committee on the 6 December and 12 December respectively.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any work undertaken in their Department in the last 12 months to implement the Armed Forces Covenant.
answer_text: There is no agreed response to The Armed Forces Covenant.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any work undertaken in his Department in the last 12 months to implement the Armed Forces Covenant.
answer_text: The Armed Forces Covenant Duty, introduced under the Armed Forces Act 2021, came into force on 22nd November 2022. This is a legal obligation on certain public bodies, including the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, to ‘have due regard’ to the principles of the Covenant and requires decisions about the development and delivery of certain services to be made with conscious consideration of the needs of the Armed Forces community.In support of this, over the past 12 months, my Departmental officials have continued to engage with relevant stakeholders, including the Office for Veterans’ Affairs and Ministry of Defence, as and when relevant issues are raised.From a Housing perspective, my officials continue to advise the Northern Ireland Housing Executive of updates to any applicable guidance or toolkits on the Covenant.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any work undertaken in her Department in the last 12 months to implement the Armed Forces Covenant.
answer_text: The Covenant has not been adopted here. My Department has done no work specific to the implementation of the Armed Forces Covenant.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work undertaken in his Department in the last 12 months to implement the Armed Forces Covenant.
answer_text: The Armed Forces Covenant has not been formally adopted in Northern Ireland.  Responsibility for implementing the Armed Forces Act 2021, which aims to support the implementation of the Armed Forces Covenant, lies with the First Minister and deputy First Minister. Notwithstanding this, Health and Social Care Trusts facilitate and coordinate care in relation to the needs of service personnel, veterans and their families living in Northern Ireland, who have equality of access to health and social care services in common with, and on the same basis as, everyone living in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of days that his Department took to provide the Committee for Health with a written or oral briefing on its submission to the January Monitoring Round.
answer_text: A written briefing on January Monitoring was issued to the Committee for Health three days after submission to the Department of Finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of days that his Department took to provide the Committee for Infrastructure with a written or oral briefing on its submission to the January Monitoring Round.
answer_text: My Department provided the Committee for Infrastructure with a written briefing five days after submission of the January Monitoring Round return to the Department of Finance. In addition, my officials provided an oral briefing to the Committee for Infrastructure on the Department’s January Monitoring Round return on 15 January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the £100 fuel payment; and (ii) to detail when people will start to receive the payment.
answer_text: My officials are working at pace to bring forward the necessary legislation and to put in place the arrangements for making the one-off fuel support payment of £100.It is anticipated the payments will be made via existing payment channels before the end of March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of days that her Department took to provide the Committee for Justice with a written or oral briefing on its submission to the January Monitoring Round.
answer_text: The Department provided the Justice Committee with a written briefing on `January Monitoring Stage 1` one day in advance of the return to the Department of Finance (DoF), and provided an oral briefing on the same submission within six days of the return to DoF. The `January Monitoring Stage 2` written briefing was provided to the Committee within one day of the return to DoF.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the waiting times for a DaTSCAN, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) any actions being taken to reduce waiting lists.
answer_text: The waiting times for a DaTSCAN, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust is outlined below.HSC TrustWaiting time for a DaTSCANBHSCT9 weeksWHSCT23 weeksSHSCT1 weekNHSCT50 weeksThe DaTSCAN is not available in the South Eastern HSC.Waiting lists vary across Trusts. One of the main causes for this relates to the Northern Trust having to replace its only SPECT scanner, which took place over a 1-2-year period. Significant estates work was required.  The region prioritised its limited budget for capital replacement to facilitate this upgrade.During this time, the Western Trust assisted the Northern Trust with its red flag and urgent nuclear medicine work. During the recent marketplace shortage of essential ingredients for radiopharmaceuticals, Southern Trust still had a supply and was able to reduce waiting times. Southern Trust has offered further capacity to assist Northern Trust with reducing its waiting times.The Northern Trust is on track to provide the most DaTSCAN activity per annum since 2016/17, which should see incremental reduction in waiting times. It is important to note HSC Trusts manage the funding they receive and the selection of patients by their clinical priority type and length of time waiting.  £76m has been provided non-recurrently for waiting list initiatives for patients requiring red flag and other time critical assessments, treatments and diagnostic investigations.  DaTSCANs will therefore be included as part of this process where patients fall into these categories.  The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) and HSC Trusts continue to work as a region to reduce variation and improve waiting times within available resources. They also work collaboratively to ensure funding is directed to the service areas with longest waiting times in the first instance and a regional process is in place to offer patients the opportunity to travel between Trusts to avail of an earlier appointment

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the number of days that their Department took to provide the Committee for the Executive Office with a written or oral briefing on its submission to the January Monitoring Round.
answer_text: January monitoring was completed in two stages. A written briefing was supplied to the Committee five days after the submission of final figures to the Department of Finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of days that his Department took to provide the Committee for the Economy with a written or oral briefing on its submission to the January Monitoring Round.
answer_text: My Department provided a written briefing, to the Committee for the Economy, for January Monitoring Stage One and Two on the same day they were submitted to the Department of Finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 20076/22-27 , to detail (i) the number of (a) domestic; and (b) non-domestic premises, broken down by local council area; and (ii) the number of (a) domestic; and (b) non-domestic premises in arrears, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: I would highlight at the outset that unpaid rates are subject to ongoing collection and recovery actions. The figures contained within this reply include rate accounts (with a combined rating debt of £59,159,457) currently paying Land & Property Services (LPS) by Direct Debit or through payment arrangement, those who have had their debt secured by LPS via the Magistrates courts, and accounts that are in the process of Enforcement of Judgement Office activity, Insolvency action and Bankruptcy action as a result of LPS referral.The table on the next page provides a breakdown at 31st December 2024 of the number of Domestic and Non-Domestic properties broken down by local council area; and the number of Domestic and Non-Domestic properties with arrears broken down by local council area. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the refurbishment of the Ballymena rail and bus station.
answer_text: The proposed new integrated new station will replace the existing separate bus and rail stations with a new modern, state of the art facility that will provide a greatly needed enhanced passenger experience. It will be fully inclusive and could be a catalyst for future investment in the Ballymena area. It also will promote sustainable transport options and act as an enhanced gateway to the wider rail network across the Island of Ireland.Translink has submitted an Outline Business Case for the project which is currently being considered by my Department. Subject to securing approval of the full business case, the availability of funding, along with other various required statutory approvals, the opening of the new station is currently planned for by Quarter 4 2030.My Department is also considering a revised business case for £5.8m to provide an additional 400 spaces at the adjacent Park & Site at the station.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the impact assessments that were conducted on the (a) £10.6 million additional revenue in 2024-25; and (b) £4.7 million additional revenue in 2025-26 from income from existing client charging in nursing and residential care homes; (ii) the average increase for the people paying this additional charge; and (iii) the number of people this affects.
To ask the Minister of Health whether he will introduce bowel cancer screening at 50 years of age.
answer_text: The three-year strategic plan for health and social care was published on 10 December 2024 and includes an outcome to expand the bowel screening programme, subject to additional funding, by April 2027. Currently, in Northern Ireland, men and women aged 60-74 years old, who are registered with a GP, are eligible for bowel cancer screening. It is important to note that screening is a pathway, not just a ‘test.’ As such, where a Screening Programme makes changes to its eligibility, it is important that the associated infrastructure, to include diagnostic capacity and any treatments that may be required, are aligned. Work to meet the commitments to lower the age range is ongoing but must be viewed within the context of wider financial and capacity challenges within the supporting services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 20171/22-27, who polices whether Bovaer is being used at the approved dose.
answer_text: DAERA is the competent authority for enforcement in Northern Ireland of The Feed Additives Regulation (Regulation (EC) No 1831/2003).  In addition, DAERA deliver controls including those on behalf of the Food Standards Agency in respect of Regulation (EU) 2017/625, on official controls and other official activities performed to ensure the application of food and feed law. There is no requirement in legislation for DAERA to certify the use of authorised feed additives.  The Feed Additives Regulation established a common procedure for authorising feed additives and lays down rules for their placing on the market, labelling and use. In 2023/24, 299 additive analyses were completed with 180 label checks to assess legislative compliance.  Official controls are risk based and focused on additives used, which if incorporated in excess, or at deficient levels, can be most harmful to livestock.  Bovaer is one of approximately 1700 additives authorised for use in animal feed.  The European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) provides a scientific opinion on the safety and efficacy of feed additives prior to EU authorisation.  The EFSA safety assessment which is applicable in NI concluded there are no safety concerns when Bovaer is used at or below the maximum recommended level.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the co-production process for the Draft Regional Being Open Framework for HSC included involvement from individuals and their families involved in inquiries and investigations into their care and treatment; and to list the particular inquiry or investigation.
answer_text: The draft Framework has been developed following a significant public consultation in 2021, following the Inquiry into Hyponatraemia-related Deaths (IHRD), to understand the impact a duty of candour would have on the health service in Northern Ireland.  That 2021 consultation process was arrived at following a comprehensive co-production process, involving hundreds of HSC practitioners and clinicians, dozens of representative organisations, unions, Royal Colleges and professional bodies, but above all, thousands of patients, carers and service users, who helped to guide and assist the Department during this time.The draft Framework has been developed taking account of the findings of this public consultation and also significant engagement across the HSC over the last 18 months, led by Mr Peter McBride (Associate Member, HSC Leadership Centre).  The Department is also organising a number of engagement sessions throughout the consultation period to ensure that the voices of everyone affected by a lack of openness in HSC services and systems are heard.  I can advise that a number of engagement sessions have already taken place including a session with the Independent Neurological Liaison Group on 11 December 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the reasons provided by the Food Standards Agency for requiring a cash advance from the NI Consolidated Fund.
answer_text: The cash advance relates to an in-year Treasury funding allocation to the Food Standards Agency (FSA) of circa £4m for activities relating to the Windsor Framework. As this allocation will not be reflected in FSA’s cash position until the 2024-25 Spring Supplementary Estimates stage is complete and associated Budget Bill receives Royal Assent, FSA require a cash advance from the NI Consolidated Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 19410/22-27, whether an assessment has been made of the rigour and robustness of the safety assessment by the Food Standards Agency and Food Standards Scotland which ensures products are safe and appropriate for the UK market.
answer_text: Bovaer has been authorised for use in Great Britain for all milk producing and breeding ruminant animals by Ministers across the nations based on the Food Standards Agency and Food Standards Scotland recommendations and supporting scientific evidence. It is also authorised for use in the EU, including Northern Ireland, for dairy and breeding cows, following a similar risk assessment by the European Food Safety Authority.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 19410/22-27, for his assessment of the accuracy of the figures that state Bovaer reduces methane in cattle by on average 30 per cent in dairy cows and 45 per cent in beef cattle.
answer_text: I accept the average figures for methane reduction in dairy cows and beef cattle which are in line with those published in peer reviewed scientific publications. I am aware that the effect of Bovaer in reducing methane in dairy cows and beef cattle is modified by the fibre, fat and starch content fed in diets.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they plan to lay a report in the Assembly on the functioning of government, as required in section 12 of the Functioning of Government (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021.
answer_text: The Functioning of Government (Miscellaneous Provisions) (NI) Act 2021 requires us to publish a report on the functioning of government every two years. In the light of the absence of a functioning Assembly and Executive Committee between February 2022 and February 2024, we think it reasonable for the first report to be prepared in respect of the functioning of government in 2024 and 2025. The first report would therefore be published during 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of perpetrators of domestic homicide committed the offence whilst out on bail in the last ten years.
answer_text: In the period, 2014 – 2023, the most recent 10 years for which information is available, there were 13 convictions for murder, where there was a domestic violence related motivation, as assessed by the Public Prosecution Service. A manual check of court records indicated that less than 5 of the 13 defendants convicted, were on court bail for another offence when the murder was committed.  The Department of Justice does not hold information on the number of persons on Police bail.  The recording of information in relation to Police bail is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board. You may, therefore, wish to direct this part of the question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of hospital admissions for flu this winter; and (ii) how this compares to previous winters over the same period, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) have provided estimates, used for surveillance, that go back to the beginning of March 2020. However, influenza was not widely circulating until autumn 2022 during the COVID-19 pandemic. Based on that caveat, the information below refers.From week 40, 2024 (commencing 30th September 2024) to week 01, 2025 (data ending 5th January 2025), there were 1,846 influenza community-acquired emergency hospital influenza admissions in Northern Ireland.From week 40, 2023 (commencing 2nd October 2023) to week 01, 2024 (week ending 7th January 2024), there were 477 influenza community-acquired emergency hospital influenza admissions in Northern Ireland. Data are presented using epidemiological weeks (a standardised method of counting weeks [Monday-Sunday] to allow for the comparison of data year after year).Community-acquired influenza emergency admissions to acute hospitals are estimated by combining data from PAS and virological reports in NIHAP. Admissions are counted where there was a positive test up to seven days before admission or up to one day after admission, and the method of admission was ‘Emergency’. The number of inpatients is counted at midnight. Admissions and occupancy refer to the first admission per infection episode. Where it is currently possible (SHSCT and WHSCT) only admissions where the method of admission was ‘Emergency’ are counted. It is not currently possible for this report to distinguish emergency from other types of admission for SEHSCT, BHSCT and NHSCT hospital data, following the introduction of a new electronic healthcare record on 06/11/2023, 06/06/2024 and 07/11/2024 respectively. For this report, all community-acquired admissions for SEHSCT, BHSCT and NHSCT are included, which will include non-emergency admissions (which are a small minority of the total admissions reported). Work is ongoing to adapt systems to new data sources and re-instate differentiation of emergency admissions. Ongoing developmental and quality assurance work may result in adjustments to figures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 19410/22-27, to detail (i) the number of cows that took part in the studies and safety assessments relating to the distribution of Bovaer on livestock in Northern Ireland; and (ii) the number of these cows that had to be prematurely euthanised, including the associated reasons.
answer_text: All feed additives that are authorised for use within the EU and Northern Ireland, are subject to a scientific assessment by the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) on the safety and efficacy of that additive specific to the proposed terms of use. The safety assessment was undertaken by the EFSA Panel on Additives and Products or Substances used in Animal Feed (FEEDAP). As part of the approval process, two studies were performed, (one in 2018 and one in 2019) with lactating dairy Holstein-Friesian cows receiving 3-NOP (Bovaer) under assessment at increasing concentrations in feed. In the first study, for each of four experimental groups (4 dairy cows/group), the total mixed ration was supplemented to provide doses of 0, 80, 400 or 800 mg/kg 3-NOP/day complete feed. This study was to establish a margin of safety if an additive is consumed at higher doses than recommended. Two cows had to be euthanised prematurely due to reduced feed intake and lethargy (one from 400 mg/kg 3-NOP/day complete feed, and one from the 800 mg/kg 3-NOP/day complete feed group).  A second study was performed with 80 Holstein-Friesian dairy cows. In each of the four experimental groups of 20 cows, the total mixed ration was supplemented to achieve intended concentrations of 0, 80, 100, or 200 mg 3-NOP/kg feed dry matter. No cows were reported to have been euthanised prematurely in this trial.  The FEEDAP Panel considers the additive as safe for dairy cows at the maximum recommended concentration in feed (100 mg 3-NOP/kg dry matter). These tolerance tests were undertaken in line with EFSA FEEDAP published technical guidance on tolerance and efficacy studies in target animals.  https://efsa.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/pdf/10.2903/j.efsa.2011.2175 The FEEDAP safety panel report is published in an open-access scientific journal: Safety and efficacy of a feed additive consisting of 3-nitrooxypropanol (Bovaer® 10) for ruminants for milk production and reproduction (DSM Nutritional Products Ltd) https://doi.org/10.2903/j.efsa.2021.6905The Animal Feed and Feed Additives Joint Expert Group (AFFAJEG) evaluated the same tolerance studies for Food Standards Agency as part of its safety assessment of 3-NOP (Bovaer).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: I accept the findings of the Food Standards Agency and European Food Standards Agency safety assessments which concluded that there are no safety concerns to consumers, animals or the environment when Bovaer is used at the approved dose.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 18913/22-27, why it took from October 2023 to 25 November 2024 for Health Estates operational staff to Inform the Director of Health Estates about the issue with the gas pipework in the maternity and neonatal hospital.
answer_text: This was a matter known to Health Estates operational officials as one of several issues being dealt with on the project prior to handover.  It was recorded at time of handover in March 2024 on a caveat list. Officials had not previously escalated this matter to the Director of Health Estates due to the limited scale of the issue and that it was being managed at an operational level.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 18916/22-27, when the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust accepted the (i) new Maternity Hospital; and (ii) Neonatal Hospital.
answer_text: The private sector Consultant Design Team with contractual responsibility accepted the new Maternity Hospital on behalf of the Trust on 29 March 2024. The Trust accepted handover on the same date. The new Maternity Hospital main building (Phase B1) includes a neonatal ward. There is no separate new Neonatal Hospital.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why Denroy DenPro FFP3 masks were quarantined on 27 December 2024.
answer_text: On 16th December 2024 all Trusts were asked to quarantine the Denpro FFP3 over-labelled (Feb 2025) masks, whilst the manufacturer updated the technical documents required to support their shelf life. BSO PaLS has been working with the manufacturer to explore extending the shelf life of their products as part of ongoing HSCNI PPE stock management. In this case, BSO was obligated to put a stop on this consignment, and ensure other stock with the original manufactured shelf life, was available for Trusts to order and use in the interim

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the evidence that livestock fed on Bovaer produce up to 30 per cent less methane when they are at grass.
answer_text: The mode of action of Bovaer, 3-nitrooxypropanol (3NOP) requires that the product is constantly present in the cattle rumen for the fullest effect of the product to be observed.For this reason, the majority of the 100-plus research studies conducted on Bovaer worldwide have been on cattle fed indoors on total mixed rations fed through a diet feeder machine which mixes all components of the diet into a consistent mix.There is less research published in the use of Bovaer fed to livestock at grass and initial studies show that a reduction in methane emissions of 30 per cent is possible where this is supplemented with access to a total mixed ration.  Research trials are also ongoing with Bovaer provided through the drinking water as a method of delivery to grazing cattle.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 19410/22-27, to detail how there can be a difference in the methane reduction in dairy cattle compared to beef cattle.
answer_text: Methane produced by cattle is influenced by the feed intake of animals and the content of that feed. Equations, published in the Journal of Dairy Science in 2023, show that the reduction in enteric methane emissions can be predicted from the feed rate of 3NOP, and the fibre, fat and starch contents of the cattle diet.The diets fed to cattle managed and bred for different purposes vary considerably in fibre fat and starch content.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 18916/22-27, at the time of the handover of the new Maternity Hospital and Neonatal Hospital who was the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s Senior Responsible Owner for the project.
answer_text: At the time of the handover of the new Maternity Hospital the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s Senior Responsible Owner for the project was Maureen Edwards.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 19410/22-27, to outline whose responsibility it is to ensure that there are sufficient checks and measures in place to certify that Bovaer is used as instructed.
answer_text: This does not sit within the remit of my Department.All authorised feed additives undergo rigorous safety assessment by the Food Standards Agency whose responsibility it is to ensure that products are safe and appropriate for the UK market.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the cost of the consultation on the reduction of the early payment discount for rates.
answer_text: There is an estimated advertising cost associated with the forthcoming consultation of around £2,500.Aside from that there is no additional cost to my Department. Consultation is part of best practice public policy making and is is undertaken by Rating Policy Division within the Department of Finance as part of their routine engagement activity.

answer_text: AQW 16239/22-27 was answered on 15 January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will consider how driving theory and awareness of the highway code could be applied to the curriculum in schools.
answer_text: The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) offers the GCSE specification in ‘Motor Vehicle and Road User Studies’ to pupils at Key Stage 4. This qualification prepares students to become responsible road users, develops interest in and appreciation of the motor vehicle and develops pupils’ knowledge of the Highway Code. Pupils also study road users’ responsibilities and explore motor vehicle and road user theory.  There are also opportunities throughout the curriculum for teachers to cover the issue of road safety. At post-primary level, the Learning for Life and Work Area of Learning requires that pupils should have opportunities to develop preventative strategies in relation to accidents in the home, school and on the road.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current staff levels of Healthcare Assistants; and (ii) the deemed safe level for Healthcare Assistants, in Antrim Area Hospital, broken down by ward.
answer_text: The funded staffing levels (FSL) for Band 2/3 Nursing assistants for medical and surgical wards are based on the Delivering Care Policy Framework 2016. This recommends a nurse to bed ratio of between 1.3 and 1.5 with a registered to unregistered ratio of 70:30.  Specialist wards would be funded at a nurse to bed ratio of at least 1.5.Safe staffing requirements will fluctuate slightly as they depend on patient acuity, patient needs and skill mix or patients requiring Enhance Patient Care Observations (EPCO).  The ward manager will use their professional judgement to decide if they need to request additional staff to ensure safe staffing.  They may also do this if they have vacancies (temp or perm) or if there is staff sickness. Bank and Agency staff will be utilised in these circumstances.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 17283/22-27, when the decision to ask the Public Health Authority and the Information Analysis Directorate to investigate excess deaths, made on 15 September 2022, was relayed to the Committee for Health.
answer_text: The Department does not hold the information on whether or when the decision to ask the Public Health Authority and the Information Analysis Directorate to investigate excess deaths, made on 15 September 2022, was relayed to the Committee for Health.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has any plans to provide grant support for the installation of solar panels within domestic settings.
answer_text: As you may be aware, the Windsor framework enables the extension to the 	North of the VAT relief for energy-saving materials that was previously 	available only in Britain. That means that people here will also be able to apply 	zero rates of VAT to the installation of energy-saving materials, such as solar 	panels.		Homeowners may also wish to seek independent advice on solar panels and 	other energy efficiency matters from organisations such as NI Energy Advice, 	the Energy Saving Trust or Citizen’s Advice. These technologies have the 	potential to provide an attractive payback to homeowners without grant support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 17640/22-27, whether gambling harm is addressed through teaching on gambling, gaming and financial capability across several areas of the curriculum in (i) all primary schools; and (ii) all post-primary schools.
answer_text: In the Northern Ireland curriculum, the topic of gambling harm is explored through the wider themes of financial capability, risk management and making responsible choices.  While the term "gambling harm" is not always explicitly referenced, the curriculum includes aspects that enable students to comprehend the dangers and outcomes linked to gambling and related behaviours. Financial capability, encompassing awareness of gambling and gaming, is already established as a statutory and essential component of the curriculum.  Opportunities for the teaching and learning of financial capability occur across the curriculum and through young people’s everyday experiences inside and outside school.  At primary level, financial capability is most closely associated with Mathematics and Numeracy, however, it features across all the areas of learning.  For example, under Personal Development and Mutual Understanding, using the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment’s (CCEA) Gambling and Gaming Awareness resource, teachers can help pupils understand what gaming is, the types of games available and the associated risks.  This includes the amount of time spent on gaming, in-game transactions, and the difference between ‘selected’ and ‘random’ (gambling) purchases.At post-primary level, financial capability is further embedded across the curriculum and teachers should provide pupils with opportunities to enhance their knowledge and understanding of a range of finance issues, to develop skills that will enable them to make competent and responsible financial decisions throughout their lives.  Under the Learning for Life and Work (LLW) area of learning, using CCEA’s Gambling and Gaming Awareness resource, teachers can help pupils recognise that gambling and gaming products are designed to make money, and that advertising actively promotes them.  Furthermore, LLW also provides young people with opportunities to explore issues around addiction and its implications for financial wellbeing.  Educating students about the risks associated with gambling is consistent with the objectives of the curriculum.  These aims include promoting critical thinking and informed decision-making, providing students with the necessary skills to navigate risks in their personal and financial affairs, and tackling contemporary societal issues, such as the increasing acceptance of gambling via digital platforms.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, given the uncertainty in the data presented by his Department to the Committee for the Economy on 11 September 2024, raising doubts on the 12 per cent return for recipients on the Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme, what more can be done to be open and transparent and to provide reassurance to participants and the wider public.
answer_text: Both I, and my officials, have been clear that some participants on the RHI Scheme are not currently receiving a prospective Internal Rate of Return of 12 per cent. I brought draft regulations before the Assembly on 21 October to introduce more appropriate tariff levels for small and medium biomass, which would have helped address the immediate issue of under-compensation while closure proposals were being developed; unfortunately, the Assembly did not approve the draft regulations and participants therefore remain on the current, lower tariffs.Regarding openness and transparency, my officials have recently had a constructive meeting with key stakeholders in the Non-domestic RHI Scheme. Further engagement is planned for early in the new year, with the intention that this engagement will continue.The Department is also finalising a report of the last public consultation exercise and will publish this at the earliest opportunity. Consultation directly with participants and with the wider public is intended as part of the process of progressing closure proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, given that a 12 per cent return has not been paid to Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme participants since 2020, what will be the cost to date of compensating participants.
answer_text: The current Executive has agreed to closure of the Scheme. A proposed interim increase in tariffs which was calculated to deliver a 12% prospective rate of return was also agreed by the Executive. The draft legislation to introduce the interim tariff increase was brought to the Assembly on 21 October 2024. Forecasting showed the ‘typical’ scheme participant would have received an uplift in the region of £5,000 per annum. The Assembly however, did not approve the draft legislation and participants with small and medium biomass installations therefore remain on the current, lower tariffs.I am now working to progress the closure of the Scheme in line with the Executive commitment. The cost of Scheme closure will depend on the outcome of the business case process.  I will aim to ensure that the closure payment mechanism and compensation will be fair to both the participants, who joined the Scheme in good faith, and the taxpayer who funds the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any health risks for human beings when consuming meat and dairy products from livestock that have come into contact with Bovaer or other methane suppressants.
answer_text: Bovaer is a feed supplement that reduces methane in cattle by on average 30% in dairy cows and 45% in beef cattle. All authorised feed additives undergo rigorous safety assessment by the Food Standards Agency and Food Standards Scotland. This ensures products are safe and appropriate for the UK market. Bovaer has been authorised for use in the UK for all milk producing and breeding ruminant animals by Ministers across the nations based on the FSA/FSS recommendations and supporting evidence. It is also authorised for use in the EU for dairy and breeding cows, following a similar risk assessment by the European Food Safety Authority. Experts in the UK and EU concluded that its use poses no safety concerns to consumers, animals or the environment when used as instructed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) how widespread is the use of Bovaer in Northern Ireland; and (ii) how this compares with England, Scotland, Wales and the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: Bovaer is a commercially available feed additive and my Department does not hold information on its use.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to detail any research that is being conducted in the use of methane suppressants in livestock, including (a) the cost of the research; and (b) who is conducting the research; and (ii) to detail the health and safety measures in place to ensure its safety.
answer_text: There are two ongoing DAERA Directed Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI) projects that include methane suppressant trials.  DAERA has recently commissioned the ‘CH4 SUPRESS’ project which aims to increase understanding of the short and long-term impacts of offering methane supressing feed additives on rumen function, methane production and dairy cow performance (milk production and animal health and welfare traits). (Research Organisation: AFBI; DAERA Funding: £3.0M).  This complements ongoing work at AFBI on the ‘BELCH4’ project which is studying the early life health, nutrition and environment in cattle and the impact that this has on the rumen microbiome and the plasma metabolome.  (Research Organisation: AFBI; DAERA Funding: £1.95M).DAERA is also co-funding two cross-border projects with Department of Agriculture, Food and the Marine. The ‘Meth-Abate’ project is aimed at developing and validating novel technologies to reduce methane emissions from pasture-based agricultural systems.  (Research Organisations: AFBI, Teagasc, National University of Ireland Galway; DAERA funding: £236K, DAFM funding: £863K). The ‘MAGS’ project, will develop, apply and validate a range of the most promising novel technologies (including breeding strategies, feed and manure additives) to mitigate enteric and manure methane in beef and dairy pasture-based production systems. (Research Organisations: AFBI, Teagasc, National University of Ireland Galway, Irish Cattle Breeding Federation; DAERA funding: £270K, DAFM funding £995K). AFBI has carried out risk assessments for each of the studies in these projects. In line with those risk assessment, its research teams use personal protective equipment when recommended.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the support currently available for people wanting to install air source heat pumps; and (ii) any plans in place to produce a support scheme in the near future.
answer_text: At present, financial assistance is available through the Northern Ireland Sustainable Energy Programme (NISEP), for fully supported, and part funded schemes, for the installation of energy efficiency measures and heat pumps, with most of the funding targeted at lower income customers. My department is providing additional funding to NISEP to support energy efficiency programmes in the current financial year and exploring the potential to further enhance support for heat pump installations alongside energy efficiency programmes over the next two financial years.		Future support is being consulted on through my department’s consultation; ‘Support for low carbon heat in the residential sector’ which was launched on the 8th of October and closes 31st December. The views provided will help my department in the development of policy in this area to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support for low carbon heat, which will include heat pumps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when a timeline will be agreed to better supply insulin pumps and hybrid closed loop systems to people living with type 1 diabetes.
answer_text: The Regional Insulin Pump Service is a clinical model currently delivered in Northern Ireland to provide equity, reduce variability, and enhance patient outcomes.  Following the endorsement of the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943 as applicable to Northern Ireland, my Department has requested work to be undertaken to understand the implications.   Overall provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements, to both enable and support, are being considered. Funding is required for devices in addition to upskilling and recruiting the workforce needed to offer Hybrid Closed Loop safely, effectively and equitably across the region.  Therefore, given the scale of investment needed, financial requirements are also being considered.  Final decision-making and commissioning arrangements for NI will be made, supported by an implementation plan. This will support the integration continuous glucose monitoring technologies into diabetes care and will be communicated to all stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current staffing levels for specialist diabetes (a) consultants; and (b) nurses, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) the staffing levels required to provide basic levels of care and annual check-ups.
answer_text: Across both adult & paediatric services, there are currently 19 Diabetes Specialist Nurses employed within Northern Trust (15.34 whole time equivalent); and 6 consultants (8.85 whole time equivalent/ Programmed activities); 18 in South Eastern Trust (16.93 whole time equivalent) and 6 consultants (5.3 whole time equivalent/ Programmed activities); 18 in Belfast Trust (16.72 whole time equivalent); 21 in the Western Trust (17.28 whole time equivalent) and 6 consultants (5.89 whole time equivalent/ Programmed activities); 21 in Southern Trust (19.7 whole time equivalent). Consultant information has yet to be provided by Belfast and Southern Trusts. Waiting lists remain long, with limited specialist workforce currently.  However, a Diabetes Programme of Care Workforce Review is near completion, led by Department of Health’s Workforce Policy Directorate.  This 10-year forward view will inform workforce planning needs, to ensure equitable access to a specialist workforce, to provide safe, effective and quality service for people living with diabetes

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when a clinical diabetes audit will commence in Northern Ireland, similar to the audit undertaken in England and Wales.
answer_text: The Diabetes Strategic Framework (2016) included an action to participate in National Diabetes Audit, similar to the Diabetes Audit used in England and Wales.The Department of Health remains committed to working towards participation in the National Diabetes Audit.  However, there have been significant challenges in progressing this recommendation, as the required infrastructure is not currently in place to participate fully in the audit.With the full implementation of Encompass, currently being rolled out across all Health and Social Care Trusts, the provision of data that is both available and extractable for the region will be available.  This will support participation in the National Diabetes Audit in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) who was the Chief Executive for the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust when they accepted the new maternity hospital and neonatal hospital; and (ii) who had responsibility within the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust for signing the buildings over from the contractor.
answer_text: The Chief Executive of the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust at the time the new Maternity Hospital was handed over was Dr Cathy Jack.The contractual responsibility for accepting the new Maternity Building as having met all the standards and requirements of the construction contract lay with the private sector Consultant Design Team.  At the time of handover the Trust’s Senior Responsible Owner for the project recommended acceptance of the new building to the Chief Executive on the basis of assurances received from the Consultant Design Team and DoH Health Estates.  The Trust’s Chief Executive accepted handover based on the assurances received.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of his statement in the Assembly on 26 November 2024 in relation to new issues with the gas pipework in the maternity and neonatal unit in Belfast, to detail (i) when he was told about this issue; (ii) who informed him; (iii) when that individual found out about the issue; and (iv) the date an official from his Department first found out about the new issue.
answer_text: I was told about this issue on the morning of 26 November 2024.I was informed by the Director of Health Estates.The Director of Health Estates found out about this issue on the evening of 25 November 2024.Health Estates operational staff had been aware of this issue since October 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of people employed through the HSC Workforce Appeal in 2020; (ii) the number of these people still employed by the health service; and (iii) the average length of employment, broken down by those in (a) clinical; and (b) non-clinical roles.
answer_text: The Workforce Appeal was established in an effort to try and build capacity across HSC Trusts and assist in meeting the exceptional demands during the pandemic with particular focus on certain roles and positions across hospitals and community care. (i) The total number of appointments made to HSC Trusts in 2020 as part of part of the Workforce Appeal was 766, covering both Health & Social Care (440 appointments) i.e. Medical, Nursing & Midwifery, Nursing Support, Pharmacy, Allied Health and Social Care and Clerical & Admin (326 appointments). As part of the vaccination programme the Workforce Appeal also delivered over 1,700 applications in December 2020. (ii & iii)	The Workforce Appeal was always designed with the aim of securing temporary employment in an effort to support the HSC Trusts through the pandemic. Appointments were likely to have been short term engagements of around 3 – 9 months. Information is not available on Workforce Appeal appointees that may now have a current contract with an HSC organisation, noting that this could be in a different role, different organisation and may not have been continuous since their Workforce Appeal temporary appointment. All of the appointments made under the HSC Workforce Appeal played a vital role in assisting the health service to cope with the additional demands placed upon it as a result of the pandemic.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are any incentive schemes for people working in the health service to purchase electric vehicles.
answer_text: There is no direct incentive scheme for HSC employees to purchase electric vehicles outright. A Car Leasing Scheme is in place for HSC staff.  The Scheme is administered by the South Eastern Trust. The Car Leasing Scheme is available in partnership with NHS Fleet Solutions. Electric Leases are available through this scheme and employees have the option to purchase the vehicle directly from the leasing company at the end of their lease. Leases are available for either a 2- or 3-year option.This scheme is currently offered to employees of the following HSC organisations;South Eastern Health & Social Care TrustBelfast Health & Social Care TrustNorthern Health & Social Care TrustSouthern Health & Social Care TrustWestern Health & Social Care TrustBusiness Services OrganisationStrategic Planning & Performance Group – Business Services OrganisationPublic Health AgencyRegulation & Quality Improvement AuthorityChildren’s Court Guardian Agency for NINorthern Ireland Blood Transfusion ServicePatient & Client Council Northern Ireland Ambulance Service Northern Ireland Dental & Medical Training Agency NI Practice & Education CouncilGiven your party’s support for the budget and it’s implications, not least for waiting lists, I hope you will agree further financial support for the purchase of electric vehicles would be questionable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will publish, either through a written statement to the Assembly or through this question, the remaining clauses of the Justice Bill she intends to include at Consideration Stage, as outlined in her contribution at the second reading, so that all Members get sight of the clauses in good time.
answer_text: In line with my commitment during the second stage debate to share draft amendments with the Justice Committee at the earliest possible juncture, rather than wait until they are due to be tabled for Consideration Stage as is the normal procedure for amendments, the text of each set of proposed amendments to the Justice Bill are being shared with the Committee as soon as drafting is complete. Three of the six sets of proposed amendments have already been shared with the Committee, and the others will follow in December.I understand the Committee will publish all amendments as part of their Call for Evidence, which I believe they intend to launch in February 2025.Members will, therefore, be able to view the amendments once that Call for Evidence goes live.  The Committee have extended the Committee stage of the bill by up to 18 months so this should give all Members more than ample time to consider the additional clauses prior to consideration stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of incidences of heart attacks of (i) pupils; (ii) teachers; and (iii) staff in a school setting or environment, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: My Department does not record or hold the information requested.    I have invested over £700,000 to provide defibrillators in around 600 schools which drastically increases the chance of pupils, teachers, visitors and the wider community surviving a cardiac-arrest.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission how it determined the cost for replacing existing printers in constituency offices.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of puppies and dogs illegally imported into Northern Ireland via Belfast and Larne ferry ports in each of the last two years.
answer_text: Under the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the import of puppies and dogs and monitoring thereof no longer sits within my ministerial responsibilities. It comes under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the non-commercial movement of pets into Northern Ireland, to detail any assessment he has made of (i) the current procedure in relation to the stop and search of vehicles entering Northern Ireland via ferry; and (ii) calls from the Dogs Trust to implement visual checks for all dogs entering Northern Ireland via Belfast and Larne ferry ports.
answer_text: Under the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the non-commercial movement of pets into Northern Ireland no longer sits within my ministerial responsibilities. It comes under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on his meeting with the Broughshane and District Community Association in relation to further funding for a new community centre for Broughshane.
answer_text: I met with Broughshane and District Community Association (BCDA) on 17 July.  The organisation outlined its plans to transform the existing Community Hall into a Health and Wellbeing Centre. At the meeting I reflected the challenging budgetary position faced by my Department but committed to supporting the project in any other way that might be helpful.  My officials have since liaised with the BCDA to confirm that no Departmental funding is currently available to support the project, but a list of non-governmental capital funding streams that they may be able to avail of has been shared. While not led by my Department, my officials have also responded to queries from the group on the Communities and Place strand of the Shared Prosperity Fund.  I also understand that the group has applied for funding from the Peaceplus programme and I wish them every success with their future aspirations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any discussions that have taken place to plan for a strategy for percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery.
To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on plans for a police museum.
answer_text: The responsibility for the development of the Police Museum is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board. Therefore, it may be prudent to direct your question to the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgeries will take place in (i) November 2024; (ii) December 2024; and (iii) January 2025.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) when percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgeries commenced in the private sector; and (ii) the number of children that received percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery through private care in the last 12 months.
answer_text: Information on when percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgeries commenced in the private sector is not held.Information on the number of children that received percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery through private care in the last 12 months is not held.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the roll-out for the plans for percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgeries in the Ulster Hospital will take place; and is this planned to be a long-term solution.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of children that have been scoped to be suitable to travel to Alder Hey Children's Hospital for percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery; (ii) how soon measures can be put in place to allow this to happen; (iii) whether this is seen as a long-term measure; and (iv) whether any other hospital in Great Britain can assist in this matter.
To ask the Minister of Health, if winter pressures have impacted the October initiative for percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgeries, whether it will recommence in 2025.
To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on the United States/Northern Ireland Cultural Working Group.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on their meeting with the Vaccine Injured and Bereaved Support group for Northern Ireland.

To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the roll-out of encompass, to detail any difference members of the public will see when obtaining their medical notes for Personal Independence Payment and Employment Support Allowance appeals.
answer_text: The current focus of the encompass programme is the successful deployment of the regional Electronic Patient record to all five HSC Trusts. Whilst functionality exists within the encompass system to support sharing of information, existing processes for exchange of information with non HSC organisations will not be replaced at this time.  Consideration will be given to expanding access, where it benefits NI citizens, to non HSC organisations in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the deaths recorded for the years (i) 2019; (ii) 2020; (iii) 2021; (iv) 2022; and (v) 2023.
answer_text: NISRA collates data on death registrations as collected by the General Register Office.  The information requested for the years 2019 to 2022 can be found in the Registrar General Annual Report. Statistical tables are available for both the number of registered deaths along with details regarding the cause of death.Final figures for 2023 are not yet available but will be published as part of the Registrar General Annual Report on 21 November 2024. Table 1c of the Registrar General Quarterly Tables for quarter 2 2024 provides provisional headline figures for deaths registered in 2023 and in quarters 1 and 2 of 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum will consider a statutory requirement for schools to educate pupils on the dangers of gambling as part of the core curriculum.
answer_text: The review of the Northern Ireland curriculum, to be undertaken by Lucy Crehan, will make a series of policy recommendations regarding the purpose, design, specification and implementation of the Northern Ireland Curriculum.  The review will commence shortly, therefore it is too soon to comment on priority areas of inquiry.  It is unlikely that the final report will include detailed proposals on individual course/subject content.  Although not explicitly mentioned within the Northern Ireland Curriculum, gambling harm is presently addressed through teaching on gambling, gaming, and financial capability across several areas of the curriculum.  At primary level this is considered within Personal Development and Mutual Understanding. At post-primary level this is taught within Learning for Life and Work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, further to the Public Health Agency’s current investigation into excess deaths in Northern Ireland, (i) to detail that investigation’s (a) scope; (b) remit; (c) timescales; and (d) deadline; and (ii) how this investigation differs from the Department’s Information and Analysis Directorate investigation.
answer_text: The decision to ask Public Health Authority (PHA) and the Department’s Information Analysis Directorate (IAD) to further investigate excess deaths was made at the meeting of 15 September 2022, which was chaired by the Chief Medical Officer, following a request from former Minister Swann on 23 August 2022. Following the meeting, the Head of Health Intelligence and a Consultant in Public Health at the PHA were tasked, along with a senior colleague in IAD, with taking forward the further investigation using Office for Health Improvement and Disparities (OHID) methodology.  The former Minister of Health was not at the meeting.As the PHA investigation into excess deaths is not a cross-cutting issue it has not been formally notified to the Executive.  I made reference to the investigation during Question Time at the Assembly on 1 July 2024. The request to commence the investigation was made to the PHA at the meeting on 15th September.  The Department does not hold information about the date on which work on the investigation commenced, or the number of PHA staff working on this investigation.  Updates to the Department of Health are provided via a Consultant in Public Health at the PHA and are received by the Chief Scientific Advisor.  The investigation was intended to be comprehensive and exploratory, initially utilising the OHID excess deaths methodology which differs from the published methodology used by Office for National Statistics/Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency/IAD. PHA initially conducted analyses, as requested, according to the OHID methods described at the following links: https://www.gov.uk/guidance/measuring-excess-mortality-a-guide-to-the-main-reports; https://www.gov.uk/government/statistics/excess-mortality-in-england-and-english-regions.  While the work was being undertaken, the UK’s statistical authorities reviewed, revised and agreed on more robust statistical methods, which were adopted by ONS and NISRA for their reporting.  OHID ceased reporting using its previous method. These changes to methods superseded the OHID method that was previously used and meant that it was no longer appropriate to continue using the previous method. These changes of method and the impact of the changes are described at the following links: https://www.ons.gov.uk/releases/newmethodologyforestimatingexcessdeathsintheukfebruary2024 https://www.nisra.gov.uk/publications/excess-deaths-change-methodology https://www.gov.uk/government/statistics/excess-mortality-within-england-post-pandemic-method/changes-to-ohids-reporting-of-excess-mortality-in-england Subsequent to this, PHA is undertaking further work, building on these agreed methods, to provide further insight into the patterns of mortality following the COVID-19 pandemic. NISRA continues to be the source of official statistics on death registration.The PHA analysis requires access to individual patient level data, which in Northern Ireland needs to be sourced in an anonymised manner th

NISRA continues to be the source of official statistics on death registration.The PHA analysis requires access to individual patient level data, which in Northern Ireland needs to be sourced in an anonymised manner th

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, further to the Public Health Agency’s current investigation into excess deaths in Northern Ireland, to detail (i) the date the investigation commenced; (ii) the number of staff working on this investigation; (iii) who updates the Department of Health; and (iv) who within the Department of Health receives these updates.
answer_text: The decision to ask Public Health Authority (PHA) and the Department’s Information Analysis Directorate (IAD) to further investigate excess deaths was made at the meeting of 15 September 2022, which was chaired by the Chief Medical Officer, following a request from former Minister Swann on 23 August 2022. Following the meeting, the Head of Health Intelligence and a Consultant in Public Health at the PHA were tasked, along with a senior colleague in IAD, with taking forward the further investigation using Office for Health Improvement and Disparities (OHID) methodology.  The former Minister of Health was not at the meeting.As the PHA investigation into excess deaths is not a cross-cutting issue it has not been formally notified to the Executive.  I made reference to the investigation during Question Time at the Assembly on 1 July 2024. The request to commence the investigation was made to the PHA at the meeting on 15th September.  The Department does not hold information about the date on which work on the investigation commenced, or the number of PHA staff working on this investigation.  Updates to the Department of Health are provided via a Consultant in Public Health at the PHA and are received by the Chief Scientific Advisor.  The investigation was intended to be comprehensive and exploratory, initially utilising the OHID excess deaths methodology which differs from the published methodology used by Office for National Statistics/Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency/IAD. PHA initially conducted analyses, as requested, according to the OHID methods described at the following links: https://www.gov.uk/guidance/measuring-excess-mortality-a-guide-to-the-main-reports; https://www.gov.uk/government/statistics/excess-mortality-in-england-and-english-regions.  While the work was being undertaken, the UK’s statistical authorities reviewed, revised and agreed on more robust statistical methods, which were adopted by ONS and NISRA for their reporting.  OHID ceased reporting using its previous method. These changes to methods superseded the OHID method that was previously used and meant that it was no longer appropriate to continue using the previous method. These changes of method and the impact of the changes are described at the following links: https://www.ons.gov.uk/releases/newmethodologyforestimatingexcessdeathsintheukfebruary2024 https://www.nisra.gov.uk/publications/excess-deaths-change-methodology https://www.gov.uk/government/statistics/excess-mortality-within-england-post-pandemic-method/changes-to-ohids-reporting-of-excess-mortality-in-england Subsequent to this, PHA is undertaking further work, building on these agreed methods, to provide further insight into the patterns of mortality following the COVID-19 pandemic. NISRA continues to be the source of official statistics on death registration.The PHA analysis requires access to individual patient level data, which in Northern Ireland needs to be sourced in an anonymised manner th

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, further to recent findings published by the Northern Ireland Audit Office, to outline why 16 recommendations of the Report of the Independent Public Inquiry into the Non-Domestic Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme, published in March 2020, have not been fully implemented.
answer_text: Collectively, the recommendations of the RHI Inquiry informed an extensive body of change in the Civil Service, the great bulk of which has been implemented in the demanding context of the Covid pandemic and the absence of political institutions. 11 of the 16 recommendations are in many cases substantially complete already and only awaiting completion and verification by the Audit Office.In respect of the remaining five, substantive change has already been achieved.  I shall be bringing recommendations to the Executive about how these shall be taken forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the campaign by families of children with complex needs who are on a waiting list for percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy insertion, to detail the measures he will take to address (i) the reported concerns of these parents; and (ii) the waiting list for this surgery.
answer_text: The issue of access to percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) insertion for children and young people is an important issue as is the range of all surgical procedures that face children and young people in Northern Ireland.  This procedure is a complex procedure that requires specialised clinical and nursing input prior to, during and post procedure.  There are currently 83 children currently on the waiting list for this elective procedure.  My officials have been working with BHSCT to identify measures in order to try and address this ongoing issue.  The Belfast Trust is currently scheduling additional clinics and theatre sessions to help reduce paediatric waiting times and additional Paediatric Gastrostomy procedures have been approved.  The Trust has now scheduled three dates between October and December to tackle the backlog.  BHSCT is exploring the potential for another NHS body to carry out the procedure and is also considering the use of the private sector to address some of the backlog of patients. There are a small number of patients who will require a post-operative bed and RBHSC are reviewing how this resource can be ring fenced as we move towards winter and the impact that unscheduled pressures have yearly on our elective services. I want to assure you that officials in the Department as well as colleagues in BHSCT are working hard to try and address this issue but, given the ongoing pressures on the service and demand for theatre capacity, this will mean that this will not be solved immediately.  We will continue to assess and gauge progress on this to ensure that all of the children and young people receive the care they require as quickly as possible

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the current position on the collection and retention of biometric material and data when a person is given a fixed penalty notice under Section 60 of the Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.
answer_text: If a person is arrested for any recordable offence (an offence that could result in a prison sentence) and is brought to a police custody suite, they will routinely have their DNA and fingerprints taken. The current law regarding the retention of DNA and fingerprints in Northern Ireland is Article 64 of the Police and Criminal Evidence (Northern Ireland) Order 1989. It states that the police may retain the DNA and fingerprints taken by police (after they have fulfilled the purposes for which they were taken) in connection with a recordable offence, irrespective of whether or not it results in a conviction.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the total sum of Annually Managed Expenditure (AME) allocated to support renewable heat energy from 1 April 2018 to 31 March 2024; (ii) the total sum paid out in support of the (a) non-domestic; and (b) domestic Renewable Heat Incentive schemes in the period 1 April 2018 to 31 March 2024; and (iii) the total sum of AME funding available for decarbonisation of heating processes returned to HM Treasury in the period 1 April 2018 to 31 March 2024.
answer_text: From 1 April 2018 to 31 March 2024, the total amount of Renewable Heat Annually Managed Expenditure allocation was £188.6m.From 1 April 2018 to 31 March 2023, the total sum paid in respect of the:non-domestic scheme was £35.8mdomestic scheme was £12.1mThis represents the last audited position and will be updated to include the 2023-24 financial year upon publication of DfE’s Annual Report.From 1 April 2018 to 31 March 2023 the last audited position, the total amount of unutilised Renewable Heat AME was £107.3m. RHI is a demand led Scheme and whilst not all of the budget made available from Treasury to fund RHI has been drawn down there remains a responsibility to taxpayers that we cannot simply adopt a tariff that maximises use of the available budget. To date the Department has used the AME renewable heat budget solely for NI RHI Schemes. It was previously agreed with Treasury that once RHI was closed in line with New Decade New Approach, the AME could be used for another scheme. However, the collapse of the Executive meant this was not progressed. The Department is now working to close RHI so that we can develop a new more effective scheme which uses the remaining AME, subject to Treasury agreement.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, further to the debate on the Second Stage of the Justice Bill on 1 October 2024, to detail the rationale for the advice issued by their Department that Ministers should limit legislation to one Bill per year.
answer_text: The advice on how many Bills to consider bringing forward was provided directly to departments from First Legislative Counsel to the Department of Justice in the specific context of that Department’s legislative proposals for the remainder of this Assembly mandate. We understand that First Legislative Counsel’s advice was based in particular on his understanding of the anticipated size and scope of the Department for Justice Bills, together with his wider assessment at the time of the amount of work likely to be involved for his office in preparing the Executive’s Legislative Programme as a whole. No restriction can be set by officials in the Executive Office on the number of Bills any Executive Minister may seek to introduce in any Assembly session.
