question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when Translink will receive new stock of concessionary fare forms for 60+ SmartPass and Senior (65+) SmartPass applications; (ii) why the new stock of forms was recalled; and (iii) the nature of the supplier error within the forms.
answer_text: The new test batch of forms has been accepted and can now be printed; we are awaiting an estimated delivery from the printer. The stock of forms was recalled due to an error with the tracking barcodes used on the forms. The nature of the error was a mistake made by the supplier when inputting the barcode range.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, given that the Northern Ireland (Regional Rates and Energy) Act 2019 and the Renewable Heat Incentive tariff are accounted by his Department officials as inadequate to deliver a 12 per cent return, whether he will propose a retroactive adjustment of the tariff to April 2019.
answer_text: It is proposed that the increase in tariff levels for small and medium biomass installations will apply on a prospective basis from 1 November 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will publish the Clean Air Strategy report.
answer_text: I have now set the policy direction for this Strategy, which my officials are progressing. Some actions will be the responsibility of other Departments and I will work with my Ministerial colleagues to finalise this important cross-cutting Strategy.  Competing resourcing pressures on existing key departmental priorities within my department may impact the timetable for delivery of this work.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when he will publish the public consultation report for the criteria required to make heat pump installation a viable option for buildings.
answer_text: My department officials are in the final stages of preparing a public consultation on a low carbon heat support scheme and expect to launch in the coming weeks. The outcome of this consultation will be reported on once due consideration of responses and subsequent evaluations have been completed, and will help to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support for low carbon heating technologies, which will include heat pumps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps he is taking to provide additional and sustainable funding to the Northern Ireland Children's Hospice that is sufficient to cover 50 per cent of the running costs.
answer_text: I understand the financial sustainability issues facing the Northern Ireland Children’s Hospice (NICH) and other hospices linked to increased operational costs and fundraising challenges. My Department has been engaging with the Northern Ireland Childrens Hospice to understand the costs incurred in delivering the services that the Department procures from them. These are being benchmarked against similar services to ensure value for money and to ensure that the funding received by the Northern Ireland Children’s Hospice is in line with the level of service provided. It is anticipated that this work will conclude within the current financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the action he is taking to ensure the number of community children's nurses employed by the health service meets safe staffing levels as recommended by the Royal College of Nursing.
answer_text: The recruitment of nurses across all fields of practice is the responsibility of individual Health and Social Care Trusts, rather than the Department. My Department, as commissioner of pre-registration training programmes, remains committed to ongoing investment in the nursing and midwifery workforce thereby ensuring a sufficient supply of newly qualified nurses for HSC. Delivering Care is the policy framework which aids in determining safe and effective staffing ranges for nursing and midwifery across a range of settings within Northern Ireland. A refresh of this work is being completed and is led by the Chief Nursing Officer and her team, together with key stakeholders from across the region.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the progress his Department has made in implementing the recommendations of the Strategy for Children’s Palliative and End-of-Life Care 2016-26.
answer_text: The Department and then Minister published the Paediatric Palliative and End of Life Care Strategy in 2016.  This strategy was drafted after considerable consultation with all relevant stakeholders across the sector in NI and beyond to ensure it was as comprehensive as possible.The issue of access to paediatric palliative care for children and young people in Northern Ireland is extremely important to me and to the work that is being done within my department.The Strategy includes 23 ambitious objectives that cover the full range of issues within paediatric palliative care, from diagnosis of a palliative or life limited condition through to bereavement support for families of those children who have passed away.Subsequent to the publication of the strategy and with the availability of the necessary funding, which was made available from 2019, as a result of Transformation Funding, a number of specific innovations were introduced in NI.  Including the following: -The appointment of a paediatric palliative care lead in each Trust;The appointment of a Regional Paediatric Palliative Care consultant who was trained at Great Ormond Street but works at the RBHSC and has input across the region;The appointment of Paediatric Palliative and Life Limited Nurse and service.  The nurse works in Royal Jubilee Maternity Hospital and Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children supporting staff and facilitating a move to non-hospital setting (home or hospice) for step down or step up care, and where appropriate, an end of life care service. As well as the specialist nursing role, this service has access to one 24/7 hospice bed or virtual hospice bed with support available for over 300 bed nights per year.In 2019 a Regional Paediatric Palliative Care Network (RPPCN) was created and this is chaired by a Paediatric Palliative Clinical Care lead.  The network is made up of key clinical and nursing leads from each Trust as well as the NI Children’s Hospice.This network works closely together on two main areas: to identify the key priorities for service delivery ensuring they are in line with the strategy and putting in place measures to deliver same; and ensure that the network acts as a vehicle for the region to identify areas of best practice and share clinical opinion on specific cases to ensure continuous improvement.To date the network has developed ante-natal care pathways, rapid discharge plans, advanced care plans and has also agreed pathways regarding pain relief, amongst others.  Currently the network is also working on other key issues including the provision of 24/7 end of life care, transitions to adult care as well as examining how colleagues can work more proactively with colleagues from the Republic of Ireland.To assist with the dissemination of clinical best practice a Project Echo scheme is run by the NI Children’s Hospice on behalf of the region.  These are monthly clinical sessions where clinicians across NI come together to discuss and disseminate cl

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will consider applying a new measure to MOT tests to check whether vehicles have been tuned to accomplish greater performance and speed.
answer_text: The existing legislative framework covering MOT testing does not provide for the introduction of a measure to test whether vehicles have been tuned to accomplish greater performance and speed. It is also not something that is included in the MOT testing regimes in the South or in Britain and would add significantly to the time taken to do each test.I therefore have no plans to consider including such a measure at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of farm holdings in North Antrim that will be excluded from the Farm Sustainability Payment due to not meeting the proposed criteria of five hectares of eligible land.
answer_text: On the basis of the 2023 Basic Payment Scheme application data, 109 farm businesses in the North Antrim constituency may be impacted by the increase in minimum claim size to 5 hectares, however they will have the option to acquire additional eligible land and/or payment entitlements to ensure they remain eligible to claim FSP.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he plans to introduce regulations that would remove the requirement to provide a hard copy of a motor insurance certificate when taxing vehicles at post offices, similar to those introduced in Great Britain in 2013.
answer_text: Vehicle Excise Duty (Motor Tax) is an excepted matter that is administered by the Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency (DVLA) in Swansea on behalf of HM Treasury. The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has no authority or influence with the DVLA on these matters. If you wish to raise this matter with the DVLA you can contact them using the following dedicated email address for elected representatives at DVLAMinisterials@dvla.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure why Northern Ireland is the only part of the United Kingdom where an individual must produce a physical copy of motor insurance to tax a vehicle.
answer_text: Vehicle Excise Duty (Motor Tax) is an excepted matter that is administered by the Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency (DVLA) in Swansea on behalf of HM Treasury. The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has no authority or influence with the DVLA on these matters. If you wish to raise this matter with the DVLA you can contact them using the following dedicated email address for elected representatives at DVLAMinisterials@dvla.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has any plans to (i) review the Bereavement Support Payment; and (ii) extend it beyond 18 months.
answer_text: Bereavement Support Payment is a grant paid in instalments to support the immediate cost of bereavement.  Unlike its predecessor, Widowed Parent’s Allowance, it is paid in addition to means tested benefits to protect the least well off.  Responsibility for reviewing Bereavement Support Payment lies with the Secretary of State for Work and Pensions.  It is reviewed annually on a discretionary basis without expectation that the rates should be increased annually.  The Secretary of State for Work and Pensions decided that the rate of Bereavement Support Payment would not be increased from April 2024.  As my department has no power to set different rates of benefits, pensions or statutory payments in Northern Ireland, I am unable to review or extend the Bereavement Support Payment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what action he intends to take following the High Court decision on 29 May 2024 in relation to the Health Protection (Coronavirus Restrictions) (No. 2) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2020.
answer_text: The High Court decision, on 29 May 2024, is a specific matter for the PSNI to consider. To better protect the population against various forms of infection and contamination including biological, chemical and radioactive in addition to infectious diseases, to enable us to respond to 21st century public health emergencies, my Department is currently reviewing our Health Protection legislative framework. I am seeking Executive colleagues’ agreement to publish a public consultation on the policy proposals underpinning a draft Bill, with a view to Introduction in late autumn of this year.Given the cross-cutting nature of the draft Bill my officials will continue to engage with stakeholders including policy areas of other Departments throughout the legislative process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to provide details on (i) the current cull of grey squirrels in the Stormont Estate, including the extent and cost of this cull; and (ii) the introduction of red squirrels to the Stormont Estate, including a timeframe and cost.
answer_text: In June 2021, Stormont Estate Management Unit commenced partnering with the North Down Red Squirrel and Pine Marten Group (NDRSPMG) with the aim of reintroducing red squirrels to the Stormont Estate. Work commenced in July 2021 to control the grey squirrel population and trapping continued until May 2023. To date, the cost to the Department is £993.36, which includes the purchase of cameras, batteries, traps and feed.	 	Since May 2023, NDRSPMG has experienced difficulty in sourcing volunteers and trapping has been paused.  Monitoring of the area using the feeding stations and cameras is continuing and when NDRSPMG has sufficient appropriate resource the project will resume.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many people are currently classified as homeless in North Antrim.
answer_text: This information is not available in the format you have requested.The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin July - December 2023 was published on 14 March 2024 and contains information on a range of areas relating to homelessness and is available here: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-july-december-2023 Please refer to Accompanying Table 2.3, in the above link which details households accepted as homeless by Local Government District.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people awaiting Personal Independence Payment appeals (i) on 30 June 2021; (ii) on 30 June 2022; (iii) on 30 June 2023; and (iv) currently in 2024, in the (a) the Ballymena area; and (b) the Ballymoney area.
answer_text: The table below shows the number of people awaiting Personal Independence Payment Appeals, as registered with The Appeals Service (NI) on the specified dates and by requested location. BallymenaBallymoneyTotal30 June 20212688835630 June 20221984023830 June 20231302915930 April 2024*12334157*2024 Current Figures can only be confirmed to this date.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for an update on the current status of electrical courses in the Northern Regional College; and (ii) why a number of campuses have decided to cancel courses that were due to commence in September 2024.
answer_text: Following engagement by my officials with Northern Regional College, the College has confirmed that (i) Northern Regional College is unable to offer a Year 1 intake for Electrical Installation courses for the 2024/25 academic year, and (ii) this is due to staffing and recruitment challenges. Subject to successful staff recruitment, Northern Regional College will open applications for a Year 1 intake in this subject area for the 2025/26 academic year. Current students on the programme, which is four years in duration, will continue to be taught as normal.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline what work is planned for the railway line between Coleraine and Belfast.
answer_text: I am pleased to report that as part of the 2024/25 capital budget for 2024/25 I plan to ring-fence funding for Phase 3 of the Coleraine to Derry project. This will enable this important project to progress with detailed design and preparation for procurement. It also demonstrates my intention to ensure public transport investment is across all of the North. In the longer-term, a programme is currently being developed to identify potential improvements in capacity / line speed enhancements between Belfast to Derry. The Strategic Outline Case is due to be submitted to my Department later this year.  My officials are also considering a business case from Translink to extend the existing Park and Ride facility at Ballymena Station. This work will be progressed in the coming period subject to the availability of funding.  A separate project is also in development for the enhancement of Ballymena Rail and Bus station. I also recently opened the new modern York Street Station which will provide access to Ulster University, Sailortown, Cathedral Quarter, City Quays, Cityside and other nearby communities.  It has the potential to be a catalyst for further investment in the area.I am keen that we maximise the potential of rail to support social and economic development; as well as encouraging modal shift, which reduces congestion and emissions and improves safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the total cost of the Covid Certification Scheme in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The HSC COVID Certification Service was introduced at the start of July 2021 to enable Northern Ireland citizens who wished to undertake International Travel, to satisfy the requirements of destination countries. Without the service citizens would have been unable to avoid restrictions, such as 10 days quarantine on arrival, imposed by other countries.The service closed to new applications on the 19th December 2023 when the last remaining countries withdrew their requirements for vaccination certification.Between July 2021 and December 2023, the COVID Certification Service in Northern Ireland cost a total of £21.5m.The funding covered, the creation and use of an NICS identity authentication service, creation and maintenance of an internationally trusted mobile app and paper certificates, andthe provision of a helpline to assist those experiencing difficulties with the automated process, provide assistance with obtaining secure paper certificates, and provide further guidance.Had the service not been available, patients would have been inundating GPs and vaccination centres with requests for documentation to undertake international travel.In November 2021 the Executive also agreed restrictions on access to a range of venues. Northern Ireland citizens were able to use the CovidCert App to meet criteria for access. The creation and maintenance of the service helped allow continued access to a range of businesses during the pandemic.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide an update on the condition of the sports facilities at Ballymoney High School.
answer_text: I am not currently aware of the condition of sports facilities at Ballymoney High School.  I have asked the Education Authority as managing authority for the school, to urgently inspect the sports facilities and provide me with a report of their condition.  Once I have considered this report I will write to the member to provide an update in respect of the condition of sports facilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to reinstate the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive, or introduce a similar scheme, in order to reduce waiting times in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Following the success of the scheme I introduced in June 2021, I had hoped to introduce a new reimbursement scheme this year. I included a bid for funding for a new replacement, which could have treated over 4,000 patients during this year’s budget setting process. Unfortunately, under the 2024/25 Budget recently approved by the Executive, precisely zero additional funding has been provided for tackling our waiting lists.Unfortunately, instead of now being able to invest in such waiting list initiatives, my Department’s budget has been cut by over 2%. This is a deeply regrettable and concerning position.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the full cost of the vaccine certification scheme in Northern Ireland.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) when the independent sector will have full access to NHS patient records on Business Services Organisation maintained systems; and (ii) what issues are being considered before granting this access.
answer_text: Northern Ireland citizens have high expectation of the measures taken to protect their personal, sensitive health and social care data.My Chief Digital Information Officer contacted key Independent Sector providers in early 2023 regarding the potential for them to contribute to the Acute and Community Care electronic patient record. The providers were invited to participate in discussions on the professional, clinical, information governance and cyber security requirements that must be met before access could be considered. Whilst these matters are being tackled citizens will shortly be able to provide “read only” access to their information to non-HSC providers at their discretion, using the facilities in the regional electronic patient record.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the provisions in place to provide specialist hearing therapy in schools; (ii) the training that is in place to assist teachers and staff when educating pupils with hearing problems; (iii) any proposals or plans in place to enhance this therapy and training; and (iv) the definition of a Teacher of the Deaf.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s (EA) Sensory Service provides support to children and young people with sensory impairment, including those who are deaf.All schools where deaf children and young people attend are offered training by the service in deaf awareness, as well as bespoke training which is relevant to the children and young people who attend the school.Deaf-Friendly Guidance to assist teachers and staff who work with deaf children in educational settings has been produced by my department in partnership with the National Deaf Children’s Society and the Sensory Service.Teachers of Deaf (ToDs) children and young people are required to continue their professional development and the Sensory Service provides professional development which aims to enhance outcomes for deaf children and young people.  A TOD is a qualified teacher who has completed a mandatory qualification to work with deaf children and young people.  DE Circular 2007/22 - Qualifications of Teachers in Nursery, Primary, Secondary and Special Schools provides a list of approved courses for teaching pupils with hearing impairment.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when will they meet with the Vaccine Injured and Bereaved Support Group Northern Ireland and other people with similar health concerns.
answer_text: We plan to meet with the Vaccine Injured and Bereaved Support Group Northern Ireland when the diary allows.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he intends to visit agricultural shows in Ballymoney and Ballymena.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of findings that deaf children have a 70 per cent higher risk of requiring sensory integration interventions, how his Department proposes to meet this provision.
answer_text: Children and young people (CYP) who are deaf may experience sensory integration difficulties, however research to support exact percentages is limited.The Education Authority (EA) Sensory Service provides family centred early intervention as soon as the child is identified as deaf or hard of hearing, which capitalises on early sensitive periods in development to prevent or minimise developmental delays. This helps to optimise the child’s outcomes, including sensory integration and executive functioning.The EA’s Sensory Service work to ensure that parents and carers are informed of the range of interventions to help them make decisions around the best way for their child to be supported.EA Teachers of the Deaf (ToD) encourage and model for parents/carers in the early years the behaviours required for executive functioning and independent learning, including the influence of play and healthy emotional development, bonding and healthy family relationships, and family members’ acquisition of knowledge and skills to nurture the child’s development.  ToDs also monitor developmental milestones and if there is a concern, support from other professionals can be identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the steps being taken to ensure that pupils with hearing problems are not grouped together with other pupils who suffer from ASD or ADHD.
answer_text: The needs of children and young people with special educational needs (SEN) are assessed individually and their school placement and support provided is determined based on this assessment.  A diagnosis of hearing impairment, Autism/Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD), Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) or any other medical diagnosis does not necessarily lead to a SEN, as each individual will have different experiences and abilities and the provision being made by the school or setting should be appropriate to the child’s learning needs.83% of children and young people who are hearing impaired are being educated in mainstream classes and are, therefore, part of an inclusive environment.  It would not be appropriate to segregate them or those with other conditions as a direct result of their medical diagnosis or disability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the contact that schools and Teachers of the Deaf have with (i) Health and Social Care Trusts; (ii) GPs; and (iii) other health specialists in relation to assessing the needs of pupils with hearing problems and their education.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s (EA) Sensory Service Teachers of Deaf Children and Young People (ToDs CYP) work with all five Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs).Referrals to the EA’s Sensory Service are usually made through the Newborn Hearing Screening Programme or a health professional.  ToDs and Educational Audiologists have direct contact with Audiology Departments and the Regional Auditory Implant Centre and work alongside Speech and Language Therapists for consultation, review meetings and joint support and advice sessions.Where parental consent has been granted, information is routinely shared between the Sensory Service and HSCTs, and if required with GPs, Speech and Language Therapists, Occupational Therapists and Sensory Service Social Workers.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have met with the Vaccine Injured and Bereaved Support Group Northern Ireland, or others injured or bereaved due to the Covid-19 vaccine, as committed to by the First Minister in the Assembly chamber on 19 February 2024.
answer_text: We plan to meet with the Vaccine Injured and Bereaved Support Group Northern Ireland when diaries permit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the number of children registered as deaf or hard of hearing; and (ii) how many of them have been assigned a fully trained and specialist hearing therapist.
answer_text: The information is not held centrally in my department and has therefore been requested from the Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts. Please find information on the number of children registered as deaf or hard of hearing detailed below. For the Belfast and Southern HSC Trusts these are children who meet the criteria for permanent childhood hearing loss (PCHI), for Northern HSC Trust these are the number of children currently active with Northern HSC Trust who wear hearing aids for any reason, for South Eastern HSC Trust these are Paediatric hearing aid users registered as hard of hearing or deaf, and for Western HSC Trust these are children with some degree of hearing loss under ongoing surveillance/management within Western HSC Trust.Table 1. The number of children registered as deaf or hard of hearing as at 30.04.2024, by HSC TrustTrustChildrenBelfast280Northern227South Eastern153Southern*27Western247Northern Ireland934Source: HSC Trusts*Southern HSC Trust advised that this number reflects children who met the criteria for permanent childhood hearing loss from the introduction of S4H in March 2021 to the 30th April 2024.The HSC Trusts advised that there are no specialist Hearing Therapists in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the (a) financial; and (b) practical support provided by his Department to deaf children and their families; and (ii) what plans he has to enhance this support.
answer_text: (i) Across Northern Ireland, support for children with disability (inclusive of deaf children & their families) is delivered by the five Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts in partnership with the community and voluntary sector in accordance with a child’s assessed need. There has been significant additional funding provided generally for services for Children with a Disability in recent years. Since approximately 2020, and including planned additional funding for 2023/24, there will be circa £7.4m specific recurrent funding provided for services and supports for Children with a Disability, which includes over £1m specifically in relation to respite (short breaks) and family support enhancement services.Support provision for deaf children and their families may be provided via community support services / activity programmes within the HSC Trust area, or via respite provision that may include overnight care in a short break residential unit or ‘Sharing the Care’ type respite services which are community based (e.g. fostering support, befriending support services, community activities such as afterschool support / youth group activities / summer schemes). The support / respite service may be provided to children with disability (inclusive of deaf children and their families) directly by the HSC Trust, by a community organisation who may have a contract with the HSC Trust to deliver the service, or via Direct Payments to the primary carer to enable support to be sourced.The Emotional Well-being of Deaf Children and Young People Draft Report (Byrne & McNamee 2022) made a strong case for a deaf specific CAMHS service to address the intersectionality between mental health support required by a deaf child because of their age, and the mental health support required because of deafness. A Regional Deaf CAMHS service received recurrent funding in 2023/24.Respite Services are also provided by the Northern Ireland Children’s Hospice for all children (inclusive of deaf children) with a life limiting condition.(ii) With regard to planning for the future, a Task and Finish group has been established by the Department to finalise the Adult Learning Disability Service Model and the Children with Disabilities Framework.  Further the Department has established a Children’s Services Reform Process to reform Children’s Services. This reform process includes Children with a Disability. One of the workstreams from this process will be looking specifically at the regional position in respect of children with disability, and this will include a focus on demand and capacity. The outcomes of this work will inform future commissioning.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given concerns that children who are deaf or hard of hearing are being directed down an ASD or ADHD pathway of care that may not be suitable for them, what measures he will take to (i) ascertain the extent of this problem; and (ii) effect positive change.
answer_text: It is recognised that some children with ASD can present as having a hearing loss when in fact they actually have normal hearing, but process sound differently. Additionally, some of the recognised causes of ADHD, such as prematurity, meningitis or anoxia, can also cause deafness.GPs and other health care providers should refer to ENT or Audiology to exclude hearing loss prior to referral for ASD/ADHD assessment.  In the majority of cases, the children have passed their Newborn Hearing Screen and parents have no concerns regarding their child’s hearing.On receipt of your question my officials contacted all HSC Trusts to enquire if any concerns had been raised with them that hearing-impaired children had been misdirected onto ASD or ADHD pathways.  If so how many in the past year and what steps they were taking to address this if it was an issue. HSC Trusts have advised that: Whilst some children with hearing impairment are referred for an ASD/ADHD assessment service, these typically are appropriate referrals. These referrals are triaged and accepted, or rejected, based on the clinical picture presented within the referral form.  At point of assessment, the assessing team take required steps to liaise with others involved in the child/ young person’s care (as in all cases) with regards to their hearing and often speech and language presentation. Clinicians would typically consult with the SLT hearing impairment lead to discuss differentials to ensure any outcome we give is safe and appropriate. All Trusts can access the regional Deaf CAMH Service to consult with for specific advice if required.All Trusts have advised that they are not aware of any complaints, concerns or patterns of inappropriate referrals for this population.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how his Department works with the Department of Education and schools to support deaf children and their families, both financially and practically; and (ii) how many fully-trained hearing specialist medical staff are involved in this work.
answer_text: (i) Across Northern Ireland, support for children with disability (inclusive of deaf children & their families) is delivered by the five Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts in partnership with the community and voluntary sector in accordance with a child’s assessed need. The Department recognises the many challenges that are faced by children who are deaf or hard of hearing. Support provision for deaf children and their families may be provided via community support services / activity programmes within the HSC Trust area, and may include fostering support, befriending support services, community activities such as afterschool support / youth group activities / summer schemes). Trusts will also use Direct Payments to the primary carer to enable support to be sourced by the family.Mental health services and support for deaf and hard of hearing children are available through Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) in each HSC Trust area. The Department has established a Children’s Services Reform Process to reform Children’s Services. One of the workstreams from this process will be looking specifically at the regional position in respect of children with disability, and this will include a focus on demand and capacity. The outcomes of this work will inform future commissioning.There are a number of standing groups between the two departments which have been established to improve cooperation and have children with disability as part of their purpose, for example, the Joint Health and Education Oversight Group.The Department also is involved with the Joint Education Authority Health and Social Care Special Schools meeting.(ii) With regard to the numbers of fully trained specialist medical staff involved – the Department would not at this information available

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what support is available for parents homeschooling their children.
answer_text: The responsibility for a child’s education rests with their parents and parents may choose to secure their child’s education either at school or outside of the formal school environment. The Education Authority (EA) has published guidelines on Elective Home Education (EHE) which provide comprehensive information for parents who are currently home educating or who are considering EHE for their child. Parents may also contact the EA EHE team for advice.The guidelines outline the roles and responsibilities of parents, the EA, schools and other partners in ensuring that a child receives a suitable education and provide examples of good practice in EHE learning. There is guidance for home educators on how to access support for children with special educational needs and in relation to matters such as safeguarding, examinations and careers guidance. Information is provided on educational resources and signposting to parents groups and organisations that can provide support or specialist advice for home educating families.  Further information on Elective Home Education can be found at:  Elective Home Education | Education Authority Northern Ireland (eani.org.uk) .

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many people have been deregistered from their dentist in (i) 2023; (ii) 2022; (iii) 2021; (iv) 2020; (v) 2019; and (vi) 2018.
answer_text: Table 1 overleaf shows the number of health service patients who were deregistered from active health service dentists in each year, from 2018 to 2023.A patient’s dental registration lasts 25 months from the date they were first accepted by the General Dental Practitioner (GDP) or the date the arrangement was last extended. With capacity within General Dental Services (GDS) significantly reduced throughout the pandemic, to prevent large numbers of patient registrations lapsing as a result of them being unable to obtain a dental appointment, the duration of patient registrations was extended between March 2020 and March 2023.With no patient registrations naturally lapsing, the number of de-registrations via the standard WCA 966 form has increased. Table 1: Number of Patient De-registrations from an active health service dentist, 2018 - 20231,2,3,4Year4Number of Patient De-registrations120185722019784202024320212,02120229,432202312,858Source: Family Practitioner Services Dental Payment System, Family Practitioner Services, HSC Business Services Organisation.1 To de-register a patient, a dentist must submit a WCA 966 form to the BSO and give the patient 3 months’ notice. The count of de-registrations is based on the number of WCA 966 forms submitted and recorded on the FPS Dental Payment System. The variable used to signify the submissions of a WCA 966 form may also be used in other scenarios e.g. removing a registration from a dentist that should have been seen on referral rather than an initial or re-registration. It is not possible to specifically extract WCA 966 forms submitted, thus resulting in a possible overcount of true de-registrations.2 An active dentist has made an arrangement with the SPPG (previously the Health and Social Care Board) to provide health service dentistry.3 Private patients who have been de-registered are not included in this data. 4 All count figures represent de-registrations across each full calendar year (1st January to 31st December). The data presented is as of 24th April 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of visitors to Dark Tourism sites in Northern Ireland in the last ten years, broken down by age profile.
answer_text: My Department and Tourism NI are unaware of a definition of Dark Tourism in a local context and therefore are not able to provide a response.  Survey data on visits to visitor attractions is published by NISRA. The most recent results are available at Visitor Attraction Survey 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what targets he has set regarding native tree planting for the next three years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what recourse a patient has when a specialist recommends a medicine or drug for their condition, but their GP refuses to prescribe it.
answer_text: Shared care involves the transfer of clinical responsibility from a hospital or specialist service to general practice, in which prescribing by the primary care prescriber is supported by a Shared Care Agreement.  A specialist should advise the General Practitioner (GP) on which medicines to prescribe, what monitoring will need to take place within primary care, how often medicines should be reviewed, and what actions should be taken in the event of difficulties, including single point of contact details. Arrangements for prescribing across the primary care and secondary care interface is set out in - https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hss-md-26-2022.pdf which includes information on Shared Care arrangements.All prescribers must adhere to the relevant standards including the General Medical Council guidance document published 5 April 2021, which outlines key principles in support of effective shared care arrangements that prescribers should observe, including: to act in the best interests of the patient;acknowledgement of the medico-legal responsibilities of the prescriber;the requirement for the agreement of all parties and that effective communication is essential;that the prescriber should have sufficient knowledge, experience and information;that the specialist should provide the GP or other primary care prescriber and the patient with sufficient information to permit the safe management of the condition; andthat if the GP or other primary care prescriber is not satisfied to take prescribing responsibility this should be explained to the specialist and patient and alternative arrangements provided.Clinical and legal responsibility for prescribing lies with the prescriber who signs the prescription and it is the responsibility of this individual to prescribe within their competence and scope of practice, and so must be prepared to explain and justify their decisions and actions. This is in line with General Medical Council guidance on prescribing https://www.gmc-uk.org/professional-standards/professional-standards-for-doctors/good-practice-in-prescribing-and-managing-medicines-and-devicesIf a GP cannot or will not prescribe a medicine at the recommendation of a specialist, then the GP should discuss their concerns with the specialist in order to either address their concerns or to make alternative arrangements for supply to meet the needs of the patient.Where a patient or their carer/guardian is dissatisfied with the treatment received, they have a right to make a complaint to the GP practice in line with the HSC’s Complaints Procedure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what treatments are currently available in Northern Ireland for people suffering from long COVID.
answer_text: Expert clinical guidance from the National Institute of Health and Care Excellence (NICE) on managing the long-term effects of COVID-19 indicates that there is currently a lack of evidence for pharmaceutical interventions for the treatment of long COVID, although established treatments for symptomatic relief can be considered in line with current national and local guidance. The current service in Northern Ireland is focussed on holistic assessment, exclusion of other significant conditions, identifying patients with breathlessness who may benefit from pulmonary rehabilitation and those who need onward referral to secondary or community services.  This is complemented by signposting to self-management resources.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to provide an update on replacing constituency office printers.
To ask the Minister of Education, in light of the news in relation to Fresh Start funding, how Slemish College, Ballymena, will achieve the proposed new capital build.
answer_text: I have placed the 10 remaining former Fresh Start projects that are not yet in contract into my Department’s conventional major works programme to continue to advance in design and planning.  The projects will proceed to construction when planning is complete and affordable within the wider education budget context.All of the projects have been submitted as priority capital bids for 2024-25 to the Department of Finance and I trust all Executive parties will support this bid for capital funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how the Energy Act 2023 impacts on (i) his Department; (ii) SONI; (iii) the Northern Ireland Utility Regulator; and (iv) households.
answer_text: The Energy Act 2023, brought forward by the Department of Energy, Security and Net Zero (DESNZ), received royal assent on 26 October 2023. Some parts of the Act extend to devolved administrations.During the absence of the Assembly, officials liaised with DESNZ to understand those sections of interest to my Department. These include provisions around the licensing of carbon dioxide transport and storage, and sections that detail arrangements for carbon capture, storage, and hydrogen production and transportation.  The Act also includes details around low-carbon heat schemes and heat networks, and the Energy Savings Opportunity Scheme.  Provisions extend the role of the existing electricity and market regulators to cover heat networks. The Utility Regulator would regulate here. The detail of the legislation will be set out in regulations which will be laid by DfE. The impact of the Energy Act (2023) on the NIAUR will be determined by implementation of energy policy by the NI Assembly. SONI is the independent Electricity Transmission System Operator for Northern Ireland and is regulated by the Utility Regulator.  Any impacts on the organisation are a matter for SONI to comment on. Officials continue to explore the local implications.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will publish the results of the public consultation on Duty of Candour and Being Open.
answer_text: Responses to the public consultation were published on the Department of Health’s website and can be found here:Duty of Candour & Being Open – Public Consultation – Organisational Responses | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk)Duty of Candour & Being Open – Public Consultation – Individual Responses | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk).Following on from this consultation, my officials are currently developing proposals for a regional Being Open Framework with plans for further consultation in the months ahead. This is a key contributing piece of work to help further support an open, just and learning culture in our HSC.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what barriers, legal or otherwise, are in place due to the UK Covid-19 Inquiry that prevents him from meeting with (i) Covid-19 sufferers; (ii) people who have recovered from Covid-19; (iii) people who have been left disabled from Covid-19; and (iv) people who have been left disabled or been injured from Covid-19 vaccines.
answer_text: With respect to the UK Covid-19 Inquiry, I must ensure I adhere to the undertaking of confidentiality I have made to the Inquiry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline what support he can provide to the three watersports centres in North Antrim along the River Bann impacted by the blue-green algae bloom.
answer_text: My Department is leading in the production of an Inter-Agency Monitoring Protocol to address blue-green algae issues.  This will provide guidance for water sports operators to consider blue-green algae risks in recreational waters.  My Department will also continue to share water quality data to assist water sports operators with their considerations.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether he will bring forward stand-alone legislation regarding the provisions relating to live links for courts and tribunals in Northern Ireland that are currently contained in the Coronavirus Act 2020, Section 90(2).
answer_text: In 2022 the Department invited views on proposals for “Audio and Video Links (Live Links) for Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals” as part of the wider digital transformation of public services and improving access to justice. We are now planing to bring forward legislative provisions reflecting the outworking of the responses to that consultation during this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will bring forward stand-alone legislation to give effect to the modifications of requirements regarding medical certificates for cremations in Northern Ireland that are currently contained in the Coronavirus Act 2020, Section 90(2).
answer_text: My officials are currently progressing work on revised Cremation Regulations for my consideration.  The proposed Regulations will update the Cremation (Belfast) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1961 and apply them to all council crematoria.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will bring forward stand-alone legislation to give effect to the provision relating to local authority meetings in Northern Ireland that are currently contained in the Coronavirus Act 2020, Section 90(2).
answer_text: The Local Government (Meetings and Performance) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 provides the Department with a power to make regulations to provide district councils with the flexibility to hold meetings remotely or by hybrid means.  My officials are currently progressing work on draft regulations for my consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he will bring forward stand-alone legislation to give effect to the provision of registration of deaths and still-births in Northern Ireland that are currently contained in the Coronavirus Act 2020, Section 90(2).
answer_text: "The temporary powers for the provision of registration of deaths and still-births contained in the Coronavirus Act 2020, in the absence of a Finance Minister, have been approved by Department of Finance officials and work is in progress to extend these until September 2024. Exploratory work which could enable this provision to be made permanent has commenced."

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what assurances were received by the Department of Health that the Emerge Concert on the 17th September 2021 was a health intervention and that there was rationale for selecting this event over other events.
answer_text: The Minister of Health assessed that the concert could contribute to an increase in the uptake of the Covid-19 vaccine.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given the numbers of elderly patients waiting more than 12 hours for a bed, what investment has been put in place to deliver a measurable improvement in medically fit for discharge patients out of hospital to facilitate improved patient flow.
answer_text: Unfortunately due to there being no Minister in post after midnight on the 27th of October, my Department will be unable to answer the question.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, with regard to the consultation on Publication of Annual Payments to Suppliers of Legal Aid, can she provide (i) an update on this matter, given recent media coverage on matters of legal aid; and (ii) an indication of when this policy or a similar policy may be implemented.
answer_text: Consistent with the briefing provided to the Justice Committee on the responses to the consultation exercise undertaken in 2020/21, it remains my intention to publish information on annual payments to suppliers of legal aid.  Following my direction, officials in LSANI are exploring how best to facilitate this process.Current intentions are to release information spanning the years 2015/16 to 2018/19 in the same format as that adopted up to and including 2014/15.  This will include the names and total annual payments made to the Top 100 solicitor firms and barristers.  This information is in the process of being reconciled with a view to triggering the process for publication before year end, in March 2023. As this information includes the publication of personal data there are a range of steps to be taken and the variable responses to these steps will dictate the timescale of publication. As such it is not possible at this time to say when the information will be sanctioned for public release.A separate approach is needed to compile and reconcile equivalent data covering the 2019/20 year.  This year has an extra layer of complexity as a new legal aid case management system (LAMS) was introduced in July 2019, which has resulted in data for 2019/20 spanning multiple systems.  A methodology has to be finalised as to how these data can best be matched, compiled, disentangled and de-duplicated.  It is envisaged that the process to trigger release of 2019/20 will commence by September 2023.  As before, it is not possible to determine when this process will conclude to enable public release of the information which will be in the same format as previous years.Once publications of the years 2015/16 to 2019/20 have completed, the cycle for 2020/21 will commence.  As the information from 2020/21 onwards will be based solely on the new LAMS case management system, this will require a further methodological iteration to be in place to produce more granular outputs indicating overall earnings by area of legal aid expenditure.  Supplementary information comprising banded summary information for all suppliers below the top 100 will be produced.  Similar to previous tranches, it is envisaged that the notification process will commence by March 2024 with the date for public release of the information not being known at this time.    Following publication of 2021/22 information, it is my intention to launch a further consultation seeking to move from the top 100 to a financial trigger for publication in future years.  This consultation will also provide an important opportunity to review other aspects of the publication process in light of the revised 2021/22 format.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following the claims in a recent article in the Telegraph that children in England who died before they left hospital were wrongly described as stillborn, (i) what measures are in place in Northern Ireland to ensure that this does not happen here; and (ii) what are the procedures here in Northern Ireland for stillbirth death as compared to children who die before leaving hospital.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, a stillbirth certificate is legally required in respect of a child which has been completely expelled or extracted from its mother after the twenty-fourth week of pregnancy and which did not at any time after expulsion or extraction breathe or show any other evidence of life. Medical Practitioners or Midwives determine whether a child has been stillborn and have a legal and professional duty to complete a stillborn certificate stating the cause to the best of their knowledge and belief.Stillbirths must be reported to the Coroner in Northern Ireland.  When a baby is born and shows signs of life, even for only a short time, it has been born alive. In those cases a doctor must issue a Medical Certificate of Cause of Death (MCCD) and both the birth and death must be registered with the GRO.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what was the job title of the official, that was provided to the Department for Communities on 1 November 2021, who was the contact point for the business case for the Emerge event; and (ii) what was their role in developing the business case for the event.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether his Department provides funding for events or organisations, which then donates money to charity from the proceeds of that event; and (ii) what governance arrangements are in place to ensure that this funding meets requirements within law, provides transparency and ensures good governance.
answer_text: Unfortunately due to there being no Minister in post after midnight on the 27th of October, my Department will be unable to answer the question.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, further to recent media reports regarding the suspension of a barrister, (i) what action the Legal Services Agency has taken; and (ii) whether this matter will be referred to the Public Prosecution Services as part of the Agency’s policy.
answer_text: In respect of recent media reports I can confirm that the Legal Services Agency followed due process:As the Legal Services Agency has a zero tolerance to fraud and investigates all cases involving an allegation or suspicion of fraud, when the Agency reviewed the claims by this practitioner it withheld payment;conducted an internal investigation;reported the matter to the PSNI;responded to all requests for information and clarification from PSNI and PPS; andwhen the PPS directed that there should be no prosecution, the Agency submitted a formal complaint to the Bar and provided a witness statement for the Bar’s disciplinary process.The Agency followed its policy by referring these claims to the PSNI before raising a formal complaint with the Bar.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will (i) carry out a review of the Northern Ireland legal aid system, including the role of the Taxing Master; and (ii) adopt a zero tolerance approach for both fraud and attempted fraud.
answer_text: (i) Departmental officials are developing a methodology for a review of criminal and civil legal aid to deliver against the Department’s statutory obligations to review legal aid remuneration that will look at issues including transparent and accountable systems for providing fair remuneration to providers of legal services, and ensuring value for public money.  Detailed plans will be brought to the Assembly in due course.The Taxing Master is a member of the Judiciary and has both a payment assessment role and an appellant role in respect to some Legal Aid cases.  It is not the intention of the Department to review the appellant role of the Taxing Master.The Department has commenced work to review the payment assessment role of the Taxing Master. A public consultation “Legal Aid Taxation Reform and Statutory Charge Registration” was published on 16 March 2022 and included proposed legislative amendments to the Judicature (Northern Ireland) Act 1978 as amended. There were two responses to the consultation, one from a member of the public and one from the Law Society. The proposed amendments are the first in a series of primary and secondary legislative changes needed to move the payment assessment function into the Department.  In support of this, the Department plan for the proposed amendment to the Judicature (Northern Ireland) Act 1978 to be laid before the Assembly in the second year of the current mandate.  This is subject to the formation of an Executive and the wishes of the new Justice Minister. While there is no functioning Assembly, there is no legislative vehicle available to bring about the necessary changes required to amend the role of the Taxing Master.(ii) The Legal Services Agency has a zero tolerance to fraud and attempted fraud. The Agency investigates all cases involving an allegation or suspicion of fraud and attempted fraud. If the Agency’s investigation substantiates an allegation or suspicion of fraud, or attempted fraud, the Agency will refer the matter to the Police Service of Northern Ireland. The Agency will also notify the appropriate regulatory body where a referral is made to the Police. It is a matter for the Public Prosecution Service whether a practitioner is prosecuted. This approach is set out in the Agency’s Internal Fraud Policy which is publicly available. The Agency also promotes an anti-fraud culture, with a focus to prevent, detect and investigate fraud which includes working with other Agencies and law enforcement and regulatory bodies. This work is led by a dedicated Counter Fraud Unit with accredited fraud investigators. The Agency has delivered fraud awareness training to its staff, has a e-learning package for new staff and has prescribed annual mandatory fraud awareness training for all staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline why her Department has not published a Fraud Policy.
answer_text: My Department has published the DoJ ‘Raising a Concern’ Policy. This specifically states that if the concern is about possible fraud the Department will deal with it under our Fraud Policy and Fraud Response Plan. A link to the DoJ Anti-Fraud and Anti-Bribery Policy and Response Plan is provided within the Raising a Concern Policy.In addition, the Legal Services Agency has published its Internal Fraud Referral Policy and Instructions which sets out how it investigates and refers potential or suspected fraud to the appropriate authorities. The Agency offers members of the public three routes to report suspected fraud:A counter-fraud hotline – 08003287360An email address - counterfraud@lsani.gov.ukthe NIdirect website online legal aid fraud reporting facility.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will publish a list of legal practitioners, both individuals and firms, who have been sanctioned or prosecuted for legal aid fraud over the last five years.
answer_text: The Legal Services Agency has a zero tolerance to fraud and investigates all cases involving an allegation or suspicion of fraud. If the Agency’s investigation substantiates an allegation or suspicion of fraud, the Agency will refer the matter to the Police Service of Northern Ireland. The Agency will also notify the appropriate regulatory body where a referral is made to the Police. It is a matter for the Public Prosecution Service whether a practitioner is prosecuted. In the last 5 years no practitioners have been sanctioned for legal aid fraud. One individual was prosecuted and acquitted of legal aid fraud.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline (i) the number of cases where legal aid fraud (a) has been discovered by legal practitioners; (b) has been referred by the Legal Services Agency for prosecution; and (c) has resulted in prosecutions; and (ii) to provide details of the cases which have resulted in prosecutions, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Legal Services Agency has a zero tolerance to fraud and investigates all cases involving an allegation or suspicion of fraud. If the Agency’s investigation substantiates an allegation or suspicion of fraud, the Agency will refer the matter to the Police Service of Northern Ireland. The Agency will also notify the appropriate regulatory body where a referral is made to the Police. It is a matter for the Public Prosecution Service whether a practitioner is prosecuted. In the last 5 years:(i)(a) three cases of suspected fraud involving legal practitioners have been identified by the Agency;(i)(b)	the Agency referred these three cases to the PSNI, two of which resulted in files being submitted to the PPS.  The third case is still being investigated by PSNI;(i)(c)	the two cases referred to the PPS did not result in a prosecution. In addition, one case which the Agency referred to the PSNI prior to the 5-year time period set out in the question, was prosecuted through the courts during the last five years. (ii)	the case in which a practitioner was prosecuted involved 8 counts of false accounting. The practitioner was acquitted. Following the practitioner’s acquittal, the case was referred to the Law Society. Outcome of this referral is awaited.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, further to the consultation on the Review of the Northern Ireland Postgraduate Tuition Fee Loan, when he will publish (i) his response; and (ii) any changes to the current policy on postgraduate support for students at private providers in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department undertook a public consultation on the Northern Ireland Postgraduate Tuition Fee Loan which explored both the level of funding available for tuition fee support and also the potential for different maintenance support systems.My Department is currently considering the responses to the consultation along with policy options, and I hope to publish a formal consultation response in the coming weeks. At that point I will be able to advise of any possible changes to postgraduate policy and funding, including for private providers. Any outcomes, however, particularly in relation to the levels of financial support available, will be dependent upon the funding that is available to the Department over the coming years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to outline whether all facemask legislation implemented in Northern Ireland applied only to public transport, or were other settings and environments involved; and (ii) to list the other settings and environments that facemask legislation was implemented.
answer_text: The Health Protection (Coronavirus, Wearing of Face Coverings) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2020 came into operation on 23 July 2020, and were revoked on 15 February 2022. When introduced on 23 July 2020, the Regulations only applied to public transport. On 10 August 2020 the requirement to wear a face covering was extended to apply to a ‘relevant place’, which covered a number of settings set out in the Schedule to the Regulations, which were in addition to the public transport requirement.The Schedule outlining the settings which were considered within the definition of a ‘relevant place’ was amended at a number of stages in the time the Regulations were in force. The link below is to the Schedule as it applied when made on 10 August. Later changes to this definition can be viewed by using the slide bar at the top of the page. This will show which settings the requirement to wear a face covering applied to at particular points in time.https://www.legislation.gov.uk/nisr/2020/151/schedule/2020-08-10

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 28662/17-22 and AQW 4363/22-27, why the consultation on mandatory covid vaccines for Healthcare staff is not referenced or listed on the departmental website.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 419/17-22 and AQW 3272/22-27, whether the sale of alcohol at the Emerge event is consistent and compatible with the awarding of a £75000 grant of Health Promotion Funding designed to promote health.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 419/17-22 and AQW 3272/22-27, (i) what evaluations have been conducted on the Emerge Concert on 17 September 2022; (ii) how did the Department measure vaccine uptake among young people as a result of the event and what was the outcome; (iii) how many Department of Health officials were present at the event and the other sites that were distributing tickets for the event; and (iv) what band or job title did these officials have.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 419/17-22 and AQW 3265/22-27, (i) why could unvaccinated young people not obtain or avail of tickets to the Emerge concert; and (ii) what assessments have been conducted to consider the impacts on those excluded young people who contributed greatly during the Covid 19 pandemic.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 419/17-22 and AQW 3266/22-27, (i) what audits and checks have been carried out by (a) his Department; (b) the Department of Finance; and (c) the Comptroller and Auditor General, on the value and spend of public monies for the Emerge event on 17 September 2021; and (ii) why no other companies or organisations were offered the opportunity to apply to stage this event or to apply for this funding.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 419/17-22 and AQW 3265/22-27, how did the Emerge concert raise awareness of the importance of vaccination other than promoting that young people who were unvaccinated would be excluded from attending the concert.
To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 28662/17-22, why the consultation on mandatory Covid vaccines for Healthcare staff is still not published on the Departmental website.
answer_text: There are no immediate plans to bring forward a document for publication.The Department wishes to keep the matter under review, taking into account the decision to revoke the requirement in England and the serious staffing pressures right across the health and social care sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given Florida Health Officer and Surgeon General’s analysis on Covid 19 vaccines and his findings that mRNA vaccines present an increased risk of cardiac-related death among men aged 18-39, what procedures exist within the Dept of Health to ensure this information is assessed to ensure the public health and safety of the population of Northern Ireland.
answer_text: COVID-19 vaccines are only approved for use in NI, following rigorous clinical trials and extensive analysis of the vaccine’s safety, quality and effectiveness by experts from the independent Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) and the European Medicines Agency (EMA). When any health medicine is authorised and then used in the UK information is continuously collected to monitor the real life experience with the product. This information is monitored to make sure the benefits of the medicine or healthcare product continue to outweigh any risks.In the context of the millions of doses of COVID-19 vaccine being administered since December 2020 reports of suspected Myocarditis and Pericarditis are very rare, and the events reported are typically mild with individuals usually recovering within a short time with standard treatment and rest.The MHRA has undertaken a thorough review of both UK and international reports of suspected Myocarditis and Pericarditis following vaccination against COVID-19 and remain assured that the benefit of COVID-19 vaccination outweighs any risks for most individuals.I will continue to be guided by the expert advice provided by MHRA and the Joint Committee of Vaccination and Immunisation (JCVI) with regards vaccination issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to explain the rationale and policy objective behind the single payment discount on rates bills.
answer_text: A single payment discount, more commonly known as the early payment discount, has been in place since the beginning of the present rating system in the 1970s.  It aims to encourage ratepayers to pay their rates in full when they fall due at the start of the rating year.The discount was reviewed in detail in 2007 during the Executive Review of Rating, conducted following the restoration of the devolved institutions.  The subsequent Outcomes Report published by the Finance Minister at that time outlined the Executive’s rationale for keeping the discount.  That was that those availing of the discount were predominantly ratepayers of pension age who used it as a household budgeting measure.  It was considered that the retention of the policy complemented the other support measures introduced into the rating system for pensioners such as Lone Pensioner Allowance and Low Income Rate Relief.  The removal or retargeting of the measure was consulted upon at the end of 2016 as part of then Minister’s Rates Rethink package, a process that could not conclude due to the subsequent absence of an Executive between January 2017 and January 2020.The viability of the discount remains under review by my Department.  The advancement of any proposals to remove or retarget the measure would require new Primary Legislation in the Assembly as well as a public consultation exercise.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 153/22-27, AQW 160/22-27, and AQW 1116/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health why numbers presenting to Emergency departments have risen in the last four months.
answer_text: Urgent and emergency care services have been under significant and increasing pressure due to a number of contributing factors, some but not all of them linked to COVID-19. ED attendances across all hospitals in the last four months (May to August 2022) are up by 2% when compared to the same four month period last year; however, when compared to pre-pandemic levels, attendances are 9% lower.  When comparing the full 2021/22 financial year to the pre-pandemic levels of the 2019/20 financial year, there were 90,000 fewer attendances across all sites.The impact of ‘No More Silos’ initiatives such as Phone First, Urgent Care Centres, Direct Access Pathways and Hospital at Home is helping to redirect some of the pressures away from Emergency Departments.The public consultation setting out the future vision for Urgent and Emergency Care Services in NI closed in July.  The proposed reforms will help to ensure that all citizens in Northern Ireland have equal access to safe urgent and emergency care services.  Stakeholder feedback is currently being considered and analysed by my officials, with a view to publishing a response in the near future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) the reasons behind the delay in sending out cervical screening results once the test has been carried out; (ii) what the wait time is in each Trust area; (iii) what is being done to reduce this delay; and (iv) what this delay could mean for any further follow up procedures that are required.
answer_text: (i) The primary reason for increased waiting times is as a direct result of a national shortage of qualified pathologists. The RCPath workforce survey in 2018 found that only 3% of histopathology departments said they had enough staff to meet clinical demand, and this demand continues to grow. A shortage in consultant grade pathologists has also been identified nationally as a serious concern in maintaining and increasing cancer diagnostic services. Many of these concerns are due to long standing problems with workforce planning. We are at the beginning of a deeper crisis precipitated by retirements, with the loss of experienced staff worsened by punitive pension tax rules. In addition to a rising number of pathology requests, the complexity of work undertaken by pathologists is increasing. This is primarily because of the increasing level of detail and extra testing required to provide the best possible treatment for cancer patients. The number of patients with a pathological diagnosis of cancer was down during the pandemic, therefore there is catch up with regards to diagnosing cancers. Laboratories prioritise patient samples with suspected cancer. These samples require considerable time and extra work in laboratories. Increased numbers of patient samples with suspected cancer has a knock on effect delaying reports for patients who have underwent routine procedures. Since the COVID pandemic reduced routine outpatient appointments, Trusts have been trying to catch up with extra clinical sessions to reduce waiting times. This leads to extra work for the laboratories which then causes backlogs in processing and reporting of biopsies taken. As Belfast Trust is the regional centre for specialist areas (e.g. lymphomas, neurosciences), this effect is further compounded, as this workload is directed to a limited number of consultant histopathologists. There has been an increase in cervical smear samples throughout Northern Ireland causing a backlog in all laboratories. The rest of the UK has implemented primary HPV screening for smears. This has led to reduced training in cervical cytology screening and loss of senior screening staff due to retirements throughout the UK. Because Northern Ireland has still not implemented primary HPV testing, there is a reduced pool of trained Biomedical Scientist screeners but still the same number of smears.  Training for this role takes approximately 18 months.Laboratories have also been affected by staff absences due to COVID. The highly specialised nature of Biomedical Scientist jobs means it is often not possible to cover by engaging staff from elsewhere in the hospital. Staff are also drawn away from their clinical roles with other aspects important for the running of the health service in Northern Ireland such as procurement, introduction of new IT systems and maintaining laboratory accreditation. How the laboratory engages with the patient journey has also changed dramatically. Working with relevant colleagues

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 3344/22-27, to confirm that a five year old child born on the 31 August 2017 can receive a Covid 19 vaccine at any time in the future, and that a five year old born on or after the 1 September 2017 cannot receive a Covid 19 vaccine unless they have an underlying health condition or they live with someone who has a weakened immune system.
answer_text: That is correct, based on the current advice of the Joint Committee on Vaccination and Immunisation (JCVI).   The same position applies to children across the rest of the UK.The advice may of course change in the future as JCVI consider further evidence and therefore any parent seeking the latest position with regards COVID-19 vaccination should check the NI Direct website at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/get-covid-19-vaccination-northern-ireland
