question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what were the reasons for ending the vaccine programme for 5-11 year olds on that particular date in August; (ii) why was this date not announced or published; and (iii) what percentage of 5-11 year olds were vaccinated by that date.
answer_text: The offer to receive COVID-19 vaccinations for all children aged 5-11 years of age has not ended.  In line with the Joint Committee on Vaccination and Immunisation (JCVI) statement published in February 2022, this was a non-urgent offer to anyone aged 5-11 years of age.  However, the JCVI statement contained a footnote that stated the offer should include those children turning 5 years of age up to the end of August 2022.   Therefore in line with the JCVI advice, the cut-off point for inclusion in this offer of vaccination was the 31 August 2022.  The offer of vaccination for children aged 5-11 years of age (ie. those 5 year olds born on or before the 31 August 2017), remains open if they have not already taken it up, and their parents can continue to book a vaccination appointment via their local Trust.  Details of how they can do this are included on the NI Direct site at: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/get-covid-19-vaccination-northern-irelandChildren with an underlying health condition or those who live with someone who has a weakened immune system become eligible to receive their COVID-19 vaccinations, as they turn 5 years of age.All COVID-19 vaccination rates, including those for children aged 5-11 years of age, are publicly available on the COVID-19 Vaccinations Dashboard - NI Covid-19 Vaccinations Dashboard - COVID-19 (Coronavirus) Northern Ireland

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 419/22-27, (i) how the Emerge Concert on 17th September 2021 raised awareness of the importance of vaccination; and (ii) how did it acknowledge the impact of the pandemic on the younger generation.
answer_text: My position regarding the Emerge concert was set out in the press release issued on 5th September 2021. https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/news/ministers-welcome-ormeau-park-concert-initiative Tickets for the concert were free, acknowledging the contribution made by young people during our society’s collective response to COVID-19.The event received widespread publicity and complemented sustained promotional work to boost vaccine take-up among young people

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 1875/22-27, (i) why the Department's Register of Gifts and Hospitality has not been updated since 30 November 2018; and (ii) when will an updated register be published.
answer_text: Annual assurance on compliance with the gifts and hospitality policy has been provided by DoH Directorates throughout this period.  However, the collation and publication of this information at departmental level had been paused to redirect staff resources in the department initially to EU Exit work and more recently the pandemic response.  Work has commenced to collate this information and it is intended that the Department’s Register of Gifts and Hospitality will be published in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 419/22-27, (i) how many other organisations applied for Health Promotion funding; (ii) how many of these organisations were successful; (iii) how many of these organisations were unsuccessful; (iv) how was the monies and funding stream publicised and advertised; and (v) what was the application process.
answer_text: My position regarding the Emerge concert was set out in the press release issued on 5th September 2021. https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/news/ministers-welcome-ormeau-park-concert-initiative. This was a unique event, recognising the unique circumstances faced at that time. I remain extremely grateful for all the promotional work undertaken to boost vaccine take-up in the population.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) what measures are in place to meet the needs of deaf children in schools; and (ii) whether there are plans to increase the number of specialist teachers for deaf children.
answer_text: (i) The Education Authority (EA) Sensory Service provides support on a needs led basis. Any needs of individual deaf children are identified by Teachers of the Deaf and appropriate support is provided.  Support is determined using a nationally approved Eligibility Framework as well as taking into account the opinions of parents/carers, schools and the professional judgement of the Teacher of the Deaf.(ii) Work is ongoing within the EA to review both levels of staffing, and the need for additional specialist posts, with the recent appointment of two Educational Audiologists as part of the Sensory Service team which will further enhance the work of Qualified Teachers Of the Deaf (QTOD), improve links with health trusts and contribute to improved language outcomes for deaf children and young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 419/22-27, (i) how did the Emerge concert on 17th September 2021 use £75000 Health Promotion funding to promote health; (ii) how was this event assessed before and after the event; (iii) what scoring or points matrix was used to justify the allocation of public funding; and (iv) was alcohol sold at this event.
answer_text: My position regarding the Emerge concert was set out in the press release issued on 5th September 2021. https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/news/ministers-welcome-ormeau-park-concert-initiativeThe event complemented sustained promotional activities to boost vaccine take-up among young people. It would not be feasible to disaggregate the individual impact of each promotional activity. The question of alcohol sales at the concert would have been a matter for the promoter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 2758/22-27, when was dexamethasone first administered to patients for the treatment of COVID-19.
answer_text: Dexamethasone was one of a number of potential treatments for COVID-19 that was part of the RECOVERY trial which began in March 2020. Trial patients received dexamethasone 6mg once per day.A joint letter was issued from the UK Chief Medical Officers and Medical Director of NHS England with the initial results from the RECOVERY trial on 16 June. This included advice for immediate action from MHRA that clinicians should consider dexamethasone for the management of hospitalised patients with COVID-19 who required oxygen or ventilation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 2758/22-27, what evidence is contained in the Recovery study to support the claim that dexamethasone reduced deaths from COVID-19 by up to a third.
answer_text: In March 2020, The RECOVERY trial was established as a randomised clinical trial to test a range of potential treatments for COVID-19. Over 11,500 patients enrolled from over 175 NHS hospitals in the UK.A total of 6,335 patients were recruited to take part in the dexamethasone trial, 2014 of which received 6mg once per day with the remaining 4,321 received usual care alone. Dexamethasone reduced deaths by one-third in ventilated patients and by one fifth in other patients receiving oxygen only. Further details of the trial can be found at RECOVERY dexamethasone trial

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 2758/22-27, what specific injectable medicines were used alongside oxygen and the use of mechanical ventilation to treat COVID-19 patients in the very early stages between the first reported case in February 2020 and July 2020.
answer_text: There were no specific treatments approved for the treatment of COVID-19 during the early stage of the pandemic. Treatment prior to July 2020 was purely supportive and in the most severe cases may have involved admission to critical care settings for invasive mechanical ventilation as respiratory support. As part of the process of receiving invasive mechanical ventilation patients are typically administered a range of injectable medicines to relieve pain and anxiety and to facilitate intubation, as part of the standard management of a patient who had been admitted to intensive care for ventilation. Administration of such medicines are not specific to COVID-19 patients. The exact products used will have varied considerably over the time period based on product availability as at that time there was a high level of demand for these medicines worldwide and this led to considerable strain on medicines supply chains, in some cases necessitating the use of alternative products than would typically be used. It is not feasible to provide a fully comprehensive list of all potential injectable medicines that may have been used during this time period as it would depend on the judgement of individual critical care clinicians as to what was required for the management of their individual patient. The main classes of medicines used to facilitate mechanical ventilation and some commonly used examples are outlined below:Neuromuscular blocking agents which are used to temporarily paralyse skeletal muscle and facilitate mechanical ventilation such as:• Atracurium besilate 250mg/25ml, 50mg/5ml, 25mg/ 2.5ml solution for injection vials• Cisatracurium 150mg/30ml solution for injection vials• Rocuronium bromide 100mg/10ml solution for injection vials or ampoules• Suxamethonium chloride 100mg/2ml solution for injection pre-filled syringes or ampoulesSedatives which can be used to relax the patient and reduce stress, anxiety and agitation such as:• Propofol 1g/50ml, 200mg/20ml emulsion for infusion vials, pre-filled injections or ampoules• Midazolam 100mg/50ml solution for injection vialsAnalgesics which can be used to relieve pain such as:• Remifentanil 5mg or 2mg powder for solution for injection vials• Fentanyl 2.5mg/50ml, 500micrograms/10ml solution for injection vials or ampoules • Alfentanil 25mg/50ml, 5mg/10ml solution for injection ampoules• Morphine 100mg/50ml solution for injection pre-filled syringes or vials

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 2214/22-27, what medicines, treatments and therapies were used for early treatment COVID-19 patients between the first reported COVID-19 case in February 2020 and 9 December 2021.
answer_text: In the very early stages of the pandemic in 2020, there were no specific treatments available for COVID-19.  Treatment offered was supportive treatments i.e. they were intended to support the patient's physiological condition while their body fought infection. These treatments included oxygen and the use of mechanical ventilation, which involved the use of a range of injectable medicines used in critical care settings such as neuromuscular blocking agents and opioid analgesics.As time progressed and the findings of clinical trials from large scale studies (such as the RECOVERY trial) became available more specific treatments that were found to have evidence of benefit in the treatment of COVID-19 became available. For example the results from the RECOVERY study in June 2020 (HSS(MD) 47/2020 refers) showed that dexamethasone, a common and inexpensive steroid, reduced deaths from COVID-19 by up to a third.These therapeutic advances tended to be repurposing existing licensed medicines for the treatment of COVID-19.  New treatments were also developed, such as remdesivir which was rolled out in July 2020 as the first direct acting antiviral agent used in the treatment of COVID-19 (HSS(MD) 49/2020 refers). In November 2020 the Interleukin-6 (IL-6) inhibitor, tocilizumab, (Interim Position Statement refers) and then later in January 2021 both IL-6 inhibitors, tocilizumab and sarilumab, were made available as a treatment option for critically ill adult patients hospitalised with COVID-19 (Interim Position Statement and HSS(MD) 6/2021 refers).In September 2021, the combination neutralising monoclonal antibody (nMAB), casirivimab and imdevimab (Ronapreve), was included in a clinical policy to support the treatment of patients admitted to hospital for the management of symptoms of COVID-19 infection (HSS(MD) 65/2021 refers) with an additional nMAB sotrovimab becoming available later in December 2021 (HSS(MD)65/2021 addendum (no.2) refers).   Since the onset of the pandemic, the Department has published all of its Health and Social Care (HSC) communications (HSS MD Circulars) on treatments for COVID-19 on its communications page on the Department’s website.  Any directions regarding treatments, therapies and medicines for COVID-19 throughout the pandemic are available on that platform.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when will she direct that Personal Independence Payment assessments are conducted in person.
answer_text: My Department resumed face-to-face assessments for Personal Independence Payment on 1 July 2021, to address the needs of a small number of customers unable to take part in a telephone assessment during the pandemic.  Since the easement of Covid-19 restrictions earlier this year, my Department has been working with the Personal Independence Payment assessment provider, to increase the volume of face-to-face assessments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant of AQW 1875/22-27, whether departmental officials and Health and Social Care staff, including contracted workers, are required to declare remuneration or gifts from (i) businesses; or (ii) businesses connected to other businesses, that make or distribute vaccines or testing equipment.
answer_text: Departmental officials and Health and Social Care staff, including contracted workers, are required to declare remuneration or gifts from (i) businesses; or (ii) businesses connected to other businesses, that make or distribute vaccines or testing equipment. The relevant guidance issued by the then Department of Finance and Personnel (DFP), now the Department of Finance (DoF) can be accessed via the following link – DAO (DFP)10-06 - Acceptance and Provision of Gifts & Hospitality - Revised 3 September 2009 . As detailed in paragraph 6 of the attached, each of the Department’s Arm’s Length Bodies (ALBs) as individual employers will have their own policy on gifts and hospitality, but those policies must be compliant with the rules and principles set out in the guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, further to changes to the Voluntary Car Service mileage rate, whether (i) a cost benefit analysis on the changes has been undertaken; and (ii) work has been undertaken on how much it would cost Trusts if there were no volunteer drivers.
answer_text: Volunteer drivers are retained by individual Trusts under local agreement. My Department does not set any terms and conditions for rates of mileage allowances for voluntary drivers. I am not aware of any such work having been undertaken at a regional level.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why the COVID-19 in Northern Ireland: Vaccination Status of Deaths and Hospitalisations report has not been published since 23 February 2022.
answer_text: Initially the report ceased due to a transfer of ownership of the vaccination data between the HSCB to the PHA and the need to put in place updated data access agreements between NISRA and the PHA.  However since then, the value of the report has changed as more people were vaccinated and people were being admitted to hospital with COVID-19 but not due to COVID-19.  Therefore, at present it is felt there is no longer the need for this report, however as we head into the winter months this may change.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what the current timeframe is for sending out an MOT test reminder letter; and (ii) whether this is sufficient due to the long delay before appointment dates are available.
answer_text: Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) officials continue to monitor the Covid situation and will make a decision on when to increase the reminder notification period from six to seven weeks when they are satisfied that it has reached a more manageable position for certainty of staffing levels. The DVA has steadily increased its vehicle testing capacity to pre-Covid levels by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the use of overtime to provide cover for leave and by offering vehicle test appointments on a Sunday and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available. To minimise late cancellations due to the non-availability of staff, the DVA is offering more short notice appointments to ensure testing can be delivered when staffing availability is confirmed. This is allowing many customers to bring their MOT appointments forward, as new appointment slots are released.The DVA continues to experience significant demand for vehicle testing services and in some cases customers may not be able to have their vehicles tested before their current MOT certificate expires. In such cases, customers must book the earliest available test appointment for their vehicle, which may mean travelling to another test centre instead of their preferred location. The DVA releases vehicle test appointments on a daily basis and customers are encouraged to regularly check the booking system as they may be able to change their existing appointment to a different test centre and time if a slot becomes available. The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/book-view-change-or-cancel-mot-vehicle-test  and is also included as an advice note with every MOT Reminder Letter issued to their customers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many people took part in the Randomised Evaluation of COVID-19 Therapy Recovery Trial in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: There have been 999 participants in the RECOVERY Trial recruited through NI sites so far.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on (i) the current wait time for an MOT appointment compared to (a) June 2021; and (b) June 2019, and (ii) what actions are being taken to reduce the waiting time for an MOT appointment.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has steadily increased its vehicle testing capacity to pre-Covid levels by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the use of overtime to provide cover for leave and by offering vehicle test appointments on a Sunday and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available.  The DVA is monitoring the number of applications received and tests carried out and expects waiting times to reduce over time through the actions they have taken to increase vehicle testing capacity across the network. The DVA continues to experience significant demand for vehicle testing services and in some cases customers may not be able to have their vehicles tested before their current MOT certificate expires.  In such cases, customers must book the earliest available test appointment for their vehicle, which may mean travelling to another test centre instead of their preferred location.  The DVA releases short notice vehicle test appointments on a daily basis to ensure testing can be delivered when staffing availability is confirmed.  Customers are encouraged to regularly check the booking system as they may be able to change their existing appointment to a different test centre and time if a slot becomes available. The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/book-view-change-or-cancel-mot-vehicle-test and is also included as an advice note with every MOT Reminder Letter issued to their customers.The DVA will continue to work hard to reduce vehicle testing waiting times as quickly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what medicines, treatments and therapies were used for the early treatment COVID-19 patients.
answer_text: From the 16 December 2021, access to monoclonal antibodies and antivirals as a treatment for COVID-19 was extended from patients hospitalised with COVID-19 infection to include non-hospitalised patients aged 12 years and above, who tested positive for COVID-19 and were considered at highest risk of progression to severe disease, hospital admission or death. The Department made an announcement on 9 December 2021 that COVID-19 treatments for non-hospitalised patients would be available through Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust led Outpatient COVID-19 Treatment Service (OCTs) for patients at highest risk from COVID-19 infection and who met specific criteria for treatment. The announcement also detailed that the PANORAMIC study had been set up by the University of Oxford to rapidly evaluate whether antiviral treatments helped people at higher risk of COVID-19 to recover sooner and prevent the need for hospital admission.There are currently four COVID-19 treatments available within the UK clinical commissioning policy: Neutralising monoclonal antibody (nMAB) treatment SotrovimabAntiviral Paxlovid (nirmatrelvir plus ritonavir)Antiviral Remdesivir (Veklury)Antiviral Molnupiravir (Lagevrio).Over 3,800 COVID-19 treatments have been provided across Northern Ireland by HSC Trust OCTs since December 2021. The patient-facing website NI Direct Treatments for coronavirus (COVID-19)provides up to date information on COVID-19 treatments for non-hospitalised patients, as well as the latest details of the PANORAMIC study.In addition, the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE guideline 191) COVID-19 rapid guideline: Managing COVID-19 covers the management of COVID-19 for children, young people and adults in all care settings. It brings together existing recommendations on managing COVID-19, new recommendations on therapeutics and is continually updated as new information emerges.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many unused facemasks or shields have been placed in storage or have been disposed of because they cannot be used; and (ii) to outline the reasons why they cannot be used.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland PPE is managed by the BSO Procurement and Logistics Service (BSO PaLS).Currently there are no facemasks or shields in storage, managed by BSO PaLS due to the fact that they cannot be used.  Furthermore, to date BSO PaLS has not disposed of any facemasks or shields.  You may be aware that a donation, which included face masks, was made to the Ukraine humanitarian aid effort, earlier this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the provision of three funded cycles of IVF treatment, including timescales, as committed to in New Decade, New Approach.
answer_text: I very much welcomed the commitment to a greater number of funded IVF cycles in the New Decade, New Approach, something which the Member may already be aware only came about after my Party specifically requested it.  I established a Project Board in February 2020 to take the implementation of this commitment forward, although due to the demands of the pandemic on the HSC, the Project Board was unable to meet for the first time until April 2021.  I have always been clear, however, that providing up to three cycles of IVF treatment for those who are eligible will require investment to enable the Regional Fertility Centre to deliver greater volumes of treatment.  Work is ongoing to develop the necessary business case and implementation plan for this.  Once funding is in place, it is likely that a phased approach to firstly two, and then three cycles will be adopted.  This is to ensure that the service is not overwhelmed by demand and that waiting lists do not increase significantly, as well as the need to ensure there is adequate capacity to deliver, including within the independent sector initially. Once there is clarity on future funding and implementation, a Frequently Asked Questions guide will be developed to assist patients to understand the new arrangements, including eligibility.  However, I can advise that there are no plans to change the IVF access criteria as laid out by the National Institute of Health and Care Excellence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the eligibility criteria for three funded cycles of IVF treatment.
answer_text: I very much welcomed the commitment to a greater number of funded IVF cycles in the New Decade, New Approach, something which the Member may already be aware only came about after my Party specifically requested it.  I established a Project Board in February 2020 to take the implementation of this commitment forward, although due to the demands of the pandemic on the HSC, the Project Board was unable to meet for the first time until April 2021.  I have always been clear, however, that providing up to three cycles of IVF treatment for those who are eligible will require investment to enable the Regional Fertility Centre to deliver greater volumes of treatment.  Work is ongoing to develop the necessary business case and implementation plan for this.  Once funding is in place, it is likely that a phased approach to firstly two, and then three cycles will be adopted.  This is to ensure that the service is not overwhelmed by demand and that waiting lists do not increase significantly, as well as the need to ensure there is adequate capacity to deliver, including within the independent sector initially. Once there is clarity on future funding and implementation, a Frequently Asked Questions guide will be developed to assist patients to understand the new arrangements, including eligibility.  However, I can advise that there are no plans to change the IVF access criteria as laid out by the National Institute of Health and Care Excellence.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how many sites in North Antrim are used for radio and telecommunications infrastructure that can transmit 4G, 5G or any other radiofrequency or electromagnetic radiation or wave.
answer_text: The Department for the Economy does not hold information on the number, location or operation of any radio or telecommunication infrastructure.This information is held by the owners of this infrastructure, the Mobile Network Operators (MNOs), Vodafone, o2, Three and BT(EE).My officials will forward you contact details of representatives from each of the MNOs, who may be able to assist you with your query.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when people will be able to avail of their GP services and surgeries at pre-pandemic levels.
answer_text: Prior to the COVID-19 pandemic, Primary Care services were already coming under increasing pressure.  Figures in 2019 indicated patient contacts were almost 15 million per year up from an estimated 12.7 million in 2014. In recognition of the increasing demand for primary care services, my Department has continued to invest in our GP workforce and has increased the number of GP trainees by over 70% from 2015 levels.  Work is underway on a review of GP trainee places to ensure we have the workforce in Primary Care we need going forward.  Whilst this work is ongoing, I recently announced that my Department would be increasing of the number of GP training places for 2022/23 by 10, bringing the total number in Northern Ireland to an all-time high of 121 training places this year.  This will help stabilise our GP workforce and ensure it is sustainable for the future.  Any future increases will be informed by completion of the review and subject to the availability of funding.It is important to note however that the increase in demand for primary care services cannot be met solely by increasing the number of GPs.  Rather this is part of a wider programme of work to help improve patient access to services in primary care.  Other elements include the wider roll-out of primary care Multi-disciplinary Teams as well as the introduction of Advanced Nurse Practitioners and additional General Practice Nurses, all of which are making a difference to how services are delivered in primary care and contributing to improved patient outcomes.It is important to emphasise that GP practices are open and will continue to provide both face to face appointments and alternative consultation options for patients as deemed clinically appropriate by GPs. The general practice telephone first consultations allow patients to seek timely medical advice from their GP for both routine and urgent problems.  The GP determines the most appropriate approach to safely addressing the patient’s needs using their clinical expertise.  Where a face to face appointment is appropriate, that will be arranged.  Alternatively, the GP may decide, based on their clinical assessment, that a telephone consultation is appropriate or that the patient should be signposted or directed to other relevant services.  This approach helps the GP to appropriately manage, treat and support the large number of us who seek clinical support and advice from our primary care practitioners.  I am aware that some patients are experiencing difficulties in relation to access to GP services and understand the frustration this can cause.  My Department has established a Working Group to explore issues around access to GP services, including demand for services, limitations of infrastructure, and staffing and resourcing issues.  The Working Group will also consider how technology can be better used to provide improved access in a standardised way across Northern Ireland.  The Working Group will propose actions to be taken in

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what surveys have been conducted to ascertain how many people will go undiagnosed, misdiagnosed, fall seriously ill or die as a result of the GP phone first system.
answer_text: GP phone first system has allowed practices to maintain the majority of GP services and has enabled GPs to appropriately manage, treat and support a greater number of people than would otherwise have been the case.  With the level of pressure on the service continuing to rise, the use of telephone consultations are an important part of how GPs can treat and support the large number of patients who need their services.   With the GP phone first system the GP determines the most appropriate approach to safely addressing the patient’s needs using their clinical expertise.  Where a face to face appointment is appropriate, that will be arranged.  Alternatively, the GP may decide, based on their clinical assessment, that a telephone consultation is appropriate or that the patient should be signposted or directed to other relevant services.   In terms of diagnoses, GPs are still expected to clinically assess for risk of serious illness and refer as clinically appropriate for diagnostic tests and to specialist services such as an outpatient clinic as they have always done.  GPs want anyone who has a health concern to feel reassured that they will be able to get an appointment and see a GP if necessary.  If people have symptoms or an unexplained illness or have any reason to be concerned, they should in the first instance contact their GP who will be able to provide advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to list any Departmental officials or HSC staff who have received remuneration or gifts from any company or business connected to the making and distribution of vaccines or testing equipment, or from any companies or businesses connected to businesses who make and or distribute vaccines or testing equipment; and to list the remuneration amount or items received.
answer_text: No Departmental officials or HSC staff have notified receiving remuneration or gifts from companies or businesses connected to the making and distribution of vaccines or testing equipment, or from companies or businesses connected to business who make and / or distribute vaccines or testing equipment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) what sites are being used for 5G transmission in North Antrim; and (ii) when was each site made 5G operational.
answer_text: The Department for the Economy does not hold information on the number, location or operation of any radio or telecommunication infrastructure.This information is held by the owners of this infrastructure, the Mobile Network Operators (MNOs), Vodafone, o2, Three and BT(EE).My officials will forward you contact details of representatives from each of the MNOs, who may be able to assist you with your query.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will put in place a systematic and robust method of auditing diabetes care and the supporting infrastructure.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, the Diabetes Network brings together people living with diabetes, carers, health and social care professionals, clinicians and Diabetes UK (NI) to work in partnership on the design and delivery of better diabetes services.  The Diabetes Network is supportive of an audit in respect of diabetes in Northern Ireland. Network representatives have met with representatives from Diabetes UK to discuss the National Diabetes Audit and data requirements.  The Diabetes Network will continue to work with Diabetes UK to scope and consider what is possible to support an audit approach in Northern Ireland.  This will include consideration of what form such an audit would take.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what health and safety checks or tests are conducted on radio and telecommunications infrastructure that can transmit 4G, 5G or any other radio frequency or electromagnetic radiation or wave; and (ii) who is responsible for conducting these tests and enforcing any measures required.
answer_text: The Department of Health and the Public Health Agency have a memorandum of understanding with Public Health England (now the UK Health Security Agency), which includes arrangements for the provision of specialist technical advice on a range of matters, including radio frequency electro-magnetic fields (RF EMF). The advice on ‘mobile phone base stations: radio waves and health’ is publically available online at: www.gov.uk/government/publications/mobile-phone-base-stations-radio-waves-and-health/mobile-phone-base-stations-radio-waves-and-health. My Department will continue to rely upon this advice and to signpost interested parties to it. In summary, the advice indicates that exposures of the general public to radio waves remains low relative to international guidelines and, as such, there should be no consequences for public health.Measures are in place to reduce risks to employees and to the general public. Health and Safety legislation requires suitable and sufficient risk assessments are carried out to ensure that risks arising from EMF exposure associated with the deployment and operation of communication networks are effectively controlled. Industry has voluntarily committed to comply with international health-related guideline levels that are used in the UK. These guidelines are from the International Commission on Non-Ionizing Radiation Protection (ICNIRP) and underpin health protection policies at UK and European levels. Existing planning guidance refers to demonstration of compliance with the ICNIRP guidelines as satisfying any potential public health concerns. Ofcom carries out an audit of the emissions from mobile phone base stations. Further information about base stations and the audit is available online from Ofcom at: www.ofcom.org.uk/spectrum/information/mobile-operational-enquiries/mobile-base-station-audits. In summary, many exposure measurements have been made in the UK at publicly accessible locations near to base stations and these have consistently been well within the ICNIRP guideline levels.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what actions are being taken to reduce the backlog of routine diabetes appointments.
answer_text: The COVID-19 pandemic has posed unprecedented challenges for the planning and delivery of health and social care services, including services for people living with diabetes.  I recognise the impact that this has had on patients across Northern Ireland who have been affected by this.The pandemic has also meant changes to how diabetes services are delivered.  For example, there has been more use of virtual clinics, zoom consultations, or follow up via telephone, with urgent face to face contacts where necessary; new phlebotomy hubs were established, blood clinics and “drive through” clinics also put in place for bloods taken and structured diabetes education programmes were delivered online with educators trained to deliver more services remotely.To support access to diabetes services and appointments, the Diabetes Network secured funding to provide IT hardware to support virtual consultations across all HSC Trusts.  This equipment has enabled access to on-line provision to support a backlog of diabetes appointments amid COVID pressures. Trusts are also using temporary contracts, peripatetic staff and bank/agency workers, where they are available, to fill staff gaps in diabetes care services. In moving forward, it will be important that we learn from the new ways of working that have been critical in the response to the pandemic, so that innovation, transformation and collaboration will be at the heart of rebuilding health and social care services.  In terms of diabetes services, this includes for example, learning from and building on the potential of technology such as virtual consultations and on-line education in diabetes treatment and care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will develop a comprehensive workforce strategy to address the service gaps which are affecting the care of people with diabetes.
answer_text: My Department published the comprehensive Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy 2026: Delivering for Our People in May 2018, with a second action plan outlining an ambitious range of actions for progression over the next three years being published on 15 June 2022.  The aim of the Strategy is that ‘by 2026, we meet our workforce needs and the needs of our workforce’.Effective workforce planning is central to the delivery of this aim and from a strategic perspective, my Department currently oversees a rolling programme of long-term, regional workforce reviews.  These reviews take into consideration issues such as service models, standards of care, innovation, skills mix, and staff retention. They provide a sound evidence base for the commissioning of pre- and post-registration training by the Department with the aim of securing required workforce and skills supply over a 5 – 10 year horizon.My Department is working closely with the Diabetes Network to develop a Programme of Care Workforce Review for Diabetes.  This review will consider staff required to deliver services to people with diabetes.   A working group has been established to consider and undertake the preliminary work to ensure that the required baseline workforce and service user information will be available to support the Workforce Review.  This includes work being progressed by the Diabetes Network to develop a regional pathway for diabetes for Northern Ireland.  It is anticipated that the Workforce Review will commence in 2022/23, subject to the necessary preliminary work being completed.I have often said that an increased and sustained investment is necessary to support our ambitions for the health and social care workforce and to deliver the workforce numbers recommended by Workforce Reviews.  My Department currently faces a very challenging financial position and I am on record as warning that funding pressures in health will become increasingly significant as this financial year progresses.  Our financial situation will undoubtedly be constrained whatever the final budget settlement and we will not be able to do everything we want.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will put in place a Diabetes Prevention Programme with recurrent and sustainable investment, as a key means to support people at risk of developing type 2 diabetes.
answer_text: Prevention of Type 2 diabetes is one of the key areas in the Diabetes Strategic Framework for Northern Ireland, published in November 2016.  Diabetes prevention and the reduction of the number of people developing Type 2 diabetes is supported through the Diabetes Prevention Programme, with implementation through the Public Health Agency and in partnership with Health and Social Care Trusts. The Diabetes Prevention Programme is an evidence based, NICE compliant, lifestyle programme promoting behaviour change to delay or prevent Type 2 diabetes.  It is for adults who are defined by a blood test as having pre-diabetes and who are referred by primary care practices and willing to take part in group based sessions.The Diabetes Prevention Programme aims to support people to reduce their risk of developing Type 2 diabetes through weight reduction, healthy food choices and increased physical activity. The programme is facilitated by health coaches who offer help and assistance to participants, helping them change their lifestyle, diet and physical activity with the aim of postponing and even preventing Type 2 diabetes. The Diabetes Prevention Programme has been delivered virtually on-line during the pandemic, although this has been on a more limited basis due to staffing pressures.  There are waiting lists for the programme, which include people who have stated a preference for face to face participation.   Recruitment to the programme in four Trusts is currently paused due to funding challenges.  The Public Health Agency remains supportive of the programme and is considering how it can be funded in the longer term.I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care.  As Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available.Amid ongoing budget uncertainty, I am on record as warning that funding pressures in health may be significant by the second half of 2022/23. The financial situation will undoubtedly be constrained whatever the final budget settlement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will implement commitments in the Mental Health Strategy to ensure the availability of mental health interventions for people with long term conditions like diabetes.
answer_text: The Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 is focused on putting the individual and their needs at the centre, with the aim of ensuring long term improved outcomes for people’s mental health. I am committed to ensuring that all 35 actions within the Strategy are implemented.  Action 22 highlights the creation of effective pathways from physical healthcare into mental health services.  This is to ensure those with a physical illness, such as diabetes, can receive the support that they require for any resultant mental ill health.Implementation of the strategy is subject to the provision of additional funding to my Department and on that basis, it is critical that a budget is confirmed and allocated at the earliest opportunity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will develop a plan to increase the uptake of diabetes technology to everyone who can benefit from it.
answer_text: The Diabetes Network for Northern Ireland sets the strategic direction for diabetes treatment and care in Northern Ireland.  The Network is comprised of people living with diabetes, carers, and health and social care professionals working in partnership with Diabetes UK to support the implementation of the Diabetes Strategic Framework.  The Diabetes Network structure includes a Digital Technology Group to develop the use of technology to support people living with diabetes.  The Diabetes Network has made access to insulin pumps a high priority.  In January 2022, I announced the launch of a new regional insulin pumps service. The service was developed in partnership alongside Diabetes UK and people living with diabetes.  The new model has been supported by £2million funding from my Department and aims to ensure equitable access to pumps and support across Northern Ireland for people with Type 1 diabetes.  The pathway, ethos and approach for the service have been agreed and this will be developed over the next 3 years.  When in place the service will help support people to get the best outcomes from their pump therapy and ensure that diabetes teams across the region can share good practice and optimise care.The Digital Technology Group is also exploring and supporting the use of Continuous Glucose Monitoring (CGM) devices.  There are many CGM systems available to support people living with diabetes, although the most appropriate system for each individual is the responsibility of the HSC Trust and their clinicians to determine in consultation with the patient and/or the patient’s carer.  The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) updated their guidelines in relation to technology in March 2022.  Whilst CGM is a priority for the Digital Technology Group, there is not currently funding provision in place to address this priority or instil CGM into clinical care. The Group is currently drafting new pathways to reflect the NICE guidance and will seek approval from the Diabetes Network Board to adopt and roll out these pathways in the event that funding is secured to expand access to devices for patients.The Diabetes Network’s Digital Technology Group has also been instrumental in the development of a regional Diabetes Apps Library for all types of diabetes.  This provides access to support and guidance for people living with Type 1 and 2 diabetes, gestational diabetes in pregnancy and any other type of diabetes as well as those who are pre-diabetic. The site https://apps4healthcareni.hscni.net hosts a selection of safe and trusted apps providing users with health and wellbeing information, and which have been independently checked and reviewed for data privacy, clinical assurance and user experience.  The pandemic has also meant changes to how diabetes services are delivered with more use of technology to support virtual clinics, zoom consultations and structured diabetes education programmes delivered online with educators trained to

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether charities which operate out of domestic properties receive Charitable Rates Relief on domestic rates.
answer_text: Eligibility for charitable exemption is dependent on the circumstances at each property.Where the rateable occupier is the charitable body itself and not the tenant, the property is valued as a single non-domestic residential care establishment with a Net Annual Value (NAV).  The occupation is then considered and, if eligible, granted exemption.  The body is typically in rateable occupation where it has provided the accommodation in furtherance of its charitable objects to persons in particular need; or it has retained a degree of control over the property more than exists in the normal landlord/tenant relationship. If, however, the charitable body has granted a standard lease to the tenant of the property (as would be the case for example with some charities that are housing associations), then the tenant is the rateable occupier, exemption from rates does not apply and a separate Capital Value is assessed for each property in the domestic Valuation List.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans are in place to review whether people with Down Syndrome should be assessed for Personal Independence Payments, Employment Support Allowance and Disability Living Allowance as frequently as other people.
answer_text: There are measures in place in each of the benefit areas to ensure that people are not required to undertake unnecessary reviews.  These processes are subject to ongoing review and enhancement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the UK infection prevention and control guidance, with new COVID-19 pathogen-specific advice for health and care professionals, was implemented in Northern Ireland from 1st June 2022.
answer_text: Guidance on infection prevention and control for seasonal respiratory infections including SARS-CoV-2 was removed from the GOV.UK website as of 30 April 2022.After this date each of the four countries transitioned to the use of their IPC manuals and the application of Standard Infection Prevention and Control Precautions (SICPs) and Transmission Based Precautions (TBPs). The other three nations also implemented COVID-19 specific guidance which has been adapted and implemented for Northern Ireland health and social care settings as of 20th May 2022. The purpose of this guidance is to provide disease specific infection prevention and control measures to prevent transmission of SARS-CoV-2 in Health and Social Care settings in Northern Ireland and is available on the PHA website here.The Northern Ireland Regional IPC Manual has also been updated to reflect this guidance and COVID-19 will be included in the table of diseases going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) how many operational single wind turbines and anaerobic digesters are still awaiting grid connection or have been refused grid connections; and (ii) what are the reasons for delay or refusal.
answer_text: Data received from NI Electricity Networks (NIEN) advises that there are currently 45 applications at various stages of the connection process which are wind turbines or anaerobic digestion (AD) renewable technologies. Of the 45 applications 3 are for AD, the remainder are wind. They also advise that, to date, 150 applications for single wind turbines or AD stations have been refused a grid connection with 121 being for wind turbines. The main reasons are the unavailability of grid capacity or grid fault level. The NIEN capacity map, which displays both generation export capacity and fault level capacity, can be found here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what his Department is doing to bring about a full and speedy implementation of the Elective Care Framework.
answer_text: In June 2021, I published the Elective Care Framework setting out 55 actions to reduce waiting times.  The Framework highlights the need for additional capacity in order to reduce waiting lists. The Framework sets out a five year plan with firm, time bound proposals for how we will systematically tackle the backlog of patients waiting longer than Ministerial standards, and how we will invest in and transform services to allow us to meet the population’s demands in future.A newly established Elective Care Management Team is overseeing implementation of the Framework. The team brings together clinicians and senior managers from across health and social care system and the Department.There are a number of initiatives in the Framework that are already well underway:The introduction of mega clinics to maximise patient throughput.  In May 2022 600 patients had already been treated already this financial year, in addition to the 6,240 patients treated last year at these clinics. The Day Procedure Centre (DPC) at Lagan Valley Hospital (LVH) is working extremely well, supporting a range of specialties from across the region to undertake the most urgent clinical cases. Approximately 3,000 patients have been treated at the Regional Day Procedure Centre at LVH, and approximately 5,000 patients have received endoscopy procedures at the centre. This is additional regional capacity to help reduce long waits.Omagh Hospital has also been set up as a Regional Day Procedure Centre and has been provided with additional funding to enhance capacity for Elective Care Services.Allowing a private Independent Sector provider to use vacant theatres in the South West Acute Hospital in Enniskillen has already seen hundreds of patients on waiting lists for hip replacement surgery being treated. This service provides good patient outcomes with over 67% of patients discharged from hospital on day of surgery and the vast reminder discharged the day after.Theatre lists for paediatric general surgery day procedures have been created or reinstated across the region and this will allow children to be treated in a more timely manner with the intention to tackling the backlog of children waiting.Opportunities to deliver more elective surgery on a regional basis are currently being explored in other units across the Health and Social Care (HSC) service.The Framework requires significant investment - an additional £707.5m over the five-year period.  The performance gains described in the Elective Care Framework will only be achieved with significant sustained investment. Funding has been maintained for additional waiting list activity at levels set out in the Elective Care Framework. This involves £46m in the first two quarters of 2022/23, covering the period April to September.  I also recently announced further recurrent funding in excessive of £16m to further enhance capacity for our elective care services. Without an agreed budget in place there remains uncertainty around the implem

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what urgent action is being taken on waiting times for life changing elective surgery; and (ii) how he can ensure that patients on the routine waiting lists will get the surgery they require.
answer_text: Elective inpatient and day cases are managed in two priorities, urgent and routine. Patients on the waiting lists will be seen and treated on the basis of their clinical urgency with red flag suspect cancer (subset of urgent) patients seen and treated first. Routine patients are seen and treated in chronological order.In June 2021, I published the Elective Care Framework setting out 55 actions to reduce waiting times.  The Framework highlights the need for additional capacity in order to reduce waiting lists. The Framework sets out a five year plan with firm, time bound proposals for how we will systematically tackle the backlog of patients waiting longer than Ministerial standards, and how we will invest in and transform services to allow us to meet the population’s demands in future.There are a number of initiatives in the Framework that are already well underway:The introduction of mega clinics to maximise patient throughput. In May 2022, 600 patients had already been treated already this financial year, in addition to the 6,240 patients treated last year at these clinics. The Day Procedure Centre (DPC) at Lagan Valley Hospital (LVH) is working extremely well, supporting a range of specialties from across the region to undertake the most urgent clinical cases. Approximately 3,000 patients have been treated at the Regional Day Procedure Centre at LVH, and approximately 5,000 patients have received endoscopy procedures at the centre. This is additional regional capacity to help reduce long waits.Omagh Hospital has also been set up as a Regional Day Procedure Centre and has been provided with additional funding to enhance capacity for Elective Care Services.Allowing a private Independent Sector provider to use vacant theatres in the South West Acute Hospital in Enniskillen has already seen hundreds of patients on waiting lists for hip replacement surgery being treated. This service provides good patient outcomes with over 67% of patients discharged from hospital on day of surgery and the vast reminder discharged the day after.Theatre lists for paediatric general surgery day procedures have been created or reinstated across the region and this will allow children to be treated in a more timely manner with the intention to tackling the backlog of children waiting.Opportunities to deliver more elective surgery on a regional basis are currently being explored in other units across the Health and Social Care (HSC) service. Funding has been maintained for additional waiting list activity at levels set out in the Elective Care Framework. This involves £46m in the first two quarters of 2022/23, covering the period April to September. I also recently announced further recurrent funding in excessive of £16m to further enhance capacity for our elective care services. This funding is for further developments at Lagan Valley Hospital and Omagh Hospital.  It will also fund developments at Musgrave Park Hospital Duke of Connaught as an orthopaedic day procedure

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what his Department is doing to provide information, support and reassessments for patients with Musculoskeletal conditions who are on waiting lists.
answer_text: Within primary care a range of multi-disciplinary teams (MDT) are in place or are in development within seven of the seventeen GP federations in Northern Ireland. These MDTs include a First Contact Physiotherapist service. These physiotherapists are employed to assess patients and identify their conditions including musculoskeletal. Once assessed these patients are then referred to the appropriate service for treatment of their condition. Information on musculoskeletal conditions is widely available in the Health and Social Care system through a range of mediums including poster/ leaflets and a range of social media campaigns. Programmes available include the Northern Health and Social Care Trust Pain Management Programme (PMP) which is a multidisciplinary service that aims to help participants with chronic musculoskeletal pain to better manage their condition and to improve their quality of life.There are no immediate plans to take forward a Musculoskeletal strategy in isolation - rather this work is included in the rebuild and transformation of services which will benefit MSK patients amongst others, including investment in initiatives to reduce waiting lists. As part of this work in June 2021 the Chief Allied Health Professions Officer secured £48,176 non recurrent Covid funding to appoint a Regional Musculoskeletal lead for a period of seven months. The post holder’s role was to commence work on developing regional musculoskeletal pathways to help reduce waiting lists. Unfortunately this funding ceased in March 2022 and the post holder returned to his HSC Trust.It would be my hope that once the Executive and Assembly recommences business that a multi-year budget would be approved and this initiative under the Elective Care Framework work could then be reinstated. The prevention, diagnosis and treatment of musculoskeletal conditions is taken very seriously by me and by my department and is one of many priorities for which I am constantly seeking additional funding and resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department categorises musculoskeletal conditions as a healthcare priority.
answer_text: Within primary care a range of multi-disciplinary teams (MDT) are in place or are in development within seven of the seventeen GP federations in Northern Ireland. These MDTs include a First Contact Physiotherapist service. These physiotherapists are employed to assess patients and identify their conditions including musculoskeletal. Once assessed these patients are then referred to the appropriate service for treatment of their condition. Information on musculoskeletal conditions is widely available in the Health and Social Care system through a range of mediums including poster/ leaflets and a range of social media campaigns. Programmes available include the Northern Health and Social Care Trust Pain Management Programme (PMP) which is a multidisciplinary service that aims to help participants with chronic musculoskeletal pain to better manage their condition and to improve their quality of life.There are no immediate plans to take forward a Musculoskeletal strategy in isolation - rather this work is included in the rebuild and transformation of services which will benefit MSK patients amongst others, including investment in initiatives to reduce waiting lists. As part of this work in June 2021 the Chief Allied Health Professions Officer secured £48,176 non recurrent Covid funding to appoint a Regional Musculoskeletal lead for a period of seven months. The post holder’s role was to commence work on developing regional musculoskeletal pathways to help reduce waiting lists. Unfortunately this funding ceased in March 2022 and the post holder returned to his HSC Trust.It would be my hope that once the Executive and Assembly recommences business that a multi-year budget would be approved and this initiative under the Elective Care Framework work could then be reinstated. The prevention, diagnosis and treatment of musculoskeletal conditions is taken very seriously by me and by my department and is one of many priorities for which I am constantly seeking additional funding and resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department is supporting people diagnosed with arthritis.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Pain Forum has been established, in order to develop and improve services across Northern Ireland for people living with a wide range of painful health conditions.  The membership of this forum spans voluntary organisations such as Versus Arthritis, Healthy Living Centres, service users and clinicians from many disciplines and organisations including primary and secondary care.  All Health and Social Care Trusts offer pain and condition management programmes when access criteria are met for those individuals with a range of physical and mental health conditions, including chronic pain, and GPs continue to provide core services to their registered patients including access to allied health professional services, such as physiotherapy.  Funding is also being provided to Versus Arthritis and the Healthy Living Centre Alliance to deliver self-management programmes in order to support adults living with all forms of persistent pain and long-term health conditions, including those on waiting lists.One of the key actions of the Elective Care Framework which I published in June 2021 is the delivery of a recovery plan for orthopaedics by the Northern Ireland Orthopaedic Network, which will set out the priority actions, targets and timescales to bring orthopaedic activity back to commissioned levels and to increase activity as rapidly as possible. My Department commissioned a rapid independent external review of the regional orthopaedic service in NI, to identify proposals for the immediate recovery of the service.   The Review was undertaken by a team from the Royal National Orthopaedic Hospital (RNOH), Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) Projects Directorate. The report detailed 21 recommendations, which were designed to increase activity in the short term with the overall aim of building a sustainable service for the future.  All 21 recommendations have been fully accepted by the Department, and work is already well underway to implement these.  Additional capacity has been secured through the Independent Sector to help patients on Health and Social Care waiting lists receive treatment more quickly. For example, an insourcing arrangement is currently underway in the South West Acute Hospital in Western Trust. Through this arrangement, an independent sector provider is using HSC facilities to treat patients on orthopaedic waiting lists for hip replacement surgery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the care companies that currently have contracts with Northern Health and Social Care Trust; (ii) the services each company provides; and (iii) the areas in which they provide these services.
To ask the Minister for Communities how many people in North Antrim are currently waiting for Personal Independence Payment appeals.
