question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, further to the publication of the Sports Sustainability Fund Awards List, (i) what evidence was supplied to Sport Northern Ireland to support the £400,000 bid by Cycling Ireland; and (ii) what evidence was supplied to Sport Northern Ireland to approve the award of £14,518 to Cycling Ireland.
answer_text: The Sports Sustainability Fund was administered by Sport NI on behalf of the Department for Communities. Any successful bid was subject to the appropriate due diligence within the criteria of the programme

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what plans and policies are in place to deliver the removal of forestry from areas of deep peat and prevent the restocking or planting of forestry on peatland sites.
answer_text: Forest Service has included the restoration of open ground semi-natural habitats, including peatland sites, in its forest planning since 2002 in line with the requirements of the UK Woodland Assurance Standard. Forest Service published a policy restricting the expansion of forests on deep peat in 2004 and this is regulated under forestry Environmental Impact Assessment legislation.  This policy is consistent throughout the UK and is incorporated within the UK Forestry Standard.  Management of forests on private land requires a felling management plan, which includes appropriate consideration of the re-establishment of felled areas, to be approved by my Department.Forest Service management plans are periodically reviewed and as part of this process a scoping consultation for plans for West Fermanagh forests, which includes a Strategy for Restoring Peatland Habitats, has been recently published. The implementation of this strategy for restoring peatland habitats in Forest Service land considers harvesting time frames and forestry re-establishment within the design plans, including those areas that will not be restocked following harvesting. The Northern Ireland Peatland Strategy to 2040, currently in draft, will guide the identification and prioritisation of afforested peatlands for restoration where biodiversity and carbon gains can be maximised.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what plans and policies are in place to make farming more sustainable for the future.
answer_text: My Department is designing a more tailored agricultural policy, which better meets the needs of Northern Ireland. In March, I announced 54 decisions which define future agricultural policy and allow schemes to be developed that will provide opportunities for all Northern Ireland’s farmers.  These policy decisions will be followed as soon as possible with new policies and systems to ensure that we will have a future sustainable agricultural industry. Schemes and support are needed to help farmers develop their businesses, no matter where they farm, to become more productive, profitable and resilient, and to maximise the sustainable returns from the assets at their disposal.However, there is still significant work to be done, which my officials will continue to take forward along with continued engagement with stakeholders.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many enforcement office investigations are currently ongoing for unauthorised development on flood plains.
answer_text: Local Council Planning Authorities have responsibility for planning enforcement issues.  I am therefore not able to confirm the number of current investigations for unauthorised development on flood plains.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans and policies are in place to ensure our planning service and planning policies remain fit for purpose amid the climate and nature emergency.
answer_text: The planning system continues be fit for purpose and evolves to reflect key developments in respect of sustainable development and climate change.  The Regional Development Strategy 2035 (RDS) and the Strategic Planning Policy Statement for Northern Ireland (SPPS) provide the key guiding principles for planning in Northern Ireland and both recognise the need to mitigate and adapt to climate change, as well as preserve and improve the built and natural environment. My Department’s planning officials continue to engage with a wide range of stakeholders; are involved in a number of forums to take forward relevant research; and share best practice to ensure that the planning system will continue to play its part in supporting wider government policy, plans and strategies in efforts to address the climate and nature emergency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) what plans and policies are in place to support the rewetting of our peatlands; and (ii) what sites will these policies concentrate on.
answer_text: DAERA is developing a Northern Ireland Peatland Strategy, which will provide a framework for conserving our intact semi-natural peatlands and restoring degraded semi-natural peatlands across Northern Ireland.  There are a number of proposed actions in the strategy which taken together should ensure that peatlands that are degraded can be set on the road to recovery, in accordance with the recommendations of the UK Climate Change Committee.NIEA through the Environment Fund grant aid not for profit organisations to undertake strategic environmental projects, including peatland restoration works, across Northern Ireland. Other potential funding steams such as PEACE Plus may also provide opportunities for funding restoration of cross-border habitats and species, including peatlands.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) what plans and policies are in place to support the expansion of native tree planting; and (ii) what sites are currently being planted.
answer_text: DAERA supports woodland creation using native tree species by offering various grants, including the Forest Expansion Scheme and the Small Woodland Grant Scheme, which was designed specifically to support the planting of native species. Native woodland and tree planting options also exist within the Environmental Farming Scheme.  New woodlands supported by these schemes will contribute to the Forests for Our Future programme’s aim to create 9,000 hectares of new woodland by 2030. The forestry grant schemes support planting woodlands that comply with the UK Forestry Standard, which requires a proportion of native trees to be planted. 	To date almost 900 hectares of new woodland has been planted and verified under the Forests for Our Future programme up until the end of the 2021-22 planting season. This represents a significant interest from private and public landowners to plant new woodland. I plan to reopen the forestry grant schemes later this month for applications to plant woodland in the 2022-23 season.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what percentage of staff within the Driver and Vehicle Agency are working from home (i) full-time; and (ii) part-time, broken down by each section within the Driver and Vehicle Agency.
answer_text: You will be aware that under the current COVID-19 guidance, the message remains that people should work from at home where they can.  Throughout the pandemic, many staff within the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) have been delivering front line services which cannot, by their nature, be delivered from home.  Our buildings have remained open, with appropriate mitigations in place, for those who need to access them for essential reasons. Some staff have therefore returned to their workplace full-time, others on a part-time basis where it has been deemed essential, while others remain working at home.While line managers closely monitor the location of staff, the DVA does not centrally hold information on the number of staff who are working from home.  This information could only be obtained at a disproportionate cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what percentage of Housing Executive staff are working from home (i) full-time; and (ii) part-time, broken down by each section within the Housing Executive.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that during the pandemic, (approximately 33% of its staff) were in their various workplaces at any one time, in order to ensure the continued delivery of services and compliance with the public health guidance. During April 2022 the Housing Executive commenced a phased return to the workplace for all its staff. Whilst the Housing Executive do not have detailed figures for each section of their workforce in terms of those working from home full time or part-time it estimates that (approximately 66% of the Housing Executive’s staff) have been in the workplace since the transition phase commenced. The aim over the coming weeks is that 100% of its employees will attend their normal place of work for at least part of the working week, with the actual percentage of time varying depending on the individual’s role.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what percentage of staff in his Department are working from home (i) full-time; and (ii) part-time, broken down by each section within the Department.
answer_text: My Department does not centrally monitor staff working locations.In line with the current Executive guidance that if you can work from home you should. The majority of my Department’s staff have been working from home.A significant number of DAERA staff have however, continued to attend the workplace throughout the COVID-19 Pandemic, to ensure the continued delivery of key services.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what percentage of staff in her Department are working from home (i) full-time; and (ii) part-time, broken down by each section within the Department.
answer_text: Information on the percentage of staff working from home broken down by each section within the Department is not recorded.  In line with the prior Executive guidance that all staff who can work from home, should work from home, the majority of my Department’s staff have worked from home, supported by enhanced IT provision which has enabled them to perform their duties effectively.  A number of staff are required to attend the office on a daily basis to provide face to face services and other staff are attending the office on an ad hoc basis for various operational and personal reasons.Following the recent change to Executive Guidance, my Department will progress with implementation of the NICS Hybrid Working policy over the coming weeks, with a greater balance of office and remote working where this best meets organisational needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) what species of animal or plant are threatened with extinction in Northern Ireland; and (ii) what plans and policies are in place to protect each of these species.
answer_text: The International Union for the Conservation of Nature and Natural Resources (IUCN) have produced categories and criteria used to assess the conservation status of species and to inform the IUCN Red List of threatened species.  NIEA experts have worked in partnership with National Parks and Wildlife Service in the Republic of Ireland to produce a number of relevant Red Data lists for the island of Ireland.  These give specific detail on the vulnerability of a wide range of species, including vascular plants, terrestrial mammals and bryophytes.  These lists can be accessed from the following link:	Irish Red Lists | Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (daera-ni.gov.uk)	The Birds of Conservation Concern in Ireland 2020-2026 is the latest assessment of the conservation status of all regularly occurring birds on the island of Ireland.  It uses data from a wide variety of sources and a traffic light system (i.e. Red, Amber and Green) to indicate the conservation status of species.  Of the 211 species assessed, 54 were placed on the Red List; i.e., the most threatened species.  These include well-known species such as Corncrake, Curlew and Barn Owl.  The full listing can be found at: Birds of Conservation Concern in Ireland - BirdWatch Ireland		NIEA ornithologists have also been involved in compiling the UK Birds of Conservation Concern No.5, published at the end of 2021:BB 2021 DECEMBER (Med-res) (britishbirds.co.uk)Please note that not every species on each of the two lists occurs regularly in Northern Ireland.The rarest or most vulnerable species receive legal protection by their listing in the schedules of the Wildlife (NI) Order 1985.  Not all species will have a dedicated plan or policy associated with them. Conservation efforts tends to be focused on the protected sites network, so-designated for the significant species of animals and plants and/or habitats each site contains. A number of environmental Non-Government Organisations receive money through the NIEA-administered environment fund to carry out a range of species-specific monitoring and management activities. Given the scale of this task to reduce risk of extinction and support species recovery, there is need for a broader programme of such work.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline his plans for Phase 2 of the Special Recognition Payments to Health and Social Care staff and independent sector staff.
answer_text: Phase 2 had been temporarily paused to allow officials to seek clarity on a number of issues raised relating to the impact of the increase in national insurance contributions, which are included as part of the payment provided to the former employer.  A further letter was issued to employers on 14 April 2022 providing clarity on the issues raised and confirming recommencement of the payment process. Claims are being processed by BSO in date order when the claimants provide all relevant information. No date has been set for the closure of the scheme and claims will continue to be processed by the Business Services Organisation during 2022/23. I am not in a position to provide a date as to when all payments will be made.I can confirm that as at 26 May 2022, a total of over £16.6m has been paid out under Phase 1 to independent sector care home and domiciliary care providers covering 28,457 individuals. As at the same date over £3.3m has been paid out to 7,999 individuals under Phase 2; these payments include staff previously employed in independent sector care home and domiciliary care providers who have since moved employer or left the sector, and staff employed with eligible day opportunity providers and buildings-based day services.In addition, we have also opened the Scheme to eligible applicants in the following groups, Personal Assistants employed by Independent Living Fund recipients, Thalidomide Health Grant recipients, and Personal Assistants employed by those in receipt of Direct Payments from HSC Trusts. This element of the scheme was launched on 23 March and is being delivered by ILF Scotland for the payments, Scotland Excel who set up and run the online portal, and SDS Scotland who manage the dedicated helpline. These delivery partners were heavily involved in delivering the equivalent scheme for the Scottish Government.  To date £300k has been paid to 863 individuals. To encourage uptake on this element Trusts have now written to all individuals in receipt of direct payments. My officials are also finalising arrangements to include eligible staff whose employers are no longer trading and I hope to be in a position to include this cohort of individuals in the coming weeks.A FAQ on the Special Recognition Payment has been published on the Department’s website as below:-https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/news/hsc-staff-recognition-payment-faqsThis continues to be updated with relevant information as each phase of the claims process progresses. I remain personally committed to ensuring the recognition payment will be paid to independent sector staff as soon as it is practicable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what percentage of staff in his Department are working from home (i) full-time; and (ii) part-time, broken down by each section within the Department.
answer_text: In response to the outbreak of COVID-19, and in line with Executive and Public Health Agency Guidance, Northern Ireland Civil Service staff including those in the Department of Health (DoH), were instructed to work from home, where possible.Given that business needs can change on a daily basis, numbers working in the office on a daily or ad-hoc basis can fluctuate however, currently the majority of DoH staff work from home at any one time.As of 6 June 2022, the Executive’s guidance on working from home was updated.  This change in guidance creates the circumstances for DoH to take the next steps to put in place suitable working arrangements that best meet organisational needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will outline the revised targets and timescales for progressing the Reshaping Stroke Care commitments that his Department made in the consultation document in March 2019.
answer_text: My Department received in excess of 19,000 responses to the ‘Reshaping Stroke Care Consultation’. In the time since the consultation closed, I have been giving careful consideration to the consultation analysis, the evidence base for reform and emerging evidence on stroke treatments. Having done so, I can advise that it is my intention to set out the way forward for the reform of stroke care in the coming weeks and that the Reshaping Stroke Care consultation analysis will be published alongside this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he intends to publish the results of the consultation on Reshaping Stroke Care.
answer_text: My Department received in excess of 19,000 responses to the ‘Reshaping Stroke Care Consultation’. In the time since the consultation closed, I have been giving careful consideration to the consultation analysis, the evidence base for reform and emerging evidence on stroke treatments. Having done so, I can advise that it is my intention to set out the way forward for the reform of stroke care in the coming weeks and that the Reshaping Stroke Care consultation analysis will be published alongside this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what the current average time is for smear test results in each Health and Social Care Trust; and how this compares to (i) 2017; and (ii) 2012.
answer_text: The requested information is not available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) what percentage of staff in her Department are working from home (i) full-time; and (ii) part-time, broken down by each section within the Department.
answer_text: Staff within the Department of Justice (DoJ) continue to work in line with the prevailing guidance.  A significant number of DoJ staff are required to be in the workplace to provide front-line services that cannot be delivered remotely.  The Department does not routinely capture information on the number of staff working from home as that changes on a daily basis

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) what percentage of staff in his Department are working from home (i) full-time; and (ii) part-time, broken down by each section within the Department.
answer_text: My Department no longer monitors staff working locations centrally. Up until 6 June 2022, in line with Executive guidance that all staff who could work from home, should work from home, the vast majority of my Department’s staff had done so, supported by enhanced IT provision which enabled them to perform their duties effectively.  Those staff who had to be in the workplace to deliver front line services were working on site. However, with the lifting of the “Work from Home“ guidance this will allow more staff to return to the workplace, fully utilising New Ways of Working including, implementing the Hybrid Working Policy, which provides for a mixture of office and home working, and the use of the Connect2 hubs. This means that staff will have more options as to how they work, depending on business need. Each department will be carefully managing a phased roll-out of New Ways of Working for staff over the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) what percentage of staff in her Department are working from home (i) full-time; and (ii) part-time, broken down by each section within the Department.
answer_text: My Department continues to deliver key services to some of the most vulnerable people in our society whilst complying with the ‘Work from Home where you can’ Executive guidance which was in place until 5 June 2022. In line with this guidance only staff who need to be in an office are requested to attend the workplace.  The numbers in attendance on any given day change depending on service need and working patterns.  On 6 June 2022, the Head of the Civil Service announced changes to the Work from Home where you can’ Executive guidance in response to prevailing health guidance.  This will allow Hybrid Working to commence across the Department and will see staff returning to the offices in a controlled and managed way.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what percentage of staff in his Department are working from home (i) full-time; and (ii) part-time, broken down by each section within the Department.
answer_text: You will be aware that under the current COVID-19 guidance, the message remains that people should work from at home where they can.  Throughout the pandemic, many staff within the Department have been delivering front line services which cannot, by their nature, be delivered from home.  Our buildings have remained open, with appropriate mitigations in place, for those who need to access them for essential reasons. Some staff have therefore returned to their workplace full-time, others on a part-time basis where it has been deemed essential, while others remain working at home.While line managers closely monitor the location of staff, my Department does not centrally hold information on the number of staff who are working from home.  This information could only be obtained at a disproportionate cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) what percentage of staff in his Department are working from home (i) full-time; and (ii) part-time, broken down by each section within the Department.
answer_text: In line with the then public health advice, approximately 96% of the Department for the Economy’s staff worked from home full-time since the start of the pandemic.Of the 4% of Departmental staff who attended our offices regularly to carry out essential business since March 2020, this was mostly comprised of Premises staff to maintain our buildings, support staff for the Minister and the Permanent Secretary, and staff working for the Office of the Industrial Tribunals and Fair Employment Tribunals, which continued to hold hearings throughout the pandemic.Following the lifting of the Executive Guidance on working from home on 6 June 2022, the Department re-opened all our offices on 27 June 2022 and commenced the implementation of the NICS Hybrid Working Policy. Whilst some job roles require full time attendance in the office, it is expected that the vast majority of staff will now adopt a hybrid working pattern under the new Policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) how many pupils from North Antrim did not receive a post-primary school place this year; and (ii) for a breakdown of their first choice schools.
answer_text: As at 25 May 2022, 17 children who reside in the North Antrim constituency and who had applied for a post-primary Year 8 place for September 2022 admission were still to secure a place.  The first preference schools nominated on these children’s applications are listed in the following table.SCHOOLNUMBER OF FIRST PREFERENCE APPLICATIONS Ballymena Academy 6Slemish College5Ballymoney High School3Cambridge House Grammar School2Ballyclare High School1 A total of 229 Year 8 places remained available across the constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) since the vaccine rollout began, how many vaccines have not been used because the expiry date has passed; (ii) how many unused vaccines had, or have, an expiry date before the end of (a) February 2022; (b) March 2022; (c) April 2022; and (d) May 2022.
answer_text: It is not possible to provide the information as requested due to the complex nature of the programme as some stock has an expiry date in a frozen state and then an expiry date in a thawed state.  In addition some stock has had its shelf life extended by the EMA/MHRA.  I can however advise that there have been 3,909,380 COVID-19 vaccinations administered to date in NI and when comparing this with the total number of doses distributed to Trusts, GPs and community pharmacies there is an attrition rate of approximately 6%.  It should be noted that this figure does not represent only stock lost due to passing its expiry date as other potential reasons may account for a proportion e.g. contaminated vial, dropped / damaged vial, storage / fridge failure.  It should also be noted that the figure will also include some stock that is currently within the fridges of Trusts/GPs and community pharmacies and while it has not been administered yet it has not yet expired and could be used.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the 2021 National Joint Council pay increase will be implemented.
answer_text: Housing Executive 2021/22 Pay Remits have been approved

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department provided guidance to businesses that administer the COVID Status Certification in domestic settings on the cyber risks of processing special category data.
answer_text: Guidance for Venues & Event Organisers using the COVIDCert Verifier App, designed to be used in conjunction with the COVIDCert App, was first provided on 28 October 2021. Details of the guidance can be found at https://covid-19.hscni.net/covidcert-check-ni-guidance-for-venues-event-organisers/ .

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what criteria was used in awarding £75,000 to Shine and Belsonic for an event on the 17 September 2021 in Ormeau Park, Belfast; (ii) what budget stream and funding mechanism was used; (iii) how this funding stream was publicised; and (iv) how many organisations applied for the funding.
answer_text: COVID-19 vaccination has saved lives and reduced hospitalisation and has been vital to NI’s pathway out of Covid restrictions. It has been essential not just on public health grounds, but to wider society and the economy.The Emerge concert date complemented a number of promotional drives to encourage young people to get vaccinated.The concert both raised awareness of the importance of vaccination and acknowledged the impact of the pandemic on the younger generation.My Department provided support funding for the event, with its component of the allocation coming from health promotion monies.Chief Scientific Adviser Professor Ian Young had publicly advised at this point in the pandemic that an increase of 5% in the percentage of the population who were vaccinated could reduce hospitalisations by as much as 50%.Professor Young also highlighted the benefits vaccination had already secured. In December 2020, for every 1000 cases of COVID-19 in NI, around 80 were admitted to hospital. By August 2021, the figure for hospitalisations was in the region of 22 per 1000 cases.The health benefits of higher vaccine take-up had therefore been clearly documented. In financial terms, benefits included reducing the cost of hospitalisations, as well as reducing disruption to society from ill health, absenteeism, extended restrictions and damaged consumer confidence.I am therefore in no doubt that the initiatives to promote vaccination in NI were both highly important and justified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what impact assessments, analysis or comparisons have been commissioned and commenced on the impact of lockdowns on the number of deaths caused by (i) missed operations; (ii) missed diagnosis; (iii) suicide; and (iv) economic impact.
answer_text: My Department has not commissioned any specific impact assessments, analysis or comparisons on the impact of ‘lockdowns’ on the number of deaths caused by (i) missed operations; (ii) missed diagnosis; (iii) suicide; and (iv) economic impact.The UK COVID-19 Inquiry has been set up to examine the UK’s preparedness and response to the COVID-19 pandemic, and to learn lessons for the future.  The Inquiry has recently consulted on draft Terms of Reference which will set the topics for its investigations into the UK’s pandemic response.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, further to the use of the SSE Arena as a COVID vaccination centre, to outline (i) the set up costs for the facility; (ii) a breakdown of the facility's running costs; (iii) how many staff were employed and at what cost; (iv) how many vaccinations were administered (a) in total; and (b) per day since the facility opened, broken down by first, second and booster doses.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the percentage capacity Musgrave Park Hospital is currently operating at and how this compares to (i) May 2021; and (ii) May 2020.
To ask the Minister of Health what engagement his Department has had with the UK COVID-19 inquiry to date; and what form this engagement has taken.
answer_text: In the absence of a First & deputy First Minister, I was consulted on the scope of the UK Covid-19 Inquiry only days after the collapse of the Northern Ireland Executive.My officials attended a meeting with members of the United Kingdom Covid-19 Inquiry Team on 25 May 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people diagnosed with Pericarditis in (i) 2021; and (ii) the first 4 months of 2022; and how this compares to each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Information on the levels of pericarditis in Northern Ireland is not specifically collected. However, the number of people admitted to hospital in Northern Ireland for each year from 2011 to April of 2022 who had a recorded diagnosis of pericarditis is provided in the table overleaf: Individuals admitted to hospitals in Northern Ireland who had a diagnosis of pericarditis from 2011 to April 2022YearPericarditis Admissions20112382012235201327520143142015290201634020174112018407201935520203512021440January – April 2022 P122Source: Hospital Inpatient System, Hospital Information Branch, Information & Analysis Directorate, Department of Health, NI.P (Provisional data)Figures across years cannot be summed as an individual may have been admitted in multiple years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many people are awaiting home care packages in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust; (ii) what is the average waiting time for a care package; (iii) what is currently the longest waiting time; (iv) how many of these people are waiting for a package while living at home; and (v) how many of these people live in (a) urban; and (b) rural areas.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people diagnosed with Myocarditis in (i) 2021; and (ii) the first 4 months of 2022; and how this compares to each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Information on the levels of myocarditis in Northern Ireland are not specifically collected. However, the number of people admitted to hospital in Northern Ireland for each year from 2011 to April of 2022 who had a recorded diagnosis of myocarditis are provided in the table overleaf: Individuals admitted to hospitals in Northern Ireland who had a diagnosis of myocarditis from 2011 to April 2022YearMyocarditis Admissions2011122012420137201472015102016820179201811201910202011202112January – April 2022 P5Source: Hospital Inpatient System, Hospital Information Branch, Information & Analysis Directorate, Department of Health, NI.P (Provisional data)Figures across years cannot be summed as an individual may have been admitted in multiple years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the George Sloane Centre, Ballymena has opened to pre-pandemic levels to provide full service for those attending; and what services are not available at this time.
answer_text: Northern Health & Social Care Trust have reported that the George Sloan Centre has a total of 69 service users currently on register.  61 of these service users have been offered services and have resumed attendance. The remaining 8 are currently on hold. This is under continuous review with Day Care management and the Adult Community Learning Disability team.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, further to the consultation on the Review of the Northern Ireland Postgraduate Tuition Fee Loan, when he will publish (i) his response; and (ii) any changes to the current policy on postgraduate support for students at private providers in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department is currently considering the responses to the consultation along with policy options, and I hope to publish a formal consultation response in the coming weeks. At that point I will be able to advise of any possible changes to postgraduate policy and funding, including for private providers. However, any outcomes, particularly in relation to the levels of financial support available, will be dependent upon the funding that is available to the Department over the coming years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether there are any grant aid support schemes available to assist people in installing (i) air source heat pumps; and (ii) solar panels in domestic settings.
answer_text: In December 2021 the Department published the new Energy Strategy for Northern Ireland, which has sets the direction of travel for achieving net zero carbon and affordable energy.  Air Source Heat PumpsThe Energy Strategy commits to support for low carbon heating technologies, including air source heat pumps, and work is underway to develop a support scheme that will be available from 2023.  In addition, my Department is currently working with public and private sector partners on a range of low carbon forms of heat demonstrator projects across Northern Ireland. The information gathered from these projects will help inform decisions on the type of future support offered. Solar PanelsSince the closure of the Northern Ireland Renewables Obligation scheme (NIRO) on 31st March 2017, there are currently no Government sponsored support mechanisms active in Northern Ireland for the installation of solar panels.As part of the development process of the Energy Strategy my Department has been considering what the next steps might be in relation to support schemes to enable Northern Ireland to meet a new renewable electricity target of at least 70% by 2030. This includes consideration of options for microgeneration such as solar panels on homes and businesses.  This target has now increased to 80% through the Northern Ireland Climate Change (No.2) Bill, which will shortly receive Royal Assent.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when health visits to assess new born babies and infants will return to pre-pandemic levels; and what are the current levels.
answer_text: Health Visiting and Family Nurses (Family Nurse Partnership) continued to provide a home visiting service throughout all stages of the pandemic and the service was not ceased.  Health Visitors and Family Nurses completed some visits remotely due to the COVID -19 situation and informed by the risk assessment.Health Visitors are returning to normal pre-Covid activity, this includes the full delivery of the Healthy Child Healthy Future programme and targeted work to support families.  It is planned that all unmet need accumulated during the pandemic will be addressed as capacity permits.  Rates of compliance across all Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust areas for the new baby review visits is consistently above 95%.Community midwives have continued with postnatal visits to women on the first day home visit following discharge regardless of day of delivery. Women have also received a visit on day 3, 5, 10 as a minimum, with selective visiting based on clinical need in addition to this. Visits are entirely flexible and woman led with increased visiting routinely offered for mothers or babies needing additional support / observation / investigations, as well as referral to other agencies / General Practitioner if indicated.  Levels of midwife visits to new born babies across all HSC Trust areas is at a pre-pandemic stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when will all restrictions on close family members or partners be lifted so they can attend antenatal appointments, maternity wards after the birth of child and postnatal care.
answer_text: Visiting in Maternity services continues to be facilitated in line with the arrangements in the Pathway to Enhanced Visiting which took effect from 7 May 2021, and is available on the Department’s website here.This guidance represents the expected visiting arrangements which should be facilitated, providing that decisions are guided by local Infection Prevention and Control risk assessments.  In the main, restrictions are limited as follows:Maternity Outpatient Services: A chosen birth partner will be facilitated to accompany the pregnant woman to any pregnancy related appointments and ultrasound scans with particular effort made to keep the environment COVID-19 secure.Labour and Birth: A chosen birth partner will be facilitated to accompany the pregnant woman for induction of labour, duration of labour and birth and, for up to three hours after the birth.Maternity Inpatient Services: One daily visit from one of two nominated individuals (from up to two households) can be permitted, with particular effort made to keep the environment COVID-19 secure. Where the visitor requires assistance then no more than 1 additional person will be permitted to accompany them when this can be accommodated within social distancing guidance. Duration of visits should be agreed with person in charge. Alternatives, for example, outdoor visiting, virtual visits, should continue to be made available.These arrangements are kept under regular review, so the most up to date information on visiting across specific services is published on each individual Health & Social Care Trust website. While I am very aware of the impact that restrictions around visiting during the COVID-19 pandemic have had on women and their birth partners, we remain committed to the protection of vulnerable people.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans are in place to install any new charging stations, particularly rapid chargers for electric vehicles, in the (i) Ballymena; (ii) Ballymoney; (iii) Ballycastle; and (iv) Bushmills areas.
answer_text: The current electric vehicle public charge point network is owned by the Electricity Supply Board (ESB) and is operated on a commercial basis. ESB announced in November 2021 that it had been awarded £3.27 million from the Levelling Up Fund to expand and enhance the EV charging network. ESB can be contacted directly regarding its plans for its charge point infrastructure. The market is also open to other commercial operators who would wish to provide charging infrastructure. I am pleased that my Department has been able to support the FASTER Project, funded through the INTERREG VA Territorial Co-operation Programme which is managed by the Special EU Programmes Body (SEUPB). The project has received funding to install a total of 73 EV Rapid charging points across the island of Ireland and the West of Scotland by 31 May 2023. It is expected that 20-30 of these will be located in the North.My Department has also set up an EV Infrastructure Task-Force which brings together representatives from government, consumers, energy providers, industry and EV drivers to consider our EV Infrastructure requirements and to help set out a clear action plan by autumn 2022 to deliver a fit for purpose, modern EV charging network.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will he make a written statement on the proposed international agreement on pandemic prevention, preparedness and response and its impact locally; and discussions or correspondence he has had with the Minister of Health and Social Care.
answer_text: The United Kingdom supports a new international instrument to strengthen pandemic prevention, preparedness and response. The purpose of the instrument is to strengthen global cooperation in order to better protect the UK from the health, social and economic impacts of pandemics. Our support for the instrument is intended to strengthen our ability to prevent, detect and respond to future health threats without the restrictions seen during the COVID-19 pandemic.  Priority areas for agreement include improving transparency, timely data sharing and supporting equitable access to vaccines and treatments.At this stage there have been no specific discussions or correspondence with the Minister of Health and Social Care in respect of the international instrument.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what assessment he has made of the role of physician associates in the health service; and how these roles will be utilised and funded.
answer_text: The Physician Associate role is one of a number of new roles that could be very much at the heart of how 21st century health and social care is delivered in Northern Ireland. Emerging evidence is that this new profession, working alongside medics, has a significant role to play in stabilising and enhancing medical teams across a range of settings.My Department is participating in UK wide work to secure statutory regulation and prescribing rights for Physician Associates. Locally, we are encouraging employers to develop a strategy for the utilisation of Physician Associates.  We have also fostered the growth of a local supply of Physician Associates by supporting students on the postgraduate training programme provided by Ulster University.  My Department intends to review these support arrangements during 2022.
